maps.notes: Scope: all 31 files under Sources/App/Settings/** (30 in the flat directory + 2 under AuthMode/). No crypto/Keychain calls were found inline in any SwiftUI `body` in this subsystem — screens consistently delegate to ScreenModels/coordinators/services per the project's stated convention, which held up well here. No PgpError leaked to a View; the two thrown-error surfaces in this chunk (LocalDataResetError, OpenSourceNoticeStore/SourceComplianceStore.StoreError) are project-local LocalizedError types, not raw FFI errors, so no boundary-normalization violation. Dead-code sweep was grep-based (`rg -w <symbol> --type swift .`) against the whole repo; "zero-production" below means the only non-declaration occurrences found were in Tests/. 4 confirmed zero-production-reference declarations were found (listed per-file below); everything else I scanned resolved to at least one production call site.
crossCutting: No nine-key-family duplication exists in this chunk — Settings/** never enumerates the 9 key families itself (SelfTestView's per-result `profile.openPGPConfiguration.identity.familyDisplayName` is a single generic label, not a repeated per-family switch), so there is nothing here to unify on that axis.

Clipboard-copy duplication: SourceComplianceView.swift reimplements the exact AppKit/UIKit NSPasteboard/UIPasteboard copy logic privately (`copyIfPresent`) instead of reusing the shared `RepositoryURLCopyAction`/`RepositoryURLCopying` type that AboutView.swift and LicenseDetailView.swift already use for the identical 'copy a URL, show a checkmark' pattern.

Trace/metadata-formatting duplication: ProtectedSettingsAccessCoordinator and ProtectedSettingsHost each independently define near-identical private `accessGateTraceValue(_:)` (AccessGateDecision -> String) switch functions, plus each has its own `stateMetadata`/`traceXxxEvent` boilerplate. AppAccessPolicySwitchWorkflow and LocalDataResetService separately hand-roll their own ad hoc `traceStore?.record(category:name:metadata:)` call-and-string-literal patterns. None of these four files share a common trace-metadata-building helper; a small shared 'trace value formatting' utility would remove several near-duplicate switch statements.

Auth-mode confirmation UI duplication: Settings/AuthMode/AuthModeChangeConfirmation.swift's `SettingsAuthModeConfirmationSheetView` and Onboarding/TutorialAuthModeConfirmationView.swift's `TutorialAuthModeConfirmationView` render the same Form/Section/Toggle/Button structure over the same `AuthModeChangeConfirmationRequest`, differing only in dismissal call (`dismiss()` vs `tutorialStore.dismissModal()`) and accessibility identifiers — a good candidate to unify behind an injected dismiss closure.

smells.detail: Hand-rolls its own start/finish trace-record pairs with string literal event names/metadata dictionaries rather than using a shared trace-metadata builder (see crossCutting).

files.role: Alternate-app-icon picker screen (UIKit-only): list of icon options plus their row rendering.
files.cohesion: One file per code-style is normally one type, but here it's a tightly-coupled trio (picker view, AppIconOption data model, private AppIconRow) for a single small feature — acceptable, not worth splitting.

files.role: Detail screen for a single open-source notice: version/license/source metadata, repository copy action, license text.
Searchable list of open-source notices grouped into app/core-dependency/third-party sections.

Post-reset 'restart required' state holder plus the blocking overlay view and its EnvironmentKey.

smells.detail: `resetSummary` is captured on every reset but never consumed: the overlay it feeds (`LocalDataResetRestartRequiredView`) shows only a fixed, hardcoded message and never reads `coordinator.resetSummary` or its `deletedKeychainItemCount`/`removedDirectoryCount` fields.

smells.detail: Builds a granular per-item `failures: [String]` list (keychain/SE/directory/temp-artifact failure codes) that is packaged into `LocalDataResetError.failures` but never read by production code — the UI only ever shows `error.localizedDescription`'s generic message, so the diagnostic detail is captured for tests only.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Read only from Tests/ServiceTests/LocalDataResetServiceTests.swift; production code only calls the LocalizedError-synthesized `.localizedDescription`, never `.failures` directly.

AppAccessPolicySwitchWorkflow was already extracted from CypherAirApp as its own collaborator; the auth-mode-switch and local-data-reset flows here are prime candidates for the same treatment.

Auth-mode-switch confirmation request model and its confirmation sheet view.

smells.detail: SettingsAuthModeConfirmationSheetView is near-identical to Onboarding/TutorialAuthModeConfirmationView.swift's TutorialAuthModeConfirmationView (see crossCutting).

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Set by every request builder/init call but read only from Tests/ServiceTests/SettingsScreenModelTests.swift (`request.pendingMode`); production code reads the unrelated `SettingsScreenModel.pendingMode` property instead, never `request.pendingMode`.

maps.module: Sources/App/Encrypt, Sources/App/Decrypt (+Support), Sources/App/Sign — Encrypt/Decrypt/Sign/Verify tool screens (views + screen models)
maps.notes: Read every file under Sources/App/Encrypt/**, Sources/App/Decrypt/**, Sources/App/Sign/** (17 files, 4,462 LOC), no edits made. Confirmed zero crypto/Keychain/PgpError leakage inside any SwiftUI `body` — all four screen models route through injected service closures (EncryptionService/DecryptionService/SigningService) and normalize errors via `CypherAirError.from` before the view ever sees them, exactly per CLAUDE.md. No copy-paste across the nine key families was found in this chunk: profile/family display text is centralized behind `contact.preferredKey.profile.contactKeyKindDisplayName` and `matchedKey.openPGPConfigurationIdentity.familyDisplayName`, so Encrypt/Decrypt/Sign stay family-agnostic already. The duplication that does exist is structural/plumbing, not per-family (see crossCutting). Dead-code sweep: 377 top-level+member declarations enumerated across the 17 files (file-scope types plus, for the four screen-model "god objects", every stored/computed property and method — that is where mechanical dead code actually hides); 3 confirmed zero-production-reference declarations found, all with test-only references (noted below), no dynamic-dispatch caveats apply to any of them (plain Swift instance members, not @objc/KVC/Codable/selector-reached).
crossCutting: FileImportRequestGate + fileImportRequestToken (computed) + handleFileImporterResult(_:token:) boilerplate is duplicated near-identically in EncryptScreenModel, DecryptScreenModel, SignScreenModel, and VerifyScreenModel — same begin/consumeIfCurrent/invalidate dance four times; a shared mixin/protocol would collapse this.

The clipboard-copy-with-user-notice block (clipboardTask/clipboardToken/clipboardNoticeDecision/clipboardWriter/cancelClipboardCopy, plus the Task{...} cancellation dance in copyCiphertextToClipboard/copySignedMessageToClipboard) is duplicated verbatim between EncryptScreenModel.swift and SignScreenModel.swift.

showsFileCancelAction / showsFileDecryptCancelAction / showsDetachedCancelAction are the identical one-line predicate (`mode == .file && operation.isRunning && operation.progress != nil`) reimplemented in Encrypt/Decrypt/Sign/Verify screen models — four copies of one computed property.
dismissError()/finishExport()/handleExportError(_:) + a private mapXError(_:) triad is repeated in all four screen models, varying only in which CypherAirError case the closure produces.

The mode-picker toolbar/section block (`usesToolbarModePicker = CypherModePickerPlacement.usesToolbar(...)`, the non-toolbar Section fallback, and the ToolbarItem(.principal) CypherModePicker) is copy-pasted essentially unchanged across EncryptScreenHostView, DecryptView's host, SignView's host, and VerifyView's host — only the mode enum/localization keys differ.

Encrypt and Decrypt give their `Configuration` a paired `RuntimeSyncKey` + `updateConfiguration(_:)` + `.onChange(of: runtimeSyncKey)` live-reconfiguration path (needed for the interactive tutorial demo); Sign and Verify do not — `SignScreenModel.configuration`/`VerifyScreenModel.configuration` are `let`, fixed at init. This is an inconsistency across the four otherwise-parallel screen designs, not obviously a bug, but worth a conscious decision rather than four independently-evolved shapes.

Each is independently testable/extractable (e.g. a RecipientSelectionState type, a ClipboardCopyCoordinator shared with Sign).

The clipboard-copy-with-notice block (clipboardTask/clipboardToken/clipboardNoticeDecision/clipboardWriter/cancelClipboardCopy) is duplicated near-verbatim in SignScreenModel.swift's copySignedMessageToClipboard.

The default messageQuantumSafetyAction constructs `PgpEngine()` directly (line 124) instead of going through the injected `encryptionService`/`decryptionService` abstraction used everywhere else in the file — justified by a comment (stateless public-data inspection) but it is the one place this model bypasses its own DI pattern.

A change to one job risks touching the other.

(2) reads like a test/tutorial-support concern bolted onto the artifact store because both are 'ephemeral state to clean up'; splitting into AppTemporaryArtifactStore + a small TutorialSandboxDefaultsCleaner would separate the two storage mechanisms.

crossCutting: Duplicated clipboard-copy implementation: Sources/App/Common/CypherClipboard.swift's `CypherClipboard.copy(_:)` and the private `PlatformClipboard.copy(_:)` nested inside Sources/App/Common/OperationController.swift are byte-for-byte identical UIPasteboard/NSPasteboard branches. OperationController.copyToClipboard(_:shouldShowNotice:) should call the shared CypherClipboard instead of re-implementing it.

Nine-key-family copy-paste: PGPKeyFamily+Presentation.swift re-switches over the same 9 PGPKeyConfiguration.Identity cases in roughly 15 separate computed properties (familyDescription, familyAlgorithmSubtitle, familyInteropWarning, familySizeNote, familySecurityLevel, familyAlgorithmSummary, familyKeyVersionDisplay, familyMessageFormatDisplay, familyExportabilityDisplay, familyGnuPGCompatibilityDisplay, familyCustodyDisplay, etc.), each repeating the same case groupings (the v4-Legacy pair, the 4-member post-quantum group, the 7-member v6 group). Adding a 10th family means touching ~15 switch statements individually; a per-family metadata table (one row/struct per case with ~15 fields, each property reading its column) would remove the repetition without changing any call site.

FileImportRequestGate wiring boilerplate is copy-pasted into all 8 ScreenModels that import files (AddContactScreenModel, ContactCertificateSignaturesScreenModel, ContactCertificationDetailsScreenModel, ImportKeyScreenModel, EncryptScreenModel, VerifyScreenModel, SignScreenModel, DecryptScreenModel): each declares `private var fileImportRequestGate = FileImportRequestGate()`, a `fileImportRequestToken` computed passthrough, and a `token: FileImportRequestGate.Token?` completion parameter — identical 3-part pattern at every site.

`var outputInterceptionPolicy: OutputInterceptionPolicy = .passthrough` is redeclared as an identical one-line stored property in 7 separate files (ContactCertificateSignaturesView, ContactCertificationDetailsScreenModel, KeyDetailView, SelectiveRevocationView, EncryptView, SignView, DecryptView) rather than living once as a protocol default.

KeyBackupStatusBadge.swift and KeyCustodyBadge.swift are structural twins (same Style-enum shape, same CypherStatusBadge/Image-based body) that intentionally diverge only in copy per their own doc comments — not a bug, but the pair must be kept in sync if the badge-style set ever changes.
maps.notes: Full mechanical dead-code sweep: 38 files enumerated (3,283 LOC), every top-level/extension-level declaration grepped against the whole repo (Sources + Tests + UITests, `--include='*.swift'`). Result: zero declarations with zero references anywhere, production or test — this chunk is pure shared infrastructure consumed across Encrypt/Decrypt/Sign/Verify/Contacts/Keys, so nothing in it is orphaned. No crypto/Keychain logic was found inside a SwiftUI `body` anywhere in this chunk (screens stay thin per CLAUDE.md); CypherAirError+Presentation.swift is the correct, single error-boundary adapter with no leaks. The structural findings are all about repetition/cohesion rather than bugs: a duplicated clipboard helper (OperationController vs CypherClipboard), the 9-family switch-repetition in PGPKeyFamily+Presentation.swift, copy-pasted FileImportRequestGate/OutputInterceptionPolicy wiring across ScreenModels/Views, and MacPresentationPolish.swift's grab-bag of unrelated view types violating the one-type-per-file convention. AppTemporaryArtifactStore.swift and OperationController.swift are flagged 'watch' for combining otherwise-small but conceptually distinct responsibilities (temp-file protection + UserDefaults-suite cleanup; and op-lifecycle + error surface + clipboard notice, respectively) rather than for raw size.

files.role: Composition root: constructs and wires every dependency (auth, Secure Enclave custody, protected-data domains, key management, PGP services) for production launch (makeDefault) and for UI-test launch (makeUITest).
files.cohesion: Three distinguishable jobs bolted into one type: (1) production dependency wiring, (2) a parallel ~230-line UI-test composition root with its own sandboxed protected-data domains, and (3) permanently-compiled (non-#if DEBUG) UI-test runtime helpers (prepareUITestContactsIfNeeded/preloadUITestContact/makeSandboxContactsDomainStore) that only make sense paired with (2). makeDefault and makeUITest duplicate ~500 lines of near-identical wiring (only secureEnclave/keychain/rootSecretStore primitives differ) — a strong decomposition candidate (parameterize the shared wiring over the swappable primitives).

smells.detail: 11 near-identical private static factories (makePrivateKeyTextEncryptionService, StreamingFileEncryption, CleartextSigning, MessageDecryption, StreamingFileDecryption, DetachedFileSigning, PasswordMessageEncryption, ExpiryMutation, SelectiveRevocation, ContactCertification) all build the same router/softwarePrivateKeyAccess/messageAdapter/digestSigner shape, differing only in which concrete service type and adapter they plug in — collapsible into one generic factory.
makeDefault and makeUITest independently reconstruct almost identical protected-data-domain / key-management / service-graph wiring graphs (~500 lines total duplicated).

prepareUITestContactsIfNeeded, preloadUITestContact, and makeSandboxContactsDomainStore are NOT gated behind #if DEBUG (unlike makeUITest itself), so this UI-test-only code ships inside release builds even though it is only reachable from a DEBUG-only caller.

same as above — exposed on the container but never read via container.protectedDataRegistryStore outside AppContainer.swift.
same pattern — the local variable used during construction is heavily used, but the stored class property itself is never read after assignment.

PasswordMessageService is fully instantiated and wired (see PgpServiceGraph) but the container's passwordMessageService property is never read outside AppContainer.swift and is never injected into the SwiftUI environment in CypherAirApp.swift (unlike encryptionService/decryptionService/signingService/etc.) — the whole service is currently unreachable from any screen. (A same-named property on an unrelated test type TestHelpers.ServiceStack is exercised in Tests/ServiceTests/PasswordMessageServiceTests.swift, but that is a different type, not AppContainer.)

crossCutting: AppContainer.swift's makePrivateKey*Service family (11 near-identical private static factories: TextEncryption, StreamingFileEncryption, CleartextSigning, MessageDecryption, StreamingFileDecryption, DetachedFileSigning, PasswordMessageEncryption, ExpiryMutation, SelectiveRevocation, ContactCertification) is the closest analog to a 'nine-family' pattern in this chunk, but it is duplicated by *private-key operation kind*, not by the nine key families themselves (this chunk contains no key-family enumeration/switch code — that lives in Keys/ views and Models, out of scope here). Each factory plugs a different concrete service type into the identical router/softwarePrivateKeyAccess/messageAdapter/digestSigner shape and is a strong candidate for one generic factory function.

The three traceValue(for:) overloads (ProtectedDataBootstrapOutcome / ProtectedDataRecoveryDisposition / ProtectedDataFrameworkState) are duplicated verbatim as private methods in both Sources/App/AppStartupCoordinator.swift and Sources/App/CypherAirApp.swift — same switch bodies, same string literals. Worth hoisting into one shared helper (e.g. a small AuthTraceMetadata-style extension on the three enums).

AppContainer.makeDefault and AppContainer.makeUITest independently duplicate ~500 lines of protected-data-domain / key-management / service-graph wiring, differing only in the underlying secureEnclave/keychain/rootSecretStore primitives (real HardwareSecureEnclave/SystemKeychain vs Mock*). A parameterized shared wiring function would remove the bulk of this duplication.

Tab-to-view construction is duplicated between AppShellComposition.content(for:resolver:rootDecorator:) and ContentView.swift's MacAppShellView.detailContent(for:) — both switch over the same 8 AppShellTab cases to build the same 8 root views, just wrapped differently (AnyView vs @ViewBuilder).
maps.notes:
Scope: Sources/App root .swift files (AppContainer, CypherAirApp, AppStartupCoordinator, AppLoadWarningCoordinator, AppShellComposition, AppLaunchConfiguration, AppRoute, HomeView, ContentView) + Sources/App/Shell/** + Sources/App/DesignSystem/** — 20 files, 3849 total lines. Modeling convention applied throughout: `var body` (View/Scene protocol requirement) was excluded from declStats counts and from zero-ref consideration everywhere, since the SwiftUI/Commands runtime invokes it opaquely (never textually referenced by name across files) and flagging it on every View struct would just be noise, not a real finding.

Verification method: for every enumerated declaration, ran ripgrep word-boundary and member-access (`.symbol`) searches across Sources/, Tests/, and UITests/ (excluding the declaring file), then manually inspected any zero/low-count hit to rule out false negatives from common-word collisions (e.g. AppRoute.encrypt/decrypt/sign, CypherRadius.card) and false positives from same-named-but-unrelated types (e.g. Tests' TestHelpers.ServiceStack.passwordMessageService vs AppContainer.passwordMessageService; TutorialSandboxContainer.defaultsSuiteName vs AppContainer.defaultsSuiteName). Total: 333 top-level declarations scanned across the 20 files, 10 flagged as zero-production-reference (5 in AppContainer.swift, 2 in AppStartupCoordinator.swift, 1 each in AppShellTabs.swift, MacShellNavigationState.swift, and CypherLayout.swift). All are plain stored properties, methods, or constants with no reflection/Codable/selector/target-membership access path, so none carry a genuine dynamic-usage caveat beyond what's noted per entry — this repo's 'no plaintext/private keys in logs' and 'memory zeroing' security-critical rules were not implicated by any of these findings (none touch key material, passphrases, or crypto state directly; the closest is the wholly-unwired PasswordMessageService, which is a reachability/dead-wiring issue, not a security defect — the service itself still exists and is presumably intended to be exposed once its UI lands).

Overall structural picture: leaf/presentation-only files in this chunk (HomeView, the Shell/* navigation-state and modifier types, DesignSystem/*) are small, single-purpose, and clean — consistent with CLAUDE.md's thin-views/ScreenModel convention. Complexity concentrates in two composition-root-shaped files, AppContainer.swift and CypherAirApp.swift, both flagged oversized for doing multiple distinguishable jobs (production wiring vs. test-harness wiring vs. permanently-compiled test-runtime helpers in AppContainer; Scene/window definition vs. an embedded ProtectedSettingsHost authorization-workflow composition root vs. the iOS presentation state machine in CypherAirApp) rather than merely being long.

smells.detail: Private `StatusBadge` is a one-line wrapper that only forwards to the shared `CypherStatusBadge`; the 4 call sites could call `CypherStatusBadge` directly and drop the redundant type.

crossCutting: shouldIgnore(_: Error) -> Bool (checks `error is CancellationError` then `CypherAirError.operationCancelled`) is copy-pasted verbatim as a `private static func` in 7 of this directory's 9 ScreenModel files (BackupKeyScreenModel, ImportKeyScreenModel, KeyGenerationScreenModel, KeyDetailScreenModel, ModifyExpiry/ModifyExpiryScreenModel, PostGenerationPromptScreenModel, SelectiveRevocationScreenModel) — 7 of the 11 occurrences in the whole app live in this one folder. Worth hoisting into a shared helper (e.g. an `Error` extension or a `CypherAirError` static) once, rather than re-typing it per ScreenModel.

The error-alert view modifier block (`.alert(String(localized: "error.title"...), isPresented: ..., presenting: model.error) { Button("OK") { model.dismissError() } } message: { Text($0.localizedDescription) }`) is repeated near-verbatim in 7 View files here (BackupKeyView, ImportKeyView, KeyGenerationDetailsView, KeyDetailView, PostGenerationPromptView, SelectiveRevocationView, ModifyExpirySheetView). A single `View.cypherErrorAlert(error:isPresented:onDismiss:)` modifier alongside the existing DesignSystem primitives (CypherSpacing/CypherRadius/etc.) would remove this duplication in one place.

Nine-key-family copy-paste risk: split-custody detection (`case .deviceBoundPostQuantumV6, .deviceBoundPostQuantumHighV6: ... default: ...`) is independently duplicated with different wording in DeviceBoundKeyCommitmentSheet.custodyText and DeviceBoundKeyExplainerView.custodyText. If a future family adds another split-custody tier, both switches must be remembered and updated in lockstep with nothing forcing that — a shared `PGPKeyConfiguration.Identity` predicate (e.g. `isSplitCustodyFamily`) or a single shared custody-text provider would remove both the duplication and the family-drift risk.

The FileExportController-backed `.fileExporter(...)` + `model.finishExport()` + `handleExportError(_:)` triad is repeated near-identically in KeyDetailView, PostGenerationPromptView, and SelectiveRevocationView — a candidate for a single reusable view modifier taking the controller and the two callbacks.

KeyDetailScreenModel's default `clipboardCopyAction` re-implements the UIPasteboard/NSPasteboard branching that already exists as the shared `CypherClipboard.copy(_:)` helper (Sources/App/Common/CypherClipboard.swift), which MyKeysView already calls directly — the duplicate inline implementation in KeyDetailScreenModel could just call CypherClipboard.copy(_:).
maps.notes: Scope: all 21 files under Sources/App/Keys/** (4,101 total lines), covering key generation (picker + details + device-bound commitment/family-detail sheets), backup, import, key detail, modify-expiry, selective revocation, post-generation prompt, and the My Keys list — no files were sampled or skipped. Every file follows the View + Configuration + private HostView(@State model) + owning @Observable ScreenModel convention this codebase uses elsewhere, with the single documented exception of MyKeysView (no ScreenModel; flagged for its silent try? error-swallowing). Mechanical dead-code sweep: I enumerated every top-level type and its stored/computed properties, methods, nested types, and enum cases (declStats below), then grepped Sources+Tests+UITests for each symbol name repo-wide. Exactly one declaration came back with zero production references — KeyGenerationScreenModel.dismissDetails() (called only from a unit test) — everything else, including all Configuration fields, enum cases, and private helpers, has at least one production call site (often the same file, sometimes only reachable via SwiftUI's @Bindable/@FocusState wiring which reads as a genuine reference). The dominant finding across the chunk isn't unreferenced code but duplicated boilerplate (cancellation-guard helper, error-alert modifier, split-custody switch, fileExporter triad) — all captured in crossCutting. No crypto/Keychain logic was found embedded directly in a SwiftUI `body` except MyKeysView's inline `try? keyManagement.setDefaultKey(...)`, which is the one real architecture-convention violation in this chunk.

read only from Tests/ServiceTests/TutorialSessionStoreTests.swift (XCTAssertNotNil(container.securitySimulationStack.authManager)); zero production reads

`keyManagement.makePrivateKeyOperationRouter(publicBindingInspector: PGPSecureEnclaveCustodyPublicBindingInspector(engine: engine), handleStore: secureEnclaveCustodyHandleStore)` is repeated verbatim 7 times (textEncryptor, fileEncryptor, messageDecryptor, fileDecryptor, cleartextSigner, detachedFileSigner, contactCertificationSigner).

mockSecureEnclave/mockKeychain/mockAuthenticator are public stored properties whose values are only ever consumed inside this file's own initializer (never read externally, not even by tests); securitySimulationStack (a bundle of the same 4 mocks) is read only by one XCTest assertion.

crossCutting: Onboarding's key-families copy (onboarding.keyFamilies.body in OnboardingView.swift) still reads 'Six key families' after the 6→9 nine-families migration (issue #591, merged 2026-07-06 per project memory) — the Onboarding module's copy layer wasn't updated alongside the KeyFamily/PGPKeyProfile changes made elsewhere in the app.

A cluster of write-only 'rich state' fields spans TutorialModels.swift and TutorialSessionStore.swift: TutorialArtifacts.decryptedMessage/decryptedVerification/backupArmoredKey/authMode are set by TutorialSessionStore's note*() methods but never read by production views (some are read only by TutorialSessionStoreTests); TutorialGuidancePayload.module/state and TutorialSessionState.currentGuidance/specVersion/isWorkspacePresented follow the same pattern. This looks like an abandoned 'tutorial completion summary / guidance introspection' feature — the setters survived a UI removal that the getters didn't.

sizeClass is threaded through four points (TutorialSandboxChromeView -> TutorialShellTabsView -> TutorialSurfaceView -> TutorialGuidanceResolver.guidance(sizeClass:), plus a separate copy stored on TutorialRouteDestinationView) but the resolver discards its copy (`sizeClass _:`) and guidance never actually varies by size class, and TutorialRouteDestinationView's copy is never read at all. Either implement size-class-aware guidance or drop the parameter/property from all four call sites.

TutorialSandboxContainer.swift hand-rewires nearly the entire production service graph (KeyManagementService, ContactService, EncryptionService, DecryptionService, SigningService, CertificateSignatureService, QRService, SelfTestService, plus 7 near-identical makePrivateKeyOperationRouter(publicBindingInspector:handleStore:) call sites) to build the sandbox. Any new production service/adapter constructor change requires a matching, easy-to-forget update here; the 7 repeated router-construction call sites could collapse into one local closure.

The tutorial's demo identity (TutorialConfigurationFactory.keyGenerationConfiguration) is intentionally pinned to a single legacy family (.modernHighSoftwareV6, i.e. Profile B / Modern·High) rather than covering the nine-family surface — not a bug, but worth knowing when reasoning about family-specific coverage in this module: the tutorial doesn't exercise Device-Bound or Post-Quantum families at all.
maps.notes:
Views in this chunk stay thin and consistent with CLAUDE.md's Screens rule: no crypto/Keychain/business logic appears directly in a SwiftUI `body` anywhere in the 18 files — workflow orchestration lives in TutorialSessionStore (the ScreenModel-equivalent store), TutorialSandboxContainer (composition root), and TutorialConfigurationFactory (screen-configuration builder). The one file that does construct security-adjacent objects (MockSecureEnclave, MockKeychain, AuthenticationManager) is TutorialSandboxContainer.swift, and it does so only in its initializer using exclusively Mock* types from Security/Mocks/, consistent with the project's mocking convention — not a security-critical-file concern per docs/SECURITY.md §10 since no real Secure Enclave/Keychain material is touched.

Methodology note on declStats: for files with few top-level types but a large internal surface (TutorialSessionStore.swift, TutorialSandboxContainer.swift, TutorialModels.swift, TutorialTaskHostView.swift, TutorialRouteDestinationView.swift), member-level properties/functions were enumerated and checked individually (via per-symbol repo-wide grep) because that's where the actionable zero-reference findings live; for files where all top-level types were confirmed used and internal members were spot-verified against the view body during full-file reads (TutorialView.swift, TutorialShellTabsView.swift, TutorialSurfaceView.swift, TutorialSandboxChromeView.swift, TutorialConfigurationFactory.swift, TutorialGuidanceResolver.swift), scanned counts include those members but no additional grep-per-symbol pass was run beyond confirming call sites in the read source. Every symbol listed in zeroRefDeclarations was independently grepped across the whole repository (not just Sources/App/Onboarding) before being included, and each was cross-checked against Tests/ to distinguish production-zero from test-only usage per the task's instructions.

Totals across the 18 files: 350–365 declarations enumerated (exact count depends on how deeply nested SwiftUI-view private members are counted per file), with 26 confirmed zero-production-reference declarations reported above, none of which have a plausible dynamic-reachability caveat other than the LocalizedError protocol bridge on TutorialSandboxContainerError.errorDescription.

files.role: Protocol for the fail-closed relock hook every ProtectedData-backed store implements so a central coordinator can fan out relock on lock/away events.
Adapter binding the plaintext-facing ProtectedOrdinarySettingsPersistence protocol to the encrypted ProtectedSettingsStore.
Tiny registry that fans relockProtectedData() out to all registered participants and reports whether any failed.
Reads/writes the small per-domain bootstrap-metadata plist (schema version, expected generation, wrapped-DMK record version) used to detect drift before a domain's ciphertext is trusted.

smells.detail: coarseRecoveryReason is written as nil at every one of the 5 call sites (Contacts/KeyMetadata/PrivateKeyControl/ProtectedSettings/FrameworkSentinel stores) and read only once (ContactsDomainStore, as a `== nil` assertion). It never actually carries a recovery reason anywhere in the codebase — the field is fully vestigial even though it does have production references.

files.role: Pure decision function turning (bootstrap outcome, framework state) into the access-gate decision the app acts on at startup/resume.
One-shot safety valve that removes an orphaned first-domain shared-right Keychain row left behind by a crash during the very first domain's creation.
Orchestrates resuming or classifying an in-flight pending registry mutation across one or many domain recovery handlers.

Secure-Enclave device-binding key that seals/opens the root-secret envelope via ephemeral-static ECDH; the SE key's dataRepresentation is folded into the envelope so there is no separate persisted key row.
Custodian of the authenticated LAContext handoff between app-session unlock and Protected App-Data authorization, plus the view-facing relock/content-clear/post-auth generation counters.
Fans "open this domain" out to every registered domain opener after a successful app-session unlock, handling first-run domain creation and pending-mutation short-circuits.
Persisted registry state-machine model (committed membership, shared-resource lifecycle, pending create/delete mutation phases) plus its consistency and recovery-disposition rules.
Wraps the Keychain root-secret store with tracing and cancellation/denial classification for the persist/remove/load/reprotect operations the session coordinator uses.
Runtime session state machine: turns an authenticated LAContext into the in-memory wrapping root key, tracks framework state, and owns the relock fan-out.

smells.detail: Consumes KeychainError / LAError directly (via KeychainFailureClassifier / AuthenticationError checks) rather than normalizing into ProtectedDataError at this layer's boundary, unlike the project's documented PgpError→CypherAirError adapter convention.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Name collides with an unrelated closure-typed property/parameter of the same name in ProtectedSettingsAccessCoordinator/ProtectedSettingsHost, which is what every textual grep hit for "authorizeSharedRight" actually is. This specific instance method on ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator (which today only checks frameworkState == .sessionAuthorized and otherwise throws) has no call site anywhere; real authorization goes through beginProtectedDataAuthorization / beginProtectedDataAuthorizationReturningContext instead.

files.role: Keychain-backed implementation of the root-secret store; seals/opens the root secret through the device-binding provider on every save/load/reprotect.

smells.detail: Throws raw KeychainError (itemNotFound/userCancelled/authenticationFailed/interactionNotAllowed/unhandledError) up through the ProtectedData layer instead of normalizing to ProtectedDataError, so callers juggle two error vocabularies.

files.role: Self-describing authenticated envelope format and codec for the root secret, sealed under the SE device-binding key via ECDH + HKDF + AES-GCM.
A canary "domain" with no real payload, opened purely to exercise the full wrap→persist→unwrap→decrypt path early so framework corruption is caught before real user data.

smells.detail: stageInitialPayload / writePayloadGeneration / readAuthoritativeSnapshot / clearUnlockedState are near-identical copies of the same methods in ContactsDomainStore, KeyMetadataDomainStore, PrivateKeyControlStore and ProtectedSettingsStore (see crossCutting).

Companion declarations with the identical name are also dead in ProtectedSettingsStore.swift and ContactsDomainStore.swift — see crossCutting.

Splitting (2)-(4) into their own files (mirroring how ProtectedDataRootSecretEnvelope.swift is already split out) would make each concern independently discoverable.

files.role: Derives the wrapping root key and per-domain wrapping keys via HKDF, and seals/persists/loads each domain's wrapped-domain-master-key record (Keychain-backed, staged-then-committed).
files.cohesion: Bundles the WrappedDomainMasterKeyRecord envelope type + its codec together with the unrelated ProtectedDomainKeyManager class (Keychain I/O + HKDF derivation + in-memory unlocked-key cache). Elsewhere in this same directory the equivalent pairing (ProtectedDataRootSecretEnvelope vs. KeychainProtectedDataRootSecretStore) is split into two files; this file breaks that convention.

files.role: Filesystem gateway enforcing the "must live inside Application Support, must support and verify NSFileProtectionComplete" contract for every protected-data path.

smells.detail: Same stageInitialPayload / writePayloadGeneration / readAuthoritativeSnapshot / clearUnlockedState shape as the other four domain stores (see crossCutting).

files.role: Transactional engine that advances the registry through its create/delete-domain phase state machine, one saved-plist write per phase, with recovery/resume/abandon operations.

files.role: Domain store for the private-key auth-mode setting plus the rewrap / modify-expiry recovery journal, and (uniquely alongside ProtectedSettingsStore) the very-first-domain bootstrap path after app authentication.

smells.detail: stageInitialPayload / writePayloadGeneration / readAuthoritativeSnapshot / clearUnlockedState duplicate the other four domain stores; bootstrapFirstDomainAfterAppAuthenticationIfNeeded duplicates the first-domain provisioning logic in ProtectedSettingsStore.ensureCommittedIfNeeded almost line-for-line (see crossCutting).

Unlike KeyMetadataDomainStore.Payload and ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore.Payload, this Payload struct has no validateContract(), so readAuthoritativeSnapshot accepts a decoded payload without the invariant checks its siblings perform.

files.role: Single source of truth for the explicit app-lock state machine (locked/authenticating/unlocked/failed), the grace-period and away/foreground bookkeeping, and unlock-flow sequencing including the authenticated-context handoff.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: @Observable class, so this private(set) counter is in principle reachable by SwiftUI's dependency tracking, but grep finds no `.onChange(of: ...transitionGeneration)` or test read anywhere. Its own doc comment ("used by views/tests as an @Observable change signal") is stale — the sibling counters `contentClearGeneration` / `postAuthenticationGeneration` on AppSessionOrchestrator ARE consumed this way; this one is only ever incremented, never read.

files.role: Domain store for the clipboard-notice flag and the ordinary-settings passthrough, and (uniquely alongside PrivateKeyControlStore) the very-first-domain bootstrap/reset path that provisions the shared root secret.
files.cohesion: Does at least three separable jobs: (1) the generic per-domain envelope store shape shared with its four siblings, (2) the app-wide "is this the first-ever protected domain" bootstrap/reset orchestration (also independently implemented in PrivateKeyControlStore), and (3) the ordinary-settings passthrough consumed only through ProtectedSettingsOrdinarySettingsPersistence. (1) and (3) could live in a generic snapshot-store helper; (2) is arguably an app-level concern that happens to be attached here.

smells.detail: First-domain bootstrap logic (provision random secret → deriveWrappingRootKey → cleanupJournaledFirstDomainSharedRightIfNeeded → performCreateDomainTransaction) is duplicated almost line-for-line in PrivateKeyControlStore.bootstrapFirstDomainAfterAppAuthenticationIfNeeded.

readAuthoritativeSnapshot here additionally tracks highestObservedGenerationIdentifier and wraps read failures in a private ProtectedSettingsStorageReadFailure to preserve the underlying error — logic the other four domain stores' equivalent method does not have, so recovery/error-reporting behavior silently diverges across siblings.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Plain computed property (`payload?.ordinarySettings`); every real caller instead goes through the throwing `ordinarySettingsSnapshot()` method used by ProtectedSettingsOrdinarySettingsPersistence. No dot-access `<store>.ordinarySettings` exists anywhere in Sources or Tests.

Plain computed Bool property; zero `.hasCommittedDomain` accesses anywhere. Companion declarations of the identical name are also dead in ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore.swift and ContactsDomainStore.swift — see crossCutting.

files.role: Low-level SQLCipher gateway: opens/keys/validates the raw-keyed database, defines its schema, and serializes the full ContactsDomainSnapshot aggregate to/from six tables.
files.cohesion: Bundles three separable layers: (1) DB lifecycle + raw-key application + PRAGMA/runtime validation, (2) schema DDL + snapshot CRUD (insert*/load* for identities/tags/key records/certification artifacts), and (3) a generic SQLite bind/column helper library (bindText/bindBlob/bindDate/... columnString/columnData/...) that has nothing to do with Contacts specifically and would read cleanly as its own small "SQLiteStatementCoding" utility reusable by any future SQLCipher-backed domain.

crossCutting: Five domain stores — ContactsDomainStore, KeyMetadataDomainStore, PrivateKeyControlStore, ProtectedSettingsStore, ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore — each hand-roll the same ~60-80 line shape: stageInitialPayload (generate DMK → wrap → write) / writePayloadGeneration (seal → write pending → read back and verify → rotate current/previous → save bootstrap metadata) / readAuthoritativeSnapshot (iterate ProtectedDomainGenerationSlot.allCases, decode each, pick the highest generationIdentifier) / clearUnlockedState / deleteDomainArtifacts. Only the Payload type and its domain-specific validation actually differ; this is the single biggest unification opportunity in the chunk (a generic ProtectedDomainSnapshotStore<Payload> would remove most of the duplication).

The 'am I the very first protected domain, so I must provision the shared root secret myself' bootstrap path is independently implemented twice — ProtectedSettingsStore.ensureCommittedIfNeeded (empty-membership branch) and PrivateKeyControlStore.bootstrapFirstDomainAfterAppAuthenticationIfNeeded — with near-identical SensitiveBytesBox provisioning, deriveWrappingRootKey, and firstDomainSharedRightCleaner wiring.

A `hasCommittedDomain` accessor (property in two stores, throwing func in a third) was copy-pasted into ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore, ProtectedSettingsStore, and ContactsDomainStore; all three are confirmed dead — zero callers in production or tests across the whole repo — suggesting a pattern that got propagated by copy-paste and then orphaned everywhere at once rather than in just one file.

Two error vocabularies cross the ProtectedData boundary without a normalization adapter: KeychainError/LAError (raw Security/LocalAuthentication types) are thrown directly out of KeychainProtectedDataRootSecretStore and ProtectedDomainKeyManager and then pattern-matched ad hoc via KeychainFailureClassifier in ProtectedDataRootSecretCoordinator and ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator, instead of being wrapped into ProtectedDataError at the point they cross into this layer (the project's own documented convention is to normalize at the boundary, as PgpError→CypherAirError does for the FFI layer).

The nine-key-family vocabulary (Portable/Device-Bound × Legacy/Modern/Modern·High/PQ/PQ·High) itself is not duplicated across multiple files inside this chunk — the one place it appears is KeyMetadataDomainStore.Payload.validateIdentityContract, a single switch cross-checking configuration.identity against privateKeyCustodyKind. No other file in Sources/Security/ProtectedData re-implements per-family logic, so the copy-paste risk here is the generic-domain-store duplication above, not a nine-family-specific one.
maps.notes: Scope: all 26 files under Sources/Security/ProtectedData/** were read in full (no sampling/truncation); no edits were made. Dead-code claims were verified with ripgrep across the whole repo (Sources, Tests, UITests), distinguishing production references from test-only/self-referential (within-declaring-file) references; a large fraction of initial 'zero external mentions' hits turned out to be Swift type-inference artifacts (a type used only via a method's inferred return type, never spelled out by name) rather than real dead code, and are not reported as findings. The 8 confirmed zero-production-reference declarations that survived that check are listed per-file above: ProtectedDataBootstrapState (whole enum), ProtectedDataError.registryMissingWithArtifacts, ProtectedDataError.restartRequired, ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator.authorizeSharedRight(localizedReason:), and hasCommittedDomain declared independently (and independently dead) in three separate stores, plus AppLockController.transitionGeneration and ProtectedSettingsStore.ordinarySettings. No SwiftUI exists anywhere in this chunk (pure model/service/security layer), so the 'crypto in a SwiftUI body' smell class does not apply here by construction — it would need to be checked in the Views layer that consumes these stores instead.

maps.module: Contacts (incl. Import/) — Sources/App/Contacts/** — the URL-scheme / QR / file public-key import path and the Contacts feature UI
maps.notes: Method: read all 28 files in full, then ran a brace-aware Python declaration scanner (types/funcs/props/cases/typealiases at true type-member level, excluding function-local variables) over every file, and grepped the whole repo (Sources vs Tests+UITests separately) for each declared symbol, excluding the declaration's own line. Flags shown by the tool (ZERO-TOTAL / TEST-ONLY) were cross-checked manually; two additional zero-ref findings that the tool's plain word-boundary grep could not isolate from unrelated same-named identifiers elsewhere in the repo (`identifier`, `intent` — both very generic words) were confirmed by hand via qualified-access greps (`selection.identifier`, `model.intent`/`self.intent`). No Rust/UniFFI code is in this chunk, so exportedFfiUnused is empty. The single highest-value finding is structural rather than a bare zero-ref: `AppRoute.contactCertificateSignatures(fingerprint:)` (declared outside this chunk, in Sources/App/AppRoute.swift) is exhaustively handled in two switch statements and unit-tested at the ScreenModel level, but is never constructed by any navigator call site anywhere in Sources — meaning the ~1,350-line `ContactCertificateSignaturesView`+`ContactCertificateSignaturesScreenModel` pair is unreachable from real app navigation, in favor of the newer `ContactCertificationDetailsView`+`ContactCertificationDetailsScreenModel` pair (reachable via `.contactCertification`). I did not mark the two orphaned types themselves as zero-ref, since they are literally constructed in AppShellComposition.swift/TutorialRouteDestinationView.swift's switch arms (a mechanical grep says 'referenced'); the dead condition is at the route-production layer, one file outside this chunk. Flagging for maintainer confirmation before any deletion, since the two screens are also structurally near-twins (see crossCutting) so deleting the orphan would also remove most of the duplication.
crossCutting: Orphaned screen: `ContactCertificateSignaturesView`/`ContactCertificateSignaturesScreenModel` (678+670 LOC) are wired into two exhaustive AppRoute switches and have unit tests, but `AppRoute.contactCertificateSignatures(fingerprint:)` is never constructed by any navigator/NavigationLink call site in production Sources — repo-wide grep for `.contactCertificateSignatures(` and `AppRoute.contactCertificateSignatures` outside the declaration/switch-arm/test files returns nothing. All real navigation to 'verify or certify a contact's key' goes through `.contactCertification` → `ContactCertificationDetailsView`/`ScreenModel` instead.

That same near-twin pair duplicates logic verbatim rather than sharing it: the `certificationKinds` static array, the `title(for: OpenPGPCertificationKind)` switch, and the default `signatureFileImportAction` closure (SecurityScopedFileAccess read) are byte-identical in `ContactCertificateSignaturesScreenModel.swift` and `ContactCertificationDetailsScreenModel.swift`. The private `shouldIgnore(_ error:)` CancellationError/`.operationCancelled` check is copy-pasted a third time into `AddContactScreenModel.swift`. At the View layer the same pair each define their own thin, differently-named wrapper views around already-shared primitives: `ContactCertificateActionButtonLabel` vs `ContactCertificationActionLabel` (both just forward to `.cypherPrimaryActionLabelFrame`), `ContactCertificateStatusBadge` vs `ContactCertificationStatusPill` (both just forward to `CypherStatusBadge`), and `ContactCertificateSignerIdentityCard` vs `ContactCertificationSignerIdentityView` (near-identical field layout).

Hand-rolled 'generation counter + cancel-then-compare Task' bookkeeping (`...LoadGeneration`/`...OperationGeneration: UInt64 = 0`, cancel old task, increment generation, guard `generation == self.xGeneration` before applying results) is reimplemented independently in `AddContactScreenModel` (qrProcessingTask/Generation), `ContactCertificateSignaturesScreenModel` (catalogLoadTask+operationTask, 2 generations), and `ContactCertificationDetailsScreenModel` (loadTask+operationTask, 2 generations) — and, outside this chunk, in `SelectiveRevocationScreenModel`, `KeyDetailScreenModel`, and `PostGenerationPromptScreenModel`. No shared 'generation-guarded async operation' utility exists project-wide; this is the single most repeated pattern in the chunk.

`ContactsAvailability.unavailableSystemImage` (in `ContactsAvailability+Presentation.swift`) was clearly meant to centralize the 'availability state → SF Symbol' mapping alongside its sibling `unavailableTitle`/`unavailableDescription` (both of which ARE reused across 6+ files) — but it is itself dead code, because `ContactsView.swift` and `ContactDetailView.swift` each hand-roll their own private `systemImage(for:)` switch instead of calling it, and the two hand-rolled copies have already drifted apart: `.availableProtectedDomain` maps to `"person.2"` in `ContactsView` but `"person"` in `ContactDetailView`. `TagDetailView`/`TagManagementView` sidestep the question entirely by hardcoding a single `"tag"` icon regardless of availability state.

Nine-key-family display logic is *not* duplicated in this chunk — `ContactKeySummaryView`, both cert-signature screens, and `ImportConfirmView` all correctly call the single shared `PGPKeyProfile.contactKeyKindDisplayName` extension (`Sources/App/Common/PGPKeyProfile+ContactPresentation.swift`, outside this chunk) rather than re-deriving a family→label mapping locally. Noted as a clean spot, not a finding.

The `outputInterceptionPolicy` customization seam on both `ContactCertificateSignaturesView.Configuration` and `ContactCertificationDetailsConfiguration` is read in production (`configuration.outputInterceptionPolicy.interceptDataExport?(...)`) but only ever given a non-default value in tests — in shipped code it is always `.passthrough`. Not dead code (it IS read), but an unused customization point worth noting alongside the two Configuration structs.

files.role: App-scene onOpenURL entry point: gates on local-data-reset-restart-required, then forwards the URL and current tutorial-presentation state to IncomingURLImportCoordinator.

Shared 'inspect key data → build a gated ImportConfirmationRequest → import on verified/unverified confirmation' workflow, reused identically by both the URL-scheme path (IncomingURLImportCoordinator) and the in-app Add Contact screen (AddContactScreenModel).

maps.module: Sources/Services/KeyManagement/** + Sources/Services/KeyManagementService.swift (key lifecycle + private-key operation routing subsystem)
maps.notes: 27 files, ~5,193 total lines (4,562 in KeyManagement/ + 631 in the facade). This is pure Swift orchestration over adapters (PGPKeyOperationAdapter / PGPMessageOperationAdapter / PGPCertificateOperationAdapter / SecureEnclaveManageable) — no direct UniFFI/#[uniffi::export] call sites in this chunk, so exportedFfiUnused is N/A here (that audit belongs to the Rust/adapter layer, not this chunk). No print/NSLog/os_log/Logger calls anywhere in the chunk (grep hits on 'Fingerprint(' are substring false-positives of 'print(') — constraint #4 (no plaintext/key logging) is clean for this subsystem. Dead-code sweep: for every declaration in every file I grepped the whole repo (word-boundary match) and classified hits into self-file / other Sources/** (production) / Tests+UITests. Every symbol with prod==0 but self>1 was internally self-consumed (called from elsewhere in its own file, e.g. a public overload delegating to a private worker) and is therefore NOT dead — only self<=1 AND prod==0 counts as a real zero-production-reference finding. That check surfaced exactly 5 declarations, all in PGPKeyCapabilityResolver.swift, all referenced only from Tests/ServiceTests/*.swift. I verified the composition root (Sources/App/AppContainer.swift) constructs every other type in this chunk (all nine Private*Service classes, both SecureEnclaveCustodyGeneration* services, KeyMutationService, KeyProvisioningService, etc.), confirming they are genuinely wired into production, not orphaned.
crossCutting: Eight near-identical 'private-key operation' service classes (PrivateKeyCleartextSigningService, PrivateKeyContactCertificationService, PrivateKeyDetachedFileSigningService, PrivateKeyPasswordMessageEncryptionService, PrivateKeyStreamingFileEncryptionService, PrivateKeyTextEncryptionService, PrivateKeySelectiveRevocationService for the signing role, plus PrivateKeyMessageDecryptionService/PrivateKeyStreamingFileDecryptionService for the key-agreement role) all repeat the exact same skeleton: route(for:) -> switch over PrivateKeyOperationRoute { .softwareSecretCertificate: unwrap+call; .secureEnclaveSigner/.secureEnclaveKeyAgreement: call external-P256 bridge; .secureEnclaveCompositeSigner/.secureEnclaveCompositeKeyAgreement: switch tier { .postQuantum: ML-DSA-65/ML-KEM-768 bridge; .postQuantumHigh: ML-DSA-87/ML-KEM-1024 bridge }; mismatched role: throw .privateOperationRoleMismatch; .blocked: throw resolution.failureCategory }. This ~30-40 line block is copy-pasted essentially verbatim across all 9 files (~700+ duplicated lines total). A single generic executor (closure-per-branch, or a protocol-witness table keyed by route case) would collapse this into one place and make future key-family additions a one-site change instead of a 9-file grep-and-paste.

The postQuantum/postQuantumHigh two-way `switch route.signingHandle.reference.tier` (or `.keyAgreementHandle.reference.tier`) dispatching to ML-DSA-65/ML-KEM-768 vs ML-DSA-87/ML-KEM-1024 provider-bridge construction repeats verbatim in 8 of the files above plus PrivateKeyExpiryMutationService.swift — the same 15-20 line branch, differing only in which adapter method and bridge type name is used.

PrivateKeyOperationRouting.swift defines four structurally identical route-payload structs (SecureEnclaveSignerRoute, SecureEnclaveKeyAgreementRoute, SecureEnclaveCompositeSignerRoute, SecureEnclaveCompositeKeyAgreementRoute) — same {identity, operation, binding-inspection, handle, operationAuthorization[, classicalComponent]} shape and same custom-init-with-nil-default boilerplate, four times over.

PrivateKeyOperationRouter.swift's routeSecureEnclaveSigningOperation/routeSecureEnclaveKeyAgreementOperation (single-key custody) and routeSecureEnclaveCompositeSigningOperation/routeSecureEnclaveCompositeKeyAgreementOperation (split custody) are two pairs of near-duplicate 'locate handle pair -> authorize -> load handle (+classical component for composite) -> construct route' methods, essentially the same control flow copy-pasted per role and per single-vs-composite custody.

SecureEnclaveCustodyGenerationService.swift duplicates its entire authorize/build/commit/rollback skeleton twice: performGenerateKey/createAuthorizedHandlePair/rollbackGeneratedState for single P-256 custody, and performGenerateCompositeKey/createAuthorizedCompositeHandlePair/rollbackGeneratedCompositeState for composite (Device-Bound Post-Quantum) custody.

KeyManagementService.generateKey and .importKey duplicate an identical 5-step pre-check dance (makeToken -> await beforeAuthModeReadCheckpoint -> checkCancellation -> checkValid -> requireUnlockedAuthMode -> checkCancellation -> checkValid -> delegate) before diverging into their own provisioning call — worth extracting into one shared 'prepare authorized provisioning token' helper.

files.role: In-memory key-identity catalog plus metadata-persistence sync (load/store/update/delete/default-key bookkeeping).
Export workflows: passphrase-protected secret-key export, revocation-certificate export, public-key export.
Key mutation orchestration: modify-expiry (software/SE/composite routes), key deletion (software+SE+composite handle cleanup), default-key set, modify-expiry crash recovery.
files.cohesion: Four genuinely separate jobs bundled under one 'mutation' umbrella — modify-expiry (routing + software rewrap + SE/composite delegation, ~260 loc), deletion (keychain + SE handle + composite handle cleanup with best-effort error accumulation, ~180 loc), default-key set (1 line, delegates), and interrupted-modify-expiry crash recovery. Splitting into a KeyExpiryMutationService and a KeyDeletionService (default-key stays trivial either way) would make each testable/readable independently; the two don't share meaningful helpers.

smells.detail: One class owns modify-expiry, deletion, default-key, and crash-recovery — four largely-independent workflows that only share the catalogStore/keychain dependencies, not logic.

deleteKeychainMaterialAndMetadata and deleteSecureEnclaveCustodyKey both follow the same 'collect errors from N sub-steps, never short-circuit, report once' shape; and the modify-expiry route switch duplicates the family-wide route-dispatch pattern documented in crossCutting.

files.role: Concurrency guard tracking in-flight provisioning commits so a relock can wait for them to drain before locking metadata.
Generation-counter token gate that cancels stale in-flight provisioning operations after a relock/invalidate.

smells.detail: Five 'static let test*' Policy fixtures live in the production file and are referenced only from Tests/ServiceTests/*.swift (PGPKeyCapabilityResolverTests, PrivateKey*ServiceTests, KeyGenerationScreenModelTests, SecureEnclaveCustodyGenerationServiceTests, KeyManagementServiceTestSupport) — zero production call sites. One (testSecureEnclaveOperationsBlocked) is self-documented in its own doc comment as a test-only fixture that 'no longer mirrors any production shape' since production now exposes those operations (P7D). These should move to a test-target fixture file rather than ship inside Sources/.

maps.module: Sources/Security — SecureEnclave custody/composite handle stack + Mocks (nine-family PGP custody surface)

files.role: Seals/unseals the classical Ed25519+X25519 (or Ed448+X448) secret halves of a Device-Bound Post-Quantum composite identity under a fixed-biometry SE envelope; the PQC halves live elsewhere (SecureEnclaveCompositeHandleStore).

smells.detail: Clean single-purpose store; nested reference-type ClassicalComponent with explicit zeroize()+deinit is a deliberate, well-documented pattern for handing plaintext across a route boundary.

files.role: Identity/reference/binding/pair vocabulary for split-custody PQC composite Secure Enclave keys: tier enum (postQuantum/postQuantumHigh), handle references, public bindings, pairs, and loaded-handle types.
files.cohesion: All 7 types are one cohesive 'composite handle vocabulary', so not several unrelated jobs — but it consolidates into one file what the classical custody family spreads across ~10 separate single-type files (HandlePair.swift, HandlePublicBinding.swift, HandleReference.swift, LoadedHandle.swift, LoadedHandlePair.swift, HandleState.swift, ...). That asymmetry is itself worth normalizing (see crossCutting) before a tenth family adds a third variant.

smells.detail: static let mldsa65PublicKeyLength / mlkem768PublicKeyLength on SecureEnclaveCompositeHandlePublicBinding duplicate the values already exposed via SecureEnclaveCompositeTier.signingPublicKeyLength/keyAgreementPublicKeyLength and are never read from either location — a second, unused source of truth for the same two magic numbers.

extension PGPKeyConfiguration.Identity (deviceBoundCompositeTier) lives inside a Security file rather than near the Models type or a dedicated routing file; defensible (it's the SE-routing dispatch key) but worth a comment pointing back to Models/PGPKeyConfiguration.swift.

files.role: Pair-level lifecycle (create-with-rollback, authenticated load, non-prompting locate-by-public-key, delete, reset cleanup) for the composite/PQC Secure Enclave handle family, mirroring SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleStore for the classical family.

smells.detail: createLoadedHandlePair/deleteReferences/cleanupAllHandlesForLocalDataReset are near-structural duplicates of the equivalent methods in SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleStore.swift (create-two-then-rollback-on-partial-failure; delete-tolerating-missing loop) — see crossCutting.

Unlike the classical store, this file has no inventorySummaryForLocalRecovery equivalent (only a bare inventoryHandleCount()), so the local-data-reset recovery report has less introspection for the PQC composite family than for the P-256 custody family.

files.role: Storage protocol abstraction (create/load/inventory/delete) for composite PQC private-key blobs; production conformer lives outside this chunk (SystemSecureEnclaveCompositeKeyStore.swift).
SE-resident ML-DSA sign / ML-KEM decapsulate primitives for the two composite tiers (65/768 and 87/1024), each with length validation and error normalization.

smells.detail: signMlDsa65Digest/signMlDsa87Digest are near-identical bodies (role check, digest-length guard, enum-case match, signature-length guard) differing only in the enum case and length constant; same pattern repeats for decapsulateMlKem768/decapsulateMlKem1024. CryptoKit's per-parameter-set static types make full generics awkward, but a small shared helper parameterized on (expectedRole, PrivateKeyPayload, expectedOutputLength) would cut this file roughly in half.

files.role: Value-type wrapper describing one SecAccessControl policy (fixed to privateKeyUsageBiometryAny today) and building the concrete SecAccessControl.

smells.detail: makeSecAccessControl() re-validates its own stored fields (accessibility/requiresPrivateKeyUsage/requiresBiometryAny/permitsDevicePasscodeFallback) against hardcoded expected values, but the only value ever constructed anywhere in the repo is the static .privateKeyUsageBiometryAny — so the struct presents as a general policy value type while only one instance is ever created; the guard is effectively unreachable-false. Not a bug, but the abstraction buys nothing yet.

files.role: P-256 raw (r,s) signature production over an SE custody signing handle, including a hand-rolled minimal ASN.1/DER parser for the X9.62 fallback path.
Two-case enum reporting whether a custody handle pair is currently usable, or unavailable with a failure category.
Value type summarizing a local-data-reset cleanup pass over custody handles (inspected/deleted counts, failure category).
Central error enum for both custody families (P-256 and composite PQC) plus the shared OSStatus/CFError-to-domain-error mapper used across the digest signer, key-agreement, and key-store paths.
Parses one raw Keychain application-tag into a typed handle reference (or marks it malformed) for inventory/recovery scanning.

smells.detail: isMalformed is dead (see zeroRefDeclarations); SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleStore.inventorySummaryForLocalRecovery() reimplements the identical 'reference == nil' check manually instead of calling item.isMalformed, so the two pieces of logic can drift.

files.role: Value type totaling complete/partial/ambiguous/malformed handle-set counts for the local-recovery report.

smells.detail: The struct's individual fields (totalHandleCount, completeSetCount, etc.) are constructed in SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleStore and threaded through SecureEnclaveCustodyGenerationRecoveryReport/Service as an opaque payload, but nothing in production code reads the individual fields back out — only XCTest assertions do. Worth confirming the recovery report actually surfaces this data to a user/log somewhere, or it's dead data flow.

files.role: Validated (signing, keyAgreement) public-binding pair for the classical P-256 custody family, with the invariant checks (matching handle-set id, distinct public keys) in its init.
One role-tagged, shape-validated uncompressed P-256 X9.63 public key bound to a handle reference.
Identity of one classical custody private-key SecKey item (handle-set id + role), including the Keychain application-tag encode/decode and random handle-set-id generation.
Four-case enum (missing/partial/complete/invalid) describing the inspected state of one handle-set identifier.

The full lifecycle surface for classical (P-256) custody handle pairs: create/load, inspect/classify availability, locate-by-public-key across the whole Keychain inventory, recovery-inventory summary, and delete (three overloads) including partial-handle-set reconciliation.
files.cohesion: One struct, five distinguishable responsibilities (create, inspect/classify, locate-by-scan, recovery reporting, delete+partial-reconciliation). The partial-handle reconciliation trio (locateMatchingPartialReferences/matchingPartialReferences/failIfPartialSetMatchesExpectedPublicKey, ~100 lines) is dense, self-contained, and only reachable from the delete-by-public-key path — a good candidate to extract into its own type (e.g. a PartialHandleSetReconciler) so the 545-line file reads as create/load/locate/delete at a glance.

smells.detail: loadSigningHandle/loadKeyAgreementHandle exist as public convenience wrappers around locateHandlePair+loadHandle, but every production call site (PrivateKeyOperationRouter.swift) calls locateHandlePair+loadHandle directly instead — the two wrappers are exercised only by SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleStoreTests.swift.

deleteHandlePair(handleSetIdentifier:) has zero references anywhere in the repo, including tests — a fully unused third overload alongside deleteHandlePair(_:) and deleteHandlePair(signingPublicKeyX963:keyAgreementPublicKeyX963:).

test-only: called from Tests/ServiceTests/SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleStoreTests.swift, never from Sources/ production code

files.role: Raw P-256 ECDH shared-secret derivation via an SE custody key-agreement handle, with a validated/zeroizable SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret carrier and a documented CoW-zeroization limitation.

crossCutting: The classical (P-256) custody family and the composite (PQC) family maintain two structurally-parallel handle-lifecycle stores — SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleStore.swift (545 lines) and SecureEnclaveCompositeHandleStore.swift (241 lines) — with copy-pasted create-two-then-rollback, delete-tolerating-missing, and cleanup-for-reset logic. As a 3rd/4th composite tier or a 10th key family is added, this duplication compounds; a shared generic 'handle-pair lifecycle' core (parameterized on the reference/binding/loaded-handle types) would remove most of it.

Error-mapping is reimplemented three times with slightly different shapes: SecureEnclaveCustodyOSStatusMapper (shared, good) is wrapped by three separate private mapCFError/mapEnclaveOperationError helpers in SecureEnclaveCustodyDigestSigner.swift, SecureEnclaveCustodyKeyAgreement.swift, and SecureEnclaveCompositeOperations.swift instead of one shared adapter.

SecureEnclaveP256RawSignature (r/s) and SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret (raw) both hand-roll the identical '32 bytes, not all-zero, else privateHandleInaccessible' validation — a shared value wrapper would remove the duplication and guarantee the two checks never drift.

The composite (PQC) vocabulary consolidates 7 types into one file (SecureEnclaveCompositeHandle.swift) while the classical vocabulary spreads the same conceptual pieces across ~10 files (HandlePair/HandlePublicBinding/HandleReference/LoadedHandle/LoadedHandlePair/HandleState/...) — worth picking one convention before a further family is added.

Mock*.swift test doubles are also live production code paths (Sources/App/Onboarding/TutorialSandboxContainer.swift and AppContainer.makeUITest under #if DEBUG use MockKeychain/MockSecureEnclave/MockAuthenticator/MockProtectedDataRootSecretStore directly), but only their protocol-conformance methods are exercised there — essentially the entire call-tracking/error-injection instrumentation surface of all three mocks is either test-only or fully unreferenced (see per-file zeroRefDeclarations), suggesting the mocks could shed a lot of unused bookkeeping.

SecureEnclaveCustodyKeyStoring (classical) is not Sendable while its composite sibling SecureEnclaveCompositeKeyStoring explicitly is — an asymmetry between two protocols meant to model the same shape of thing for different key types.
maps.notes: Scope covered: all 23 files matching `Sources/Security/SecureEnclave*.swift` at the package root plus all 3 files under `Sources/Security/Mocks/`. Methodology: read every file in full, then for every top-level and nested declaration ran `grep -rn --include='*.swift' -w '<symbol>'` across the whole repository (excluding node_modules/build artifacts), classifying each hit as production (Sources/, App target) vs. test (Tests/, UITests/). A declaration is reported zero-ref only when grep found no reads/calls beyond its own declaration/self-mutation site; where the only external hits were from Tests/*, it is still listed per the task's rule but the dynamicUsageCaveat says 'test-only'. Two files that implement protocols declared in this chunk — `Sources/Security/SystemSecureEnclaveCompositeKeyStore.swift` and `Sources/Security/SystemSecureEnclaveCustodyKeyStore.swift` — sit outside the `SecureEnclave*.swift` glob (their names start with `System`) and were consulted only to confirm cross-file usage, not enumerated for their own dead code. One property, MockProtectedDataRootSecretStore.deleteError, could not be confirmed as fully test-only in the time available (a static grep found no external assignment, but the search space for this one symbol name is small enough that a false negative is more likely than for the others) — flagged with a caveat rather than a hard verdict. All findings are grep-based/mechanical as instructed; final adjudication (e.g. via the Swift compiler's unused-declaration warnings, or SwiftUI/Codable reflection that a text grep can't see) is left to a follow-up pass.

files.role: Grab-bag of the auth domain model: app-session (lock-screen) authentication policy, the AuthenticationMode enum, the private-key-control recovery-journal state machine (+ its in-memory test double), and the AuthenticationEvaluable protocol the file is named after.

Splitting into AppSessionAuthenticationPolicy.swift, PrivateKeyControlState.swift, and a slim AuthenticationEvaluable.swift would make each file's name match its content.

files.role: Central @Observable authentication manager: evaluates Face ID/Touch ID for private-key ops and app-session unlock, creates SecAccessControl, and orchestrates/delegates mode-switch and crash-recovery to the sub-coordinators it constructs.
files.cohesion: One coherent responsibility (authentication), but evaluate(mode:reason:source:) and evaluateAppSession(policy:reason:source:) are near-duplicate ~150-line methods (bypass short-circuit -> withPrivacyPrompt wrap -> policy-specific LAError catch cascade -> trace start/finish at every branch) that could share a single generic helper, which would materially shrink the file.

smells.detail: private func traceErrorMetadata(_:extra:) here duplicates the same private helper in PrivateKeyRewrapWorkflow.swift and PrivateKeyModeSwitchAuthenticator.swift, and all three overlap heavily with the already-shared AuthErrorTraceMetadata.errorMetadata used elsewhere in this same subsystem.

evaluate(mode:reason:source:) and evaluateAppSession(policy:reason:source:) repeat the same bypass/prompt-wrap/LAError-cascade/trace-at-every-step shape almost line-for-line for two different policy types.

files.role: Tracks nested 'privacy' vs 'operation' system-authentication-prompt depth/generation counters so app-lifecycle handlers (away/resume) can distinguish a real background event from a transient Face ID sheet.

Read only from Tests/ServiceTests/AuthLifecycleTraceStoreTests.swift; zero production call sites.
