smells.detail: The entire SensitiveData class (and every member: data, withUnsafeBytes, count, isEmpty, init, zeroize(), deinit) is exercised only by Tests/FFIIntegrationTests/FFIIntegrationTests+MemoryZeroing.swift. All production sensitive-buffer zeroing in this codebase actually goes through the separate Data.zeroize() extension (Sources/Extensions/Data+Zeroing.swift) called directly on raw Data, not through this wrapper — e.g. PrivateKeyRewrapWorkflow.swift's `rawKeyBytes.resetBytes(in:...)`. Either SensitiveData should be adopted where raw key/passphrase Data is held, or the class (and its test) should be removed as unused SECURITY-CRITICAL surface.

zeroRefDeclarations.kind: type (final class, plus all its members)
zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Only referenced from Tests/FFIIntegrationTests/FFIIntegrationTests+MemoryZeroing.swift (init + .data + .zeroize()); zero references anywhere in Sources.

crossCutting: private func traceErrorMetadata(_:extra:) is copy-pasted (near-identical body: errorType + LAError code/name) across AuthenticationManager.swift, PrivateKeyRewrapWorkflow.swift, and PrivateKeyModeSwitchAuthenticator.swift, and it overlaps heavily with the already-shared AuthErrorTraceMetadata.errorMetadata(_:extra:) used elsewhere in the same subsystem (e.g. AuthenticationPromptCoordinator.swift, AuthTraceMetadata.swift) — collapsing all three private copies onto the one shared utility would remove ~45 lines with no behavior change.
private extension NSLock { func withLock<T>(...) rethrows -> T } is duplicated verbatim between AuthLifecycleTraceStore.swift and AuthenticationPromptCoordinator.swift.
KeychainManager.swift repeats an identical OSStatus->KeychainError switch across load/update/delete/listItems (4x); a single mapping helper would remove most of that file's bulk.

No nine-key-family duplication was found in this chunk: AuthenticationManager, KeychainManager, KeyBundleStore, PrivateKeyEnvelope, and the rewrap/migration coordinators all operate purely on opaque fingerprint strings and KeyBundleNamespace, with zero per-profile (Legacy/Modern/Modern-High/PQC/PQC-High, Portable/Device-Bound) branching anywhere in these 20 files. Any family-specific logic lives in Services/KeyManagement and the SecureEnclaveComposite*/SecureEnclaveCustody* files, which are out of scope for this chunk.
maps.notes: Scope covered: the 20 root, non-SecureEnclave .swift files under Sources/Security (Argon2idMemoryGuard, MemoryInfoProvidable, AuthErrorTraceMetadata, AuthLifecycleTraceStore, AuthTraceMetadata, AuthenticationEvaluable, AuthenticationManager, AuthenticationPromptCoordinator, EnvelopePlistInspector, KeyBundleStore, KeyMetadataPersistence, KeyMigrationCoordinator, KeychainFailure, KeychainManageable, KeychainManager, PrivateKeyEnvelope, PrivateKeyModeSwitchAuthenticator, PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator, PrivateKeyRewrapWorkflow, MemoryZeroingUtility). MemoryInfoProvidable.swift was included even though the prompt's explicit list names only Argon2idMemoryGuard, because it is Argon2idMemoryGuard's sole protocol dependency and is one of the root non-SecureEnclave .swift files; flag if it should be excluded. All 25 SecureEnclave*/SystemSecureEnclave*.swift files and the Mocks/ and ProtectedData/ subdirectories were intentionally left untouched per the task's file list. Zero-reference determination method: ripgrep for each declaration's identifier across the whole repo (Sources = production, Tests/UITests = test), manually verifying call sites (not just substring hits) and distinguishing 'zero references anywhere' from 'test-only, zero production references' per the task's instructions. No files were edited. Standout finding: Sources/Security/MemoryZeroingUtility.swift's SensitiveData class — a SECURITY-CRITICAL-labeled auto-zeroing wrapper — has zero production adopters; the codebase zeroizes sensitive Data directly via the Data.zeroize() extension instead. Two other findings (KeyMigrationRecoverySummary.isNoActionSafeOnly, AuthenticationManager's private UserDefaults.contains(key:)) have zero references anywhere, including tests. The remaining seven zero-production-reference items are read only from unit/device tests.

Scope: 24 files, 840 LOC (Contacts models 767 LOC across 21 files; Extensions 79 LOC across 3 files). All are pure Swift value types (structs/enums) plus a handful of extensions — no SwiftUI `body` code and no crypto/Keychain calls live in this chunk directly, so the "crypto in a body" smell class doesn't apply here; the two Extensions files that ARE security-sensitive (Data+Zeroing.swift, and indirectly Data+BigEndian.swift for AAD/HKDF binding) are flagged per CLAUDE.md's security-file convention. "Production" = Sources/**; "test" = Tests/** + UITests/**; a symbol with reads only in Tests/UITests is reported as zero-production-ref per the task's instruction. Every top-level type/enum and its direct members (stored/computed properties, funcs, cases) was grepped individually across the whole repo (Sources, Tests, UITests, pgp-mobile, .pbxproj); declStats.scanned counts reflect that per-declaration enumeration, not file/type counts. Two genuinely dead value types (ContactMergePreview entirely; ContactImportResult.contact/.key convenience accessors) and one dead ContactsAvailability predicate pair (requiresUnlock/blocksMutations) plus one dead ContactKeySummary predicate (isOpenPGPCertified) were found with zero references anywhere, including tests. ContactMergeResult's three fields are produced by ContactService but the sole production caller discards the whole struct (`_ = try`) — only test code reads survivingContact/removedContactId/preferredKeyNeedsSelection, suggesting a "select preferred key after merge" UI follow-up was planned but never wired up. Data+Zeroing.swift's `Array<UInt8>.zeroize()` extension is unused in production; real [UInt8] key-material zeroing in the codebase (SQLCipherRawKey.zeroize, SQLCipherPreflightProbe.zeroizeKey) reimplements the same dead-store-elimination-defeating pattern independently rather than calling this extension — a duplication smell alongside the dead-code one. ContactCandidateMatch.contactIds is written in production but only ever read by test assertions. ContactIdentity/ContactIdentitySummary.notes is threaded all the way through the SQLCipher schema and the summary projector but is always constructed nil, has no mutation API, and no view ever reads it — a dead feature stub, called out as a smell rather than a zero-ref finding since the field does have production references (persistence code touches it, just no consumer). No 9-key-family-specific duplication was found in this chunk (Contacts models are almost entirely family-agnostic — only ContactKeyRecord.profile: PGPKeyProfile touches key-family identity); the duplication that does exist is the repeated "persisted record + Hashable/Identifiable summary projection" pairing (ContactIdentity/ContactIdentitySummary, ContactKeyRecord/ContactKeySummary, ContactTag/ContactTagSummary), which looks like a deliberate, consistent convention rather than accidental copy-paste.
crossCutting: ContactKeyRecord vs ContactKeySummary duplicate ~17 fields verbatim (fingerprint, profile, primaryAlgo, subkeyAlgo, hasEncryptionSubkey, isRevoked, isExpired, manualVerificationState, usageState, certificationProjection, certificationArtifactIds, ...) plus an identical canEncryptTo computed property in both — worth a shared 'key facts' protocol if a 10th key family is ever added.

ContactIdentity/ContactIdentitySummary and ContactTag/ContactTagSummary repeat the same persisted-record + read-model-projection doubling pattern as ContactKeyRecord/ContactKeySummary — consistent across the whole directory (a real convention), but ContactIdentity(Summary).notes shows the doubling can drift: the field is carried through both types and the SQLCipher schema with zero consumers.

No nine-key-family-specific duplication found in this chunk: only ContactKeyRecord.profile: PGPKeyProfile references key-family identity at all; everything else here is family-agnostic, so there's nothing to unify across the nine families in Contacts/Extensions specifically.

Two independent 'defeat compiler dead-store elimination' byte-zeroing implementations exist for [UInt8]: Sources/Extensions/Data+Zeroing.swift's `Array<UInt8>.zeroize()` (unused in production, only called from Tests/FFIIntegrationTests/FFIIntegrationTests+MemoryZeroing.swift) vs Sources/Services/SQLCipher/{SQLCipherRawKey.zeroize, SQLCipherPreflightProbe.zeroizeKey} (the ones actually used for SQLCipher key material) — same opaque-write trick reimplemented twice.

files.role: Value type describing a fuzzy-match result surfaced during public-key import (match strength + candidate contact ids/display info).

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Set by ContactImportMatcher when constructing the match; only ever read via `candidate.contactIds` in XCTAssertEqual in ContactServicePersonModelTests.swift — no production reader (ImportConfirmView only reads .strength/.displayName/.primaryEmail).

files.role: Enum of fuzzy-match confidence tiers (strong/weak/ambiguousStrong) used by ContactCandidateMatch and rendered as conflict-warning copy in ImportConfirmView.

Certification-artifact domain model: source/validation-status vocab, a target selector (direct-key vs specific User ID) with its own validation, and the artifact record itself with persistence-shaping logic (validatedForPersistence/validatePayload) and a SHA-256 digest helper.
files.cohesion: Three related-but-separable jobs live here (enum vocabulary, ContactCertificationTargetSelector + its validate()/deduplicationKey, and ContactCertificationArtifactReference + its persistence-shaping methods and static crypto helper); largest file in the directory by a wide margin. Splitting TargetSelector into its own file would reduce it without hurting cohesion, since it already has an independent validate() contract.

smells.detail: validatePayload/validatedForPersistence throw ContactsDomainValidationError and are invoked directly by ContactsDomainSnapshot.validateContract and ContactSnapshotMutator — a Models/ file carries persistence-shaping business rules (normally a Services-layer concern) rather than being a plain value type. Consistent with how ContactsDomainSnapshot itself is written (same convention), so likely intentional, but worth confirming it's the sanctioned pattern rather than scope creep.

files.role: Derived read-model flattening a key's certification artifacts into a single status + artifactIds projection, embedded in both ContactKeyRecord and ContactKeySummary.
Persisted contact-identity record (display name, primary email, tag membership, timestamps) — the DB-facing counterpart to ContactIdentitySummary.

smells.detail: The `notes` field is threaded through construction (ContactSnapshotMutator always passes notes: nil), the SQLCipher schema, and ContactSummaryProjector, but there is no API anywhere to set it to a non-nil value and no view reads `.notes` — it has production references (so it's not a strict zero-ref finding) but is effectively an inert stub for a contact-notes feature that was never built out.

files.role: Read-only Hashable/Identifiable projection of a contact identity plus its resolved tags and keys (preferredKey/additionalActiveKeys/historicalKeys/canEncryptTo/hasUnverifiedKeys), the primary type consumed by Contacts and Encrypt UI.

smells.detail: Same `notes` passthrough issue as ContactIdentity.swift — carried in the summary but never read by any view.

files.role: Enum describing the outcome of importing a public key (added/addedWithCandidate/duplicate/updated) plus convenience `.contact`/`.key` accessors.

smells.detail: Every consumer (ContactImportWorkflow, TutorialSessionStore) destructures the enum cases directly via `switch result { case .added(let contact, _), ... }` rather than calling the `.contact`/`.key` computed properties, so those two accessors are unreferenced by anyone.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Computed var switching on self; zero call sites (`result.contact` / `importResult.contact`) found anywhere in Sources or Tests.
Computed var switching on self; zero call sites (`result.key` / `importResult.key`) found anywhere in Sources or Tests.

files.role: One-tap self-diagnostic covering all software key families: generation → encrypt/decrypt → sign/verify → tamper-detection → export/import round-trip, plus a profile-agnostic QR round-trip, producing an in-memory export-only report (wired to a Settings screen).
files.cohesion: Single well-scoped job; the per-profile test loop generalizes automatically across all nine key families via PGPKeyProfile.allCases rather than hardcoding per-family branches.

crossCutting: verificationContext() — a private helper that builds a PGPMessageVerificationContext from contactService.contactsVerificationContext() + keyManagement.keys.map(\.publicKeyData) — is duplicated near-verbatim across DecryptionService.swift, SigningService.swift, and PasswordMessageService.swift (3 separate private implementations of the same 6-line body). Worth extracting to a single shared helper (e.g. a small free function or an extension on ContactService/KeyManagementService) since all three need to stay in sync whenever the verification-context shape changes.

The 'require-available, snapshot, mutate, persist-if-mutated' shape (mutableRuntimeSnapshot() → snapshotMutator.X(&snapshot) → if mutation.didMutate persist) is repeated by hand at ~15 call sites in ContactService.swift; a single generic wrapper (`func mutate<T>(_ body: (inout ContactsDomainSnapshot) throws -> Mutation<T>) throws -> T`) would remove that boilerplate.

None of the 20 files in this chunk hardcode per-key-family branching — profile-specific logic lives in the Rust engine/FFI adapters, not this layer. SelfTestService's PGPKeyProfile.allCases loop is the one place driven by the family count, and it already generalizes correctly across all nine families without special-casing, so there is no nine-family copy-paste to unify here.

Two structurally similar 'diagnostic/self-check' subsystems exist side by side with very different reachability: SelfTestService is wired into a Settings screen and fully reachable, while SQLCipherPreflightProbe (conceptually the same kind of one-shot sanity check, for SQLCipher this time) has no UI or startup wiring at all and is only exercised by its own unit test — worth deciding whether it should be surfaced similarly or moved under Tests/ since it currently reads as production code with no production caller.
maps.notes:
Headline finding: Sources/Services/PasswordMessageService.swift is constructed and stored on AppContainer but has zero reachable call sites anywhere in Sources/App — none of encryptText/encryptBinary/decryptMessageDetailed are invoked by any View or ScreenModel; it is only exercised by its own unit tests. This differs from every other sibling crypto-orchestration service (Encryption/Decryption/Signing/CertificateSignature/QR/SelfTest), all of which are wired into real screens with multiple call sites. Worth a maintainer decision: either wire a password-message UI, or remove the service (and its still-referenced PasswordMessageEncrypting/PasswordMessageEnvelopeFormat dependencies would need re-checking before deletion, since those live outside this chunk).

Second-largest theme: a small set of ContactService/KeyManagementService/DecryptionService/CertificateSignatureService public convenience methods are dead — each has a 'the production code does the equivalent thing a slightly different way' explanation (e.g. exportKeyBackupData+confirmKeyBackupExported instead of KeyManagementService.exportKey; parseRecipients+decryptDetailed instead of DecryptionService.decryptMessageDetailed; direct .artifact != nil check instead of ContactCertificationArtifactValidation.canSave). These read like APIs that were the 'obvious first convenience method' when the surrounding feature was built, then got bypassed once the real UI flow needed a different shape, and were never deleted.

QRService.inspectKeyMetadata(keyData:) and ContactService.refreshCertificationProjections() are the two strongest findings — zero references anywhere in the entire repo, including tests, not just zero production references.

Dead-code methodology note: "zero production references" was computed per-declaration via whole-repo grep, counting any Sources/ call site (including from within the same file, and including call sites in another Sources file that is itself unreachable from the UI, e.g. ContactSnapshotMutator.removeKey is still counted as "used" because ContactService.removeContact calls it, even though removeContact itself has no UI caller). Only declarations with literally zero Sources-tree references (or references from Tests/ only) are listed in zeroRefDeclarations. sizeConcern was judged by distinct-responsibility count, not raw line count — KeyManagementService is long but mostly thin delegation (watch, not oversized); ContactSnapshotMutator and ContactService are flagged oversized because they each visibly bundle 3-4 weakly-related jobs.

files.cohesion: One cohesive job (certificate signing/certification/revocation), but exploded into a plain + P256 + composite + composite-High quadruplet per operation (12 public + ~15 matching private `performXxx` twins); a generic 'external signer provider' abstraction over the signer type would remove most of the bulk without changing the job.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: written by performGenerateKey and zeroized by zeroizeSensitiveMaterial(), but its content is never read anywhere in Sources or Tests — it round-trips generation-to-zeroization without ever being consumed.

smells.detail: Re-implements a parallel generateKey/exportSecretKey/importSecretKey/metadata/encodeQrUrl/decodeQrUrl surface that independently wraps the same underlying engine.* calls as PGPKeyOperationAdapter/PGPContactImportAdapter/PGPKeyMetadataAdapter — a deliberate isolation boundary (self-test must not touch Keychain/ProtectedData) but means a signature change on the shared engine calls must be mirrored in two independent adapters.

crossCutting: The six ExternalXxx*ProviderBridge files (P256 sign, P256 ECDH, ML-DSA-65 sign, ML-DSA-87 sign, ML-KEM-768 decap, ML-KEM-1024 decap) each hand-roll a private callbackCategory(for:) that repeats the same ~14-arm PGPKeyOperationFailureCategory switch; the ML-DSA-65/87 pair and the ML-KEM-768/1024 pair are near-byte-identical modulo type names. A single generic bridge parameterized over the digest/request/response type and the target category enum would collapse six files into one.

PGPErrorMapper's four externalXxxCategory functions repeat the same case list four times, and its five mapExternalXxx/mapRecipientMatching wrappers repeat the same 3-line cast-and-delegate shape five times — the same duplication pattern as the provider bridges, one level up the error-boundary.

PGPCertificateOperationAdapter, PGPKeyOperationAdapter, and (most heavily) PGPMessageOperationAdapter each carry a plain / WithExternalP256Signer(/KeyAgreement) / WithExternalCompositeSigner(/KeyAgreement) / WithExternalCompositeHighSigner(/KeyAgreement) quadruplet for every crypto operation, each with a matching private performXxx twin — this nine-family-driven duplication is the dominant reason PGPMessageOperationAdapter alone is 1,506 lines. Because each 'WithExternalYyySigner' variant calls a distinct generated engine.xxxWithExternalYyy... UniFFI method, full unification would require a corresponding change on the Rust/UniFFI signature side too, not a Swift-only refactor.

The postQuantum/postQuantumHigh tier-switch duplication recurs in both PGPSecureEnclaveCompositeBindingInspector (performInspect/performInspectHigh) and PGPSecureEnclaveCompositeGenerationAdapter (performGenerateCompositeCertificate/performGenerateCompositeHighCertificate) — smaller in scope than the message/cert adapters but the same shape.

Generation-time fingerprint echoes are dead weight in production: both PGPSecureEnclaveCustodyGeneratedMaterial and PGPSecureEnclaveCompositeGeneratedMaterial return signingKeyFingerprint/keyAgreementSubkeyFingerprint fields that no production caller reads — the routing layer re-derives the same fingerprints later from the persisted public certificate via the corresponding PublicBindingInspector/BindingInspector instead. Only Tests/DeviceSecurityTests reads them, which counts as zero production reference per this task's rule.
maps.notes: Methodology: for every public/internal declaration I grepped call sites across the whole repo, then manually opened the consuming file to confirm real field/case usage rather than trusting bare identifier matches — this caught several false positives where a DTO's type name never appears at the call site (Swift type inference, e.g. `let mergedResult = try adapter.mergePublicCertificateUpdate(...)`) even though the value is fully consumed. After that correction, this chunk turned out to be a densely-wired, actively-used adapter layer: of ~346 scanned declarations across 19 files, only 9 properties across 3 files had zero production references (2 in PGPSecureEnclaveCompositeBindingInspector, 2 in PGPSecureEnclaveCompositeGenerationAdapter, 2 in PGPSecureEnclaveCustodyGenerationAdapter, 3 in PGPSelfTestOperationAdapter) — all are DTO fields that are populated by the adapter but never read by their sole caller, not unused methods/types. No SwiftUI `body` crypto/Keychain logic was found anywhere in this chunk (pure service/adapter layer, consistent with the project's 'screens stay thin' rule), and every public throwing entry point routes its catch through PGPErrorMapper (or a PGPErrorMapper.mapExternalXxx variant) before the error can reach Models/ScreenModels — I found no PgpError/PgpMobile type leaking past this chunk's file boundaries. The single largest structural theme is the nine-key-family combinatorial duplication (plain / external-P256 / external-composite / external-composite-High variants of the same operation), concentrated most heavily in PGPMessageOperationAdapter.swift (1,506 lines) and, to a lesser extent, PGPCertificateOperationAdapter.swift, PGPKeyOperationAdapter.swift, and the six ExternalXxxProviderBridge files.

crossCutting: Nine-key-family mapping is hand-duplicated across three separate exhaustive switches in two files: PGPKeyConfiguration.Identity.configuration (Identity -> PGPKeyConfiguration), PGPKeyConfiguration.Identity.equivalentSoftwareProfile (Identity -> PGPKeyProfile?), and PGPKeyProfile.openPGPConfiguration (PGPKeyProfile -> PGPKeyConfiguration) — plus the 9-entry static-let table in PGPKeyConfiguration itself. Adding/renaming a family means touching all of these in lockstep; worth collapsing to a single family->configuration table the other views derive from.

The two have already drifted: the certificate variant's shortKeyId field is dead, the message variant's is used.

Fingerprint-formatting has one real implementation (IdentityPresentation.formattedFingerprint(_:)) but three redundant wrapper declarations funneling to it, two of which are entirely dead: PGPKeyIdentity.formattedFingerprint (test-only), PGPKeyIdentity.formatFingerprint(_:) (dead everywhere), and SignatureVerification.SignerIdentity.formattedFingerprint (dead everywhere). maps.notes: Scope: all 36 top-level .swift files directly under Sources/Models/ (no subdirectories). Every file was read in full and every top-level/nested declaration (types, stored/computed properties, funcs, enum cases, static lets/funcs) was grepped repo-wide (Sources + Tests, excluding Sources/PgpMobile and bindings/ generated FFI code) to classify production vs. test-only vs. zero references. Total declarations enumerated across the chunk: 351; zero-production-reference declarations found: 13 (listed per-file above), none of which have a plausible dynamic-dispatch reach (no #selector/KVC/Codable-only paths among them — the two dynamic-usage caveats noted are 'test-only', not 'reachable dynamically'). The strongest finding is the AppConfiguration vestigial-session-state cluster (4 dead members) and the exact-duplicate ExportableFile.swift (entire file superseded by FileExportController.swift's ExportPayload). All nine key-family vocabularies (PGPKeyConfiguration/PGPKeyProfile/PGPPrivateKeyCustodyKind/PGPPrivateOperationKind/PGPKeyOperationFailureCategory) were fully live with every case referenced. No SwiftUI-body crypto/Keychain smells were found (these are pure Models files with no view bodies); the structural smells are duplication-shaped (parallel family switches, twin signer-identity types, redundant fingerprint-formatting wrappers) rather than layering violations or boundary leaks — CypherAirError.swift in particular is clean about the PgpError-normalization boundary being owned elsewhere, per its own doc comment.

smells.detail: encrypt/encrypt_with_external_p256_signer/encrypt_with_external_composite_signer/encrypt_with_external_composite_high_signer/encrypt_binary repeat identical Message/Armorer/Encryptor scaffolding, differing only in the setup_*_signer call and finalize path.

files.role: Two-phase decrypt protocol (unauthenticated recipient/quantum-safety parsing) plus authenticated decrypt_detailed, the shared DecryptHelper/FixedSessionKeyDecryptHelper VerificationHelper+DecryptionHelper impls, and the hybrid classify_decrypt_error/is_expired_error error taxonomy used crate-wide.
files.cohesion: Genuinely one job (decrypt + its error classification) but classify_decrypt_error has grown a second responsibility: downcasting into external_decryptor's and external_composite_decryptor's typed error enums, which pulls two leaf modules' error types up into this lower module.

smells.detail: classify_decrypt_error (line ~398) must know about ExternalP256DecryptorError and ExternalCompositeDecryptorError from two sibling leaf modules to preserve typed failures; this couples the 'core' decrypt module upward to every external-decryptor family instead of those modules pushing a PgpError themselves.

The 'find_map over error.chain(), downcast to known external error enum, map variant-by-variant' shape in classify_decrypt_error is structurally identical to map_external_signing_error in external_signer/error.rs (signing side) — same pattern implemented twice.

files.role: Constant-memory streaming analogs of encrypt/decrypt/sign/verify for file I/O, with progress reporting, cancellation, zeroizing copy, and success-only (.tmp+rename) output guarantees.
files.cohesion: Bundles four distinct crypto operations (file encrypt, file decrypt, detached file sign, detached file verify) that are already split into separate files at the in-memory layer (encrypt.rs/decrypt.rs/sign.rs/verify.rs); a parallel split (e.g. streaming/{encrypt,decrypt,sign_verify,support}.rs) would restore that symmetry and shrink this file well under 1000 lines.

smells.detail: ProgressReader and DetachedVerifyProgressReader are near-identical Read wrappers (same fields, same on_progress/cancellation logic) differing only in which error path retries on Interrupted; could be a single generic type.

encrypt_file / encrypt_file_with_external_p256_signer / encrypt_file_with_external_composite_signer / encrypt_file_with_external_composite_high_signer repeat the same Encryptor/setup/write_streaming_encrypted_file scaffolding, mirroring the same pattern already flagged in encrypt.rs.

files.role: Cleartext-signing entry points (software + external P-256/composite/composite·High signers) and the shared select_external_signing_key fingerprint-selection helper.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: cfg(test)-gated constructor; only referenced from pgp-mobile/src/external_composite_signer/tests.rs. Zero production references by design, mirrors the same pattern in external_signer/error.rs.

files.role: Adapts Swift-implemented ExternalMlDsa65SigningProvider/ExternalMlDsa87SigningProvider callbacks into the CompositeSigner closure shape for both tiers.

crossCutting: The *_with_external_p256_signer / *_with_external_composite_signer / *_with_external_composite_high_signer trio is copy-pasted at every call site across the nine key families: encrypt.rs (5 funcs), streaming.rs (4 encrypt-file + 4 sign-detached-file funcs), cert_signature.rs (4 certification-generation funcs), and sign.rs (3 cleartext-sign funcs) all repeat the same parse/validate/select-key/build-external-signer/pipeline scaffolding, varying only which setup_external_*_signer or composite/high signer constructor is called. A shared higher-order 'signer setup' parameter or trait would collapse most of this into one parameterized implementation per site.

The RFC 9980 65-tier vs 87/·High-tier split is handled two different ways in the same subsystem: external_composite_signer/core.rs and external_composite_decryptor/core.rs correctly factor it through a generic CompositeSignerTier/CompositeKemTier trait, but composite_classical.rs (the lower-level curve ops) and the outer external_composite_decryptor.rs (helper structs + entry points) instead hand-duplicate the whole tier's code. Extending the existing trait pattern downward (composite_classical) and outward (external_composite_decryptor.rs's helper/entry-point layer) would remove the largest blocks of copy-paste found in this chunk.

Case-insensitive fingerprint-matching / signing-key-selection logic is independently reimplemented at least six times: sign.rs::select_external_signing_key, cert_signature.rs::select_external_certification_primary_signing_key, external_decryptor.rs::select_external_p256_key_agreement_key (+ its private fingerprint_matches), and external_composite_decryptor.rs::select_external_composite_key_agreement_key / select_external_composite_high_key_agreement_key (+ its own duplicated fingerprint_matches). A single crate-level 'find key by fingerprint among capability-filtered candidates' helper would remove this repetition.

The 'downcast error.chain() into each known external-provider error enum, remap variant-by-variant to PgpError' pattern appears twice at the same conceptual boundary: decrypt.rs::classify_decrypt_error (decrypt-side: ExternalP256DecryptorError, ExternalCompositeDecryptorError) and external_signer/error.rs::map_external_signing_error (sign-side: ExternalP256SignerError, ExternalCompositeSignerError). Both could share a small generic 'try downcast to each of these types' combinator.
maps.notes:
Scope note on exportedFfiUnused: the only #[uniffi::export] attribute inside this chunk's 21 files is `#[uniffi::export(with_foreign)] pub trait StreamingProgressReporter` in streaming.rs, which Swift implements (confirmed call sites in Sources/PgpMobile/pgp_mobile.swift and Sources/Services/FFI/PGPMessageOperationAdapter.swift) — not a candidate for 'unused export'. The actual FFI-exported surface for this subsystem is the single `#[uniffi::export] impl PgpEngine` block in pgp-mobile/src/lib.rs (out of the requested file list). Every `pub fn` in the 13 requested files was cross-checked against lib.rs and has a corresponding PgpEngine method that calls it, so none of this chunk's production entry points are FFI-dead; lib.rs itself would need its own pass to check for PgpEngine methods with no Swift call site.

Dead-code count: of ~200 top-level declarations enumerated across 21 files (13 requested + 8 submodule files under the four `+dir` entries), only 5 have zero production references, and all 5 are either (a) cfg(test)-gated test helpers that never compile into production (sign.rs::sign_detached_with_signer, and the two `external_operation_failed()` constructors in external_signer/error.rs and external_composite_signer/error.rs), or (b) redundant pub getter methods over an already-public uniffi::Record field, exercised only by pgp-mobile/src/external_decryptor/tests.rs (ExternalP256KeyAgreementRequest::recipient_public_key/ephemeral_public_key in external_decryptor/core.rs). No product-facing dead branches, no unreachable PgpError variants, and no crypto/Keychain-in-SwiftUI-body smells (this chunk is pure Rust FFI, no SwiftUI at all). The most valuable follow-up is the crossCutting duplication list above, especially generalizing external_composite_decryptor.rs's outer helper/entry-point layer over the CompositeKemTier trait it already has one layer down, and composite_classical.rs's 25519-vs-448 duplication, both of which are large, mechanical, and low-risk to unify.

maps.module: pgp-mobile core: lib.rs / error.rs / armor.rs / qr_url.rs / keys.rs + keys/** / password.rs
maps.notes:
Methodology: for every file I enumerated top-level declarations (types, traits, fns, consts) and grepped the whole repo (pgp-mobile/src, pgp-mobile/tests, Sources/**/*.swift, Tests/**/*.swift, bindings/pgp_mobile.swift) for each symbol. For lib.rs's 82 PgpEngine methods I converted each Rust snake_case name to the UniFFI-generated Swift camelCase method and grepped Sources/ (excluding the generated Sources/PgpMobile/pgp_mobile.swift binding) for ".methodName(" call sites; Tests/ call sites were tracked separately and do NOT count as production per the task's rule. For large "shape" enums shared across the nine key families (ExternalP256/CompositeSigningFailureCategory, ExternalP256/CompositeKeyAgreementFailureCategory - 12/13/14/15 cases respectively) I spot-checked several cases in Sources/Services/FFI/*Bridge.swift and CypherAirError+Presentation.swift and found them exhaustively and genuinely consumed (not decorative), so I did not explode all ~54 variants into zeroRefDeclarations; PgpError (error.rs) and ArmorKind (armor.rs) were checked at full case granularity because sampling those first turned up real findings. get_key_version and parse_revocation_cert are each called only from Tests/ (FFIIntegrationTests / ServiceTests), never from Sources/ - per the task's rule these count as zero production references even though the Rust call chain from lib.rs into keys/profile.rs and keys/revocation.rs is intact.

Headline findings: (1) two whole FFI methods (get_key_version, parse_revocation_cert) are wired end-to-end but never called by the shipping app, only by tests; (2) four PgpError variants (BadSignature, UnknownSigner, KeyExpired, Argon2idMemoryExceeded) are declared in the FFI error enum and dutifully mapped by Swift's PGPErrorMapper.swift, but are never constructed anywhere in pgp-mobile - the equivalent CypherAirError cases the app actually throws come from independent native-Swift call sites (SelfTestService.swift, Argon2idMemoryGuard.swift), so those Rust variants and their Swift mapping arms are decorative; (3) ArmorKind's 4 non-Signature cases are effectively write-only: encode_armor is only ever invoked from Swift with kind: .signature, and both production callers of decode_armor (lib.rs dearmor, password.rs normalize_message_bytes) discard the returned kind via `_kind`; (4) the "one function per device-bound tier" (P-256 / ML-DSA-65+Ed25519 / ML-DSA-87+Ed448) pattern is hand-triplicated across expiry.rs, revocation.rs, and password.rs rather than factored through a shared generic/macro - this is the clearest nine-family duplication opportunity in this chunk. Total across 19 files: 322 top-level declarations enumerated, 10 with zero production references (2 in lib.rs, 4 in error.rs, 4 in armor.rs); every keys/* submodule's own private helpers were confirmed reachable.
crossCutting: Three-tier external-signer triplication: expiry.rs's modify_expiry_with_external_{p256,composite,composite_high}_signer and revocation.rs's generate_subkey_revocation_with_external_{p256,composite,composite_high}_signer / generate_user_id_revocation_by_selector_with_external_{p256,composite,composite_high}_signer, plus password.rs's encrypt_impl/encrypt_external_impl/encrypt_external_composite_impl/encrypt_external_composite_high_impl, are each 3-4 near-identical bodies differing only in which *_signer_for_provider constructor is called and which HashAlgorithm is forced (SHA256 for P-256, SHA512 for both composite tiers). A shared generic helper (or a small macro keyed on a 'signer tier' trait) could collapse roughly a dozen near-duplicate functions in this chunk alone.

Parallel fixed-length public-key validators: composite_custody_generation/mod.rs's validate_mldsa65_public_key/validate_mldsa87_public_key and validate_mlkem768_public_key/validate_mlkem1024_public_key are each a pair of identical-shape functions differing only in the expected length constant and error text (the KEM pair also shares mlkem_packed_vector_is_canonical already) - collapsible to one generic `fn validate_fixed_length_public_key(bytes, expected_len, label)` per family.

CONTACT_IMPORT_PUBLIC_ONLY_REASON is a Rust const in keys.rs consumed by public_certificates.rs, but Swift's PGPContactImportAdapter.swift redeclares the identical string as its own literal (publicOnlyReasonToken) instead of deriving it from the FFI value - the stable-reason contract is duplicated by hand across the boundary and can drift silently.

Stale 'Profile A' / 'Profile B' terminology persists in doc comments in keys.rs (KeyProfile), lib.rs (export_secret_key), and secret_transfer.rs even though the nine-family model now uses descriptive names (Universal/Legacy, Advanced/Modern·High, Modern, PostQuantum, PostQuantumHigh) - consistent with the already-tracked repo-wide Profile A/B naming retirement (not a new finding, just visible again in this chunk).
exportedFfiUnused: get_key_version — pgp-mobile/src/lib.rs:448 (PgpEngine.getKeyVersion) delegating to pgp-mobile/src/keys/profile.rs:4 (keys::get_key_version); zero production Swift call sites — only called from Tests/FFIIntegrationTests+RoundTrip.swift

parse_revocation_cert — pgp-mobile/src/lib.rs:1036 (PgpEngine.parseRevocationCert) delegating to pgp-mobile/src/keys/revocation.rs:492 (keys::parse_revocation_cert); zero production Swift call sites — only called from Tests/FFIIntegrationTests+ErrorMapping.swift, Tests/FFIIntegrationTests+Revocation.swift, and 3 files under Tests/ServiceTests

files.role: FFI facade: the single #[uniffi::export]'d PgpEngine object exposing every crypto/key/armor/streaming/QR operation as a thin pass-through method into the domain modules.
files.cohesion: One job by design (the UniFFI export surface must live on one object's methods), but it spans 11 largely independent capability domains (keygen, SE custody, cert info, cert merge, expiry, encrypt, decrypt, sign, verify, cert-signature, export/import, revocation, armor, streaming, QR) inline in one file. Rust allows multiple `impl PgpEngine` blocks each carrying its own #[uniffi::export]; splitting into per-domain files (ffi_encrypt.rs, ffi_decrypt.rs, ffi_revocation.rs, ...) would cut navigation/diff cost without behavior change.

smells.detail: ~82 FFI methods across 11 unrelated capability domains live in one 1427-line impl block; each method itself is a 1-10 line delegation, so the smell is breadth/navigability, not logic density.
About 30 of the 82 methods are members of a P-256 / composite / composite-High triad that only differs in which domain-module function and signer type is threaded through (see crossCutting).
get_key_version and parse_revocation_cert are fully wired FFI methods with zero production Swift callers (test-only) — see exportedFfiUnused.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Called only from Tests/FFIIntegrationTests+RoundTrip.swift (test-only); a plain UniFFI method call, no dynamic dispatch, so this is a genuine production-dead export.
Called only from Tests/FFIIntegrationTests+ErrorMapping.swift, Tests/FFIIntegrationTests+Revocation.swift, and 3 files under Tests/ServiceTests (test-only); no dynamic dispatch involved.

files.role: Defines PgpError, the single FFI-crossing error enum that every Swift CypherAirError case is derived from (via generated PgpError bindings).

smells.detail: 4 of 24 PgpError variants (BadSignature, UnknownSigner, KeyExpired, Argon2idMemoryExceeded) are never constructed anywhere in pgp-mobile/src or pgp-mobile/tests. Swift's PGPErrorMapper.swift still exhaustively maps them (compiler-required exhaustive switch), and Swift throws the identically-named CypherAirError cases from unrelated native call sites (SelfTestService.swift, Argon2idMemoryGuard.swift) — not from these Rust variants. The FFI contract implies these four errors can flow from Rust; in practice they never do.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Mirrored 1:1 in generated Swift bindings and matched by PGPErrorMapper.swift's exhaustive switch (case .BadSignature -> .badSignature); Swift's own CypherAirError.badSignature is thrown independently from Sources/Services/SelfTestService.swift, not derived from this Rust variant, which is never constructed.

Same pattern: mirrored + exhaustively matched in PGPErrorMapper.swift; never constructed by any Rust code path (do not confuse with the unrelated DetailedSignatureStatus::UnknownSigner in signature_details.rs, which is a different, actively-used enum).
Same pattern: mirrored + exhaustively matched in PGPErrorMapper.swift; never constructed by any Rust code path.

Mirrored + exhaustively matched in PGPErrorMapper.swift; Swift's Argon2idMemoryGuard.swift throws the same-named CypherAirError case as a pre-flight Swift-side check before ever calling into Rust, so the Rust variant itself is never constructed.

files.role: ASCII-armor encode/decode helpers plus the ArmorKind FFI enum and the armor_writer helper reused by keys/secret_transfer.rs.

smells.detail: ArmorKind has 5 cases, but PgpEngine.armor(kind:) is only ever invoked from Swift with kind: .signature (Sources/Services/FFI/PGPCertificateOperationAdapter.swift), and both production callers of decode_armor (lib.rs's dearmor, password.rs's normalize_message_bytes) discard the returned kind via `_kind`. The other 4 cases are exercised only inside armor.rs's own match arms, never consumed by any caller.

zeroRefDeclarations.dynamicUsageCaveat: Constructed/matched only within armor.rs's own encode_armor/decode_armor bodies; no Swift call site passes kind: .publicKey, and every production decode_armor caller discards the returned kind.
Same as PublicKey.
Same as PublicKey.
encode_armor explicitly rejects Unknown as input (dead on the input side by construction); as decode_armor's fallback output it is always discarded by production callers, same as PublicKey.

files.role: Encodes/decodes the cypherair://import/v1/ QR URL scheme, rejecting secret-key payloads and oversized public keys.

Root of the keys module: declares every FFI record/enum/trait for key generation, SE custody, composite custody, key info, and certificate-selector types, plus shared cert-introspection helpers used by its submodules.
files.cohesion: Two different jobs cohabit one file: (a) a ~40-record/enum FFI type catalog for the 4 device-bound families' request/response payloads and failure categories, and (b) a set of shared certificate-introspection helpers (select_display_user_id, discover_subkey/discover_user_id, current_*_fingerprints, find_user_id_by_selector) that key_info.rs, selector_discovery.rs, and revocation.rs all depend on via `super::`. Splitting into keys/types.rs (FFI records/enums) + keeping keys.rs as the thin re-export root + shared helpers would separate the two concerns cleanly.

smells.detail: CONTACT_IMPORT_PUBLIC_ONLY_REASON is a Rust const consumed by public_certificates.rs, but Swift's PGPContactImportAdapter.swift redeclares the identical string as an independent literal (publicOnlyReasonToken) rather than deriving it from the FFI value.

files.role: Generates the 5 portable (software) key families via Sequoia CertBuilder profile/cipher-suite selection (Legacy, Modern, Modern·High/Advanced, Post-Quantum, Post-Quantum·High).

Re-signs a certificate's expiration (primary key, all User IDs, and non-revoked subkeys), for both local-secret-key and external-signer (Secure Enclave custody) callers across all 3 device-bound tiers.
files.cohesion: Single cohesive job (expiry mutation), but the 3 external-signer entry points hand-triplicate the same ~40-line control flow (verify signer is primary, reject subkey-signer-requiring cases, select signing key, build tier-specific external signer, force a hash algorithm) — see crossCutting.

smells.detail: modify_expiry_with_external_p256_signer / _composite_signer / _composite_high_signer differ only in which *_signer_for_provider constructor is called and whether HashAlgorithm is forced to SHA256 or SHA512.

files.role: Generates key/subkey/User-ID revocation signatures for local-secret-key and external-signer callers, and cryptographically verifies a revocation certificate against its target key.
files.cohesion: Single cohesive job (revocation generation/verification), but generate_subkey_revocation_with_external_* and generate_user_id_revocation_by_selector_with_external_* are each triplicated per device-bound tier — see crossCutting. Note: parse_revocation_cert (this file's export) is fully wired through lib.rs but has zero production Swift caller — see exportedFfiUnused; not double-counted as zero-ref in declStats below because the Rust call chain (lib.rs -> keys::parse_revocation_cert) is intact.

smells.detail: generate_subkey_revocation_with_external_{p256,composite,composite_high}_signer and generate_user_id_revocation_by_selector_with_external_{p256,composite,composite_high}_signer are each 3 near-identical ~40-line functions differing only in the signer constructor and forced HashAlgorithm.

files.role: Parses S2K (passphrase-derivation) parameters from a key so Swift can pre-check Argon2id memory requirements before import.
Classifies a certificate's KeyProfile from its primary-key algorithm/version, and reports the raw OpenPGP key version.
Parses a certificate into the display-facing KeyInfo summary (fingerprint, user ID, expiry, revocation status, algorithms).
Validates contact-import data as a public-only certificate and merges same-fingerprint certificate update material.
Discovers subkey/User-ID selector metadata from binary certificate bytes for the certificate-selector UI (binary-only contract).
Exports/imports passphrase-protected secret keys, choosing Iterated+Salted vs. Argon2id S2K by profile.

smells.detail: The `match profile { Universal => encrypt_secret, _ => encrypt_key_argon2id }` dispatch is repeated verbatim once for the primary key and once for the subkey loop in export_secret_key; low severity.

files.role: Assembles and inspects the two split-custody PQ composite certificate tiers: Device-Bound Post-Quantum (ML-DSA-65+Ed25519 / ML-KEM-768+X25519) and Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High (ML-DSA-87+Ed448 / ML-KEM-1024+X448).
files.cohesion: Single cohesive job (owns exactly the 2 PQ composite families) and already well factored via shared generics (assemble_composite_public_certificate, inspect_composite_bindings) — size is proportionate to owning both tiers' assembly + inspection + validation, not scope creep.

smells.detail: validate_mldsa65_public_key/validate_mldsa87_public_key and validate_mlkem768_public_key/validate_mlkem1024_public_key are parallel pairs differing only in the expected-length constant and error text (the KEM pair shares mlkem_packed_vector_is_canonical already) — collapsible to one generic helper per family.

files.role: Unit tests for the ML-KEM/ML-DSA public-key shape and canonical-coefficient validators.
Assembles and inspects the two Secure Enclave P-256 custody certificate families: Device-Bound Legacy (v4) and Device-Bound Modern (v6).
End-to-end generation/inspection/failure-path tests for Secure Enclave P-256 custody certificates, using an in-process oracle signer plus several deliberately-failing signer doubles.
Password-based (SKESK) encrypt/decrypt, including all external-signer signing combinations, and password-candidate SKESK parsing/validation for decryption.
files.cohesion: Single cohesive job (password-based messages), but the four encrypt_*_impl functions duplicate the same ~25-line Armorer/Encryptor scaffold end to end.

smells.detail: encrypt_impl / encrypt_external_impl / encrypt_external_composite_impl / encrypt_external_composite_high_impl each rebuild the identical sink/Armorer/Encryptor-with-passwords/AEAD-branch scaffold and differ only in which setup_*_signer + write_and_finalize* pair is invoked at the end; the same pattern (out of scope) recurs in encrypt.rs and streaming.rs.

leaks.note: Doc-comment prose only ("Raw values intentionally match the historical generated `KeyProfile` Codable representation..."). The generated enum is not imported, referenced as a type, or used in any signature here — PGPKeyProfile is a fully independent app-vocabulary enum. Not a real shape-leak, just a comment mentioning the FFI type by name for context.

Doc-comment prose only ("Generated UniFFI `PgpError` values are intentionally normalized by `PGPErrorMapper` in `Sources/Services/FFI`, not here."). This comment explicitly documents that the normalization boundary lives elsewhere; the generated type itself is never used as a parameter/return/case type in this file. Not a shape-leak.

typealias MessageQuantumSafetyAction = @MainActor (Data) throws -> MessageQuantumSafety - the ScreenModel's own action-closure type is typed directly in the raw generated enum, not an app-vocabulary type.

var resultQuantumSafety: MessageQuantumSafety? - internal (non-private) stored property on the ScreenModel holds the raw generated enum directly; only shielded from the View by two computed booleans (showsQuantumSafeBadge / showsMixedQuantumSafetyCaption at lines 197/203) that switch on its raw cases (.fullyPostQuantum, .mixed).

private func classifyEncryptedFileQuantumSafety(at url: URL) -> MessageQuantumSafety? - return type is the raw generated enum. The default implementation of messageQuantumSafetyAction (lines 123-125) instantiates PgpEngine() (the raw UniFFI-generated class) directly inline inside the ScreenModel and calls .messageQuantumSafety(ciphertext:) on it, entirely bypassing Sources/Services/FFI/ (no adapter exists anywhere in Sources/Services or Sources/Models for this call - grep for messageQuantumSafety/MessageQuantumSafety outside PgpMobile/App returns nothing). Every other PgpEngine call site in the app goes through an FFI/ adapter (PGPKeyOperationAdapter, PGPMessageOperationAdapter, etc.) constructed once in the AppContainer.swift composition root (lines 721, 1065) and TutorialSandboxContainer.swift (line 52) - this is the sole call path that skips that pattern. Introduced by commit d9a9100 'feat: derive the quantum-safety claim from the produced artifact' (PQC Phase 2 campaign, #571/#572). It only reaches the View (EncryptResultSections.swift:101) through the two normalized booleans, so the SwiftUI body itself never touches the raw enum directly, but the project rule as stated names ScreenModel as a high-level layer, and this is a genuine, unambiguous violation of it - not a legitimate mapping point, since no Services/FFI adapter or Models/ wrapper exists anywhere in the codebase for this generated type.

Protocol requirement `SecureEnclaveCompositeDecapsulating.decapsulateMlKem768(request:using:)` declares the raw UniFFI record type as its parameter instead of an app/Security-level shape. The sibling protocol in the same file, `SecureEnclaveCompositeSigning`, takes a plain `Data` digest (no generated type at all) and the whole family's return types are also plain `Data`, so this is not an unavoidable boundary constraint — it looks like a real shape-leak, not a deliberate mapping point.

Same protocol (`SecureEnclaveCompositeDecapsulating.decapsulateMlKem1024`) also takes the raw generated request record directly. Same reasoning as the ML-KEM-768 sibling: inconsistent with the normalized `Data`-only signing half of this protocol pair.

Concrete implementation `SystemSecureEnclaveCompositeOperations.decapsulateMlKem768`. The body reads the generated struct's raw fields directly at lines 92 (`request.recipientMlkemPublicKey`), 95 and 104 (`request.mlkemCiphertext`) — i.e. business logic in Security/ branches on generated-type field names rather than an app vocabulary shape. This is called from `Sources/Services/FFI/PGPExternalMlKem768DecapsulationProviderBridge.swift`, which receives the same generated type from the actual UniFFI callback and forwards it unchanged instead of normalizing it at the FFI boundary as the project rule requires.

Concrete implementation `decapsulateMlKem1024`, mirrors the 768 case: fields read directly at lines 157 (`request.recipientMlkemPublicKey`) and 160/169 (`request.mlkemCiphertext`). Forwarded unnormalized from `Sources/Services/FFI/PGPExternalMlKem1024DecapsulationProviderBridge.swift`.

Protocol requirement `SecureEnclaveCustodyKeyAgreement.deriveSharedSecret(request:using:)` takes the raw generated record type as a parameter. Note the return type here (`SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret`) IS an app-owned type, not the generated `P256RawSharedSecret` — so the codebase already normalizes on the way out but not on the way in, reinforcing that the input leak is an inconsistency rather than an intentional design choice.

Concrete implementation `SystemSecureEnclaveCustodyKeyAgreement.deriveSharedSecret`. Body reads generated fields directly: `request.recipientPublicKey` (line 52) and `request.ephemeralPublicKey` (lines 56 and 65, the latter passed straight into `SecKeyCreateWithData` via `importP256PublicKey`). Forwarded unnormalized from `Sources/Services/FFI/PGPExternalP256KeyAgreementProviderBridge.swift`, which receives it from the real `ExternalP256KeyAgreementProvider` UniFFI callback and passes it straight through instead of mapping to an app/Security-owned request shape.

files.reason: Verifies temp-file lifecycle (creation, complete file-protection attribute, cleanup on finish()) and that file-export mode does not delete the caller's source file.
Exercises the token-based single-consumption gate (begin/consumeIfCurrent/invalidate) that suppresses stale file-importer completions.
Verifies injected-action override precedence and correct progress-reporter threading (including a cancelled reporter) through FileOperationAction's closure composition.
Not a test file - a bundle-fixture loading helper with no test methods; used by GnuPGInteropTests and the revocation-selector duplicate-occurrence fixture test.

Exercises real Rust/UniFFI/Swift interop against pre-generated GnuPG fixtures: parse, decrypt, verify, tamper-detection, round-trip. Ongoing cross-tool compatibility requirement, not deprecated/obsolete-format testing.

Covers file-content classification, stale file-importer suppression after content clear, successful import clearing sensitive state, suppressing late import completion after clear, and mapped error surfacing.
Verifies raw-data/text-snapshot invalidation semantics and re-import replacement - genuine state-machine behavior a refactor could break.

Exercises real contact-import flows via generated keys and the QR/import URL encoder: invalid URL errors, confirmation-then-import persists a contact, candidate matching, already-pending rejection, tutorial-presentation blocking.

Exercises the Keychain-backed bundle store's crash-consistency primitives (save/load round-trip, write-failure rollback, receipt rollback, duplicate rejection, pending-to-permanent promotion including failure paths, delete) against a mock Keychain with injectable failures.

Covers export-interception routing, configuration-gated copy/save, revocation export success/failure/late-completion suppression, default/delete dispatch, modify-expiry routing, and device-bound degraded-availability ordinal mapping across several disjuncts, with an explicit #512 regression comment.

Exercises locked-family/prefill behavior, family-detail presentation independent of selection, available-families/custodies gating on resolver policy AND wired service, device-bound commitment confirmation flow, content-clear suppression of late generation results, and mapped error surfacing.
Exercises modify-expiry crash recovery across all reachable bundle states including write-failure paths and retryable/unrecoverable outcomes against a real journal and mock Keychain.

Exercises export/import round-trips for both profiles, backed-up-flag timing, metadata-update-failure divergence, duplicate-fingerprint rejection without SE generation, and revocation-cert re-validation after import.
Verifies Keychain item counts, returned identity fields, default-key assignment, and Secure Enclave generate/wrap call counts for both profiles during real key generation.

Covers delete-key removal/reassignment, partial-delete-failure sync, interrupted-modify-expiry/rewrap recovery clearing, private-key unwrap correctness including an explicit off-main-actor regression guard, and default-key persistence across a simulated cold restart.
Verifies identity persistence through injected metadata persistence, empty-persistence load, and load-failure entering recovery-needed state.

Extensive checkpoint-gated race-condition coverage for generate/import provisioning vs concurrent relock: cancellation at multiple points, rollback of only new state while preserving unrelated existing keys, metadata-discard-failure preserving the new bundle. Exactly the regression class a concurrency refactor could reintroduce.

Pins fail-closed security invariants for selective revocation: selector discovery never unwraps/rewrites metadata, mismatches and cross-certificate/tampered selectors rejected before any SE unwrap, missing revocation artifacts fail closed.

Covers hidden SE custody generation rollback racing relock, recovery-report refresh on load/delete/backup-confirm, custody-key deletion edge cases, and fail-closed behavior when the routing service isn't wired.
Exercises fingerprint validation, High-Security-mode export failing without biometrics, wrong-passphrase import rejection, and Argon2id low-memory rejection/acceptance split by profile.

Exercises the protected key-metadata domain's crash/recovery semantics: mismatched configuration/custody, corrupt/missing current generation not falling back to stale data, unsupported schema failing closed, pending-create recovery, mutation persistence across relock/reopen. Active infrastructure, not deprecated migration code.

Despite the name, tests the current interrupted-mode-switch/rewrap bundle-recovery coordinator used by the standard/high-security mode switch - active crash-consistency logic, not obsolete migration code.
Verifies the single-pre-authentication-context design for modify-expiry, including a documented #495 field-failure regression guard for honest error surfacing when the catalog relocks mid-action.
Verifies key generation/import SE-wrap windows run inside the operation-prompt session versus outside it, guarding against regressions that could lock mid-action or fail to relock when they should.
Not a test file - shared mocks/helpers for the KeyManagementService test suite, no test methods of its own.

Injected generate/render actions exercise real state transitions (unavailability, qrCGImage) for success, generation-nil, render-nil, throw, and oversized-key (keyTooLargeForQr) paths. A regression that swaps the oversized-key full-page state for a spinner/alert would be caught.

Security-critical parser test against untrusted QR input: wrong scheme/host, unsupported version, missing/invalid base64, non-PGP payload, secret-key-material rejection, 4096-char DoS length guard (both boundaries), and full generate->decode->parse round trips via the real engine. Every assertion pins real rejection/acceptance behavior.

Exercises real key-spec hex encoding, invalid-length rejection, and a live SQLCipherPreflightProbe run against an actual encrypted database (verifying cipher version/compile options and no plaintext sidecars remain).

Verifies exact evidence-log line format plus an exhaustive structural sanitization invariant across every scenario x configuration x outcome x failure-category combination (no path/PEM/base64 characters can leak), a genuine security property a formatting refactor could silently break.

Real concurrency/timing race tests: resign during in-session custody authorization must defer (never mid-action lock) while a resign after the authorization window locks immediately; also verifies back-compat without a coordinator and that pre-authentication runs inside the operation-prompt session exactly once.

Exercises the recovery-classification decision tree across available/missing-handle/ambiguous/wrong-public-key/metadata-mismatch/inventory-failure scenarios through real collaborators (handle store, inspector mocks) -- genuine regression coverage of a security-relevant recovery classifier.

Covers transactional rollback on builder/metadata failure, rollback-failure-keeps-handles fail-safe, cancellation/lockout error mapping, single-authentication-per-generation invariant, and context invalidation-count guarantees -- all real behavior with meaningful mocks.

Overwhelmingly real-guard: handle creation/rollback, access-policy attribute checks, availability classification (partial/ambiguous/wrong-role/wrong-key), inventory/cleanup accounting including malformed and non-UTF8 tags, and locate/load fail-closed paths are all genuine regression coverage. One outlier, test_storeDoesNotUseLegacySoftwareWrappingServiceNames, only asserts that tag strings do NOT contain old dead substrings (".se-key.", ".salt.", ".sealed-key.") -- a brittle-negative that re-encodes a retired naming scheme; the same file's positive assertions on the current applicationTagString format already cover the real behavior.

Verifies the cleanup-ordering invariant on a security-scoped resource acquirer: partial acquisition failure stops only the resources that were actually started (not ones that failed to start), and full success stops all of them -- a real resource-leak/double-stop regression guard.

Thorough async state-machine coverage: load-once/selection-preservation, cancellation races (disappear during load, disappear during export), stale-attempt-loses-to-newer-attempt, output-interception routing, and export failure preserving selection -- all exercised via real timing gates, not vacuous.

Runs actual end-to-end PGP self-tests through the real engine (no mocks) for both profiles, checks report content/filename, and verifies export-completion success/failure clears or preserves the pending report for retry.

Large but substantive: auth-mode-switch confirmation flows (including stale-request races), local-data-reset success/auth-failure/auth-unavailable paths, operation-prompt session timing invariants (resign deferred vs. locks immediately), and an extensive ProtectedSettingsHost authorization/reset/retry state machine verified via exact event-ordering assertions. Two lightweight copy-content checks (test_portableKeyProtectionCopy_describesPortableOnlyScope, test_noIdentitiesCopy_isPortableAccurate) guard a real UX/security-clarity distinction (device-bound vs portable custody scope) rather than an arbitrary constant, so not a prune recommendation.

Signer-sync recovery after default-key change/deletion, output-interception routing for clipboard/export, file-signing cancellation, and content-clear-during-in-flight-signing races that must suppress late plaintext/signature writes -- genuine privacy-hard-fail coverage.

Verifies multi-signer detailed verification folding (valid/expired/bad/unknown-signer combinations) against real fixtures generated by the Rust engine, plus streaming verification cancellation -- exercises genuine cross-FFI behavior.

End-to-end sign/verify round trips for both profiles, tamper detection (signature integrity), unknown-signer/contacts-locked handling, expired-key behavior, and High Security biometrics gating -- all through the real engine.
Verifies the compliance-info parser correctly decodes real stable-release and local-build fixture files (version, commit SHA, dependency list, license fields).

End-to-end streaming encrypt/decrypt/sign/verify round trips for both profiles, unique-operation-directory collision avoidance, failed-repeat-preserves-previous-output, cancellation, insufficient-disk-space, AEAD tamper detection (no plaintext on failure), no-matching-key, and file-protection-level checks -- all real security-relevant behavior.
Not a test suite -- shared factory/infrastructure code (service-stack builders, key-generation helpers) used by other test files. No XCTest methods to classify or prune.
Small but pins an actual width-threshold boundary (default 900x560 window's ~680pt detail column must stay single-column) -- a real regression a careless change to wideLayoutMinWidth would break.

Extensive coverage of the tutorial sandbox lifecycle: concurrent-open serialization, cancellation/reset/finish races, navigation-state preservation across surface switches, guidance-resolver routing across modules/modals, and output-interception policy actually blocking real clipboard/export/file effects while in tutorial mode -- all genuine, security-relevant state-machine behavior.

Precise state-invalidation rules that are easy to get subtly wrong: editing signed input clears only cleartext state (not detached), mode switches preserve per-mode results, disappear clears only imports, and content-clear suppresses a late in-flight verification result. All genuine regression coverage.

Security-critical envelope codec test: seal/open round trip, distinct-nonce-per-seal, wrong-wrapping-key rejection, tampered-ciphertext/tag rejection, cross-domain AAD binding rejection, and malformed-contract/undecodable-payload rejection on encode/decode. All real cryptographic invariants.

Exercises real AuthenticationManager against real SE/Keychain (createAccessControl, canEvaluate, evaluate+unwrap) plus crash-recovery scenarios (old+pending envelope, only-pending, no-flag, unrecoverable, retryable-failure with a MockKeychain forced to fail mid-save) that assert exact outcome enums and envelope/auth-mode state after each recovery path. A regression in the rewrap-crash-recovery state machine would break these.

Creates a real custody handle pair, runs the actual Reset-All-Local-Data cleanup path against real Secure Enclave kSecClassKey rows, and asserts the handle count goes to zero and trace log entries are sanitized. Guards the destructive reset path from silently under/over-deleting.

Full PGP workflow (keygen/encrypt/decrypt/sign/verify/armor/export/import) run under real Secure Enclave + MIE hardware for both classical profiles, plus 50x/100x repetition loops explicitly designed to catch intermittent MTE tag-mismatch crashes. Every assertion checks real plaintext/signature equality, not framework internals.

Exercises the Standard<->High-Security mode-switch feature (an active, ongoing app feature, not deprecated behavior) against real SE/Keychain and mock-forced-failure rollback scenarios (single key, 12-key stress, fail-mid-batch), asserting original keys survive rollback and no pending items leak. 'Migration' here means the live re-wrap flow, not obsolete-format handling.

Runs real crypto (encrypt/keygen/SE wrap-unwrap/Argon2id) under XCTest's measure() API. Note: no baseline is configured, so the documented latency thresholds in the doc comments are never actually asserted in code -- these only fail if the operation throws/crashes via try!, not on a latency regression. Real crash-guard value, weaker than the comments imply; not a deletion candidate.

Exercises real KeychainProtectedDataRootSecretStore with real LAContext authentication (userPresence and biometricsOnly policies), asserting fail-closed on interactionNotAllowed and correct round-trip once authenticated.

End-to-end Device-Bound Post-Quantum (and ·High) split-custody lifecycle against real SE hardware: generate composite cert through the external ML-DSA bridge, decrypt through real ML-KEM decapsulation + RFC 9980 combiner, sign/verify, plus a negative test that a wrong classical component fails closed before touching the enclave. This is the real coverage that supersedes the PqcHighProbeTests spike (see finding above).

Real SE key-agreement decrypt route for v4/v6 messages and mixed-recipient files, plus a payload-tamper test asserting hard-fail with no plaintext output on the filesystem -- directly guards the AEAD hard-fail invariant (CLAUDE.md hard constraint 3).

Real SE custody handle create/reload/inventory/delete lifecycle, private-operation success after biometric auth, fail-closed on interactionNotAllowed, wrong-public-binding and cross-role (signing handle used for key-agreement, etc.) guard tests, plus a trace-sanitization assertion. All assert against real SecKey operations and real failure categories.

Builds a public-only certificate through the real SE signing handle and asserts key version/profile/fingerprints and that the handle pair is locatable afterward -- exercises the hidden-generation adapter against real hardware.

macOS-only bidirectional interop test against a real local gpg binary: gpg imports a real SE-generated certificate, verifies an SE-signed message, and encrypts a message that the SE key-agreement bridge must decrypt. Genuine cross-implementation compatibility guard, not framework-only.

Both methods call SecureEnclave.MLDSA87/MLKEM1024 directly with no @testable import CypherAir -- they test Apple's CryptoKit/SE framework capability, not any CypherAir code. The file's own header labels it a one-time 'Spike S0' investigative probe for issue #591 ('does this device's Secure Enclave support the tier'). Per project history, #591 (nine-key-families) is complete and merged, and the real feature is now covered end-to-end through app code by DeviceSecureEnclaveCompositeCustodyTests.swift. No CypherAir regression can break this test; it only reflects Apple's own hardware/OS support.

Comprehensive real-hardware SE wrap/unwrap/reconstruct/Keychain round-trip suite, including wrong-fingerprint domain-separation failure, tampered-envelope hard-fail, full SE->FFI decrypt pipeline for both profiles, deletion->itemNotFound, and a 10x lifecycle loop checking for state leaks. All assert real cryptographic outcomes.

Shared base class (setUp/tearDown, fingerprint/keychain tracking, AuthenticationManager factory) with no test methods of its own; it underlies the genuine hardware-backed tests in this directory and its own correctness (cleanup logic) matters for their soundness.

Verifies SystemMemoryInfo returns a sane value bounded by physical memory on real hardware, and runs a full real 512 MB Argon2id import through parseS2kParams -> guard -> importSecretKey with real (not mocked) memory info.

Shared base class for custody device tests (hardware/biometric guards, real SecKey sign/verify/ECDH helpers, failure-category mapping, sanitized-output assertions). No test methods itself, but its assertSanitizedText/assertDoesNotLeak helpers directly enforce the no-fingerprint/no-locator-leak invariant used by several real tests above.

Non-test support type (evidence-line formatting for the SE custody evidence matrix in docs). No test methods; the type is sanitized-by-construction (only enums/counts, no free text) and is exercised/pinned by SecureEnclaveCustodyEvidenceLogTests.swift, which lives outside the audited directories (Tests/ServiceTests).
Base XCTestCase with setUp/tearDown (real PgpEngine instance) and fixture/temp-file/selector helpers shared by every extension file below; no test methods of its own.

Boundary-value tests for Argon2idMemoryGuard (75% threshold, exact/off-by-one boundary, Profile A no-op, unknown S2K no-op) using a real generated/exported Profile B key plus mocked memory to hit deterministic edge cases. Directly guards the memory-DoS mitigation logic.

Verifies direct-key and user-ID-binding certification signatures across the FFI boundary (valid/invalid/signer-missing/wrong-target/duplicate-occurrence/out-of-range selector), asserting exact status enums and fingerprints against both generated keys and binary fixtures.

Certificate merge/update outcomes (expiry refresh, no-op duplicate, primary-UID switch, revocation, added encryption subkey) and selector discovery (userIds/subkeys, duplicate-userid occurrence state, public-cert validation rejecting secret-bearing input) all driven through real generated keys and fixtures with concrete assertions.

10-way concurrent encrypt, and mixed concurrent encrypt+decrypt (including Profile B/AEAD) task groups against a shared PgpEngine, asserting every task succeeds -- genuine thread-safety regression guard for the FFI boundary.

Detailed verify/decrypt result shape tests (multi-signer, known+unknown signer, repeated signer, cancellation) against binary fixtures, asserting exact per-signature status/fingerprint arrays and summary index -- guards the richer signature-result API that the UI's SignatureVerification model consumes.

Maps real failure conditions (wrong key, tampered ciphertext for both profiles, garbage input, wrong passphrase, expired key, no recipients, bad signature, unknown signer) to the expected PgpError/summary-state values. Uses tolerant multi-case matches only where the underlying crypto library's behavior is legitimately non-deterministic (documented in comments), not as a weakening of the assertion.

Six of seven methods genuinely read back buffer contents and assert all-zero after zeroize() (Data, [UInt8], SensitiveData) -- real guards for the memory-hygiene hard constraint. The seventh, test_sensitiveData_deinit_zerosStorage, only checks that releasing a SensitiveData inside an autoreleasepool doesn't crash; the comment's claim that 'deinit zeroing worked' is not actually verified by any assertion.

Password/SKESK round-trip for SEIPDv1 and signed SEIPDv2, no-SKESK detection, wrong-password rejection, and targeted-tamper tests that search for a deterministic bit-flip position and assert the exact resulting integrity-failure error per format.

Two-phase decrypt (parseRecipients then decryptDetailed) and matchRecipients across single/multi-recipient and wrong-key scenarios, including garbage-data error handling, all asserting concrete fingerprints/counts against real encrypted ciphertext.

Key/subkey/user-ID revocation generation and validation (round-trip against source cert, public-only-input rejection, selector-miss/out-of-range/bytes-mismatch rejection) using both generated keys and fixtures, asserting InvalidKeyData is thrown at the right boundaries.

Binary and Unicode round-trip tests (including 1 MB payloads and CJK/emoji/zero-width/RTL text) across both profiles, plus KeyProfile enum -> key-version/profile-detection assertions. Directly guards Data<->Vec<u8> FFI marshaling correctness.

Drives the real macOS app via XCUIApplication through key generation, key detail/QR/backup/expiry/revocation screens, contacts/certification flow, tutorial flows, tab-switch route-preservation, menu commands and keyboard shortcuts, and settings navigation -- each asserting the correct next screen's ready marker or control state appears. Genuine end-to-end navigation regression coverage, not tautological.

Exercises AddContactScreenModel against a real PgpEngine/ContactService stack: mode-reset/prefill precedence, confirmation-flow routing (candidate detection, stale-candidate fail-closed, already-pending errors), QR-photo race suppression on clear/disappear, and file-import staleness. All assertions are on observable screen-model state or actual contact-store side effects.

Uses a spy to verify the policy-switch workflow's actual branch logic: evaluate/reprotect/discard ordering, context invalidation count, no-op on unchanged policy, and (macOS) that the switch runs inside one operation-prompt session with resigns deferred to the prompt's end. All real control-flow guarantees.

Tests AppLaunchConfiguration's environment-gated derivation (UITest overrides honored only when allowed/detected). test_retiredSettingsRoot_fallsBackToMain looks like it pins a removed feature but actually guards a still-needed defensive fallback (unknown root value -> .main), not decode support for a format being deleted, so not a prune candidate.

Extensive coverage of the lock/authentication state machine via a spy: fail-closed ordering (relock-before-prompt), grace-period math, bypass, the foreground-active gate (spurious-.active loop regression), and the macOS .authenticating-rule deferred-away/session-hop races. Every assertion checks real state transitions or spy call ordering/counts.
Verifies real filesystem cleanup (temp artifacts + tutorial sandbox UserDefaults suites) and startup message merging/sanitization against actual coordinator methods.
Confirms unique per-operation owner directories and .complete file protection on real filesystem artifacts.
Exercises the classifier against real fixture data (armored encrypted vs cleartext-signed vs binary vs oversized), asserting on actual classification output.

Large but substantive: verifies trace capacity/enable-disable, monotonic timing, prompt/session boundary events across AuthenticationPromptCoordinator/AuthenticationManager/PrivateKeyAccessService, and repeatedly asserts NO sensitive values (fingerprint, private key, reason text) leak into trace metadata -- a real security property, not framework restatement.

Verifies the onOperationPromptsEnded/onOperationPromptSessionBegan hooks fire only at true stack-empty/stack-open transitions (not on nested prompts, not for privacy prompts) -- real nesting-depth behavior.

Confirms backup confirmation only fires after successful export (file exporter vs inline preview), that exporter failure doesn't confirm, and that content-clear suppresses a late in-flight export -- all real async race/ordering guarantees.

Security-relevant crypto-adjacent coverage: direct-key and user-ID-binding signature verification against real generated keys and FFI fixtures, certification-kind round trips, mismatched/out-of-range selector rejection without SE unwrap, and signer-identity resolution. All exercised through the real engine/adapter.

Two screen-model test classes covering catalog load/retry/cancel races, mode gating, binary-vs-text signature import precedence, export/save interception policy routing, and relock disabling persistence actions -- all against a real ContactService/CertificateSignatureService stack.

Verifies ContactDetailScreenModel projections (merge candidates, tags, preferred key) and mutation routing (merge, tag, set-preferred, delete) against a real protected ContactService, plus error surfacing on invalid mutation.

Exercises real certification-artifact save/dedupe/projection logic in ContactSnapshotMutator: stale-digest rejection, digest backfill, dedupe metadata refresh, and projection recompute (valid/invalidOrStale/certified) -- genuine business-rule enforcement, not format-migration cruft.

Covers identity/merge/tag-union/preferred-key persistence invariants in ContactSnapshotMutator and the protected ContactService, including reopen-after-relock round trips -- all real behavior a later refactor could break.

The corruption/missing-DMK/legacy-bootstrap tests read like migration tests but actually guard current, still-required fail-closed behavior (recoveryNeeded states, no silent domain recreation on corrupt/missing artifacts) -- this is live defensive logic, not cruft slated for removal, so not a prune candidate.

Verifies tag normalize/dedupe/persist-across-reopen, rename/delete/membership-replace, locked-state error mapping, and search ranking/scoping (contact fields vs recipient-only preferred-key matching) against a real ContactService.
