Shared XCTestCase base + fixture/harness builders (no assertions of its own); used by the other ContactService* test files to stand up a real protected-domain ContactService. Not itself prunable -- it backs real-guard tests across the C-file cluster.

Core contact add/update/remove/lookup behavior against a real PgpEngine-backed ContactService: duplicate/candidate detection, material/revocation/encryption-subkey updates via fixtures, binary vs armored import, and snapshot projection invariants (contactId/keyId not legacy-prefixed).

Verifies tag-management screen models are gated by protected-contacts availability and that create/rename/delete/membership-save/discard/clear-transient-input operate correctly against a real ContactService.

Directly tests ContactsDomainSnapshot.validateContract()'s actual invariants (schema version, duplicate IDs/fingerprints, foreign keys, preferred-key exclusivity/encryption capability, artifact digest/ownership matching) -- genuine data-integrity contract enforcement, not restating constants.
Round-trips a rich snapshot through the real SQLCipher-backed store, and verifies wrong-key and missing-database open attempts fail closed without creating a fallback authority.
Verifies search/tag-filter interaction, clear-transient-input, and stale-tag-filter pruning against a real protected ContactService and ContactsScreenModel.

Four of five tests are real-guard (non-empty/distinct per-category copy, cancellation reads as neutral not a failure, no leaked implementation vocabulary across all categories). One test only asserts an old retired generic string is absent -- a brittle-negative that mostly duplicates the distinctness test's coverage and will need edits every time unrelated copy changes.

Large but substantive: configuration/callback-capture races, content-clear suppression of late plaintext/callbacks mid-decrypt, file-vs-text import suggestion switching, cancellation cleanup (including cancel-after-service-success unpublished-output cleanup), and separate text/file verification state. All against real DecryptScreenModel async orchestration.

Security-critical Phase 1/Phase 2 boundary coverage (parseRecipients must not trigger SE unwrap; decrypt must): AEAD/MDC hard-fail on bit-flip and targeted tamper for both profiles, signer-identity resolution (own-key/contact/unknown), streaming file decrypt parity with in-memory, and cancellation cleanup. Exactly the kind of coverage that guards real regressions.

Verifies device-bound custody presentation: healthy vs missing vs degraded recovery-report mapping to a degraded flag, and that the backup screen fails closed (never shows the passphrase form) for device-bound keys.

Very large but substantive: configuration/callback-capture races, unverified-recipient warning gating (including preferred-vs-historical verification and re-validation after removal mid-flow), tag-based recipient selection/filtering stability, stale-recipient handling, quantum-safety badge derived from the actual encrypted artifact (not live selection), clipboard/export interception routing, and cancellation cleanup. All against real EncryptionService/ContactService.

Core encrypt-to-self, cross-profile (v4/v6 mixed recipients -> SEIPDv1), signing, and no-default-key error-path coverage, each verified by actually decrypting the produced ciphertext (or confirming the sender's own key cannot decrypt when encrypt-to-self is off). Real round-trip crypto behavior.

Exercises the real LocalDataResetService against real keychain/storage-root/SE-custody-handle-store collaborators: verifies keychain items, protected-data storage, SE custody handles, and root-secret checks are actually removed, and that failures (keychain delete failure, root-secret still present, SE inventory/cleanup failure) fail-closed with specific error codes. A regression that silently skipped deleting a keychain class, left an SE custody handle behind, or let reset succeed despite residual root secret would be caught here.

Not a test file itself — a small mock (DiskSpaceProvidable) with no XCTestCase/assertions of its own; it exists purely to support disk-space-related tests elsewhere. Nothing to prune here; the mock is simple enough that reviewing it is sufficient.

Wires a real AuthenticationPromptCoordinator + AppLockController + AuthenticationManager.switchMode and asserts the pre-auth prompt runs inside an operation-prompt session, a resign during it is deferred (never a mid-action lock), and an early-exit path leaves the session balanced. Directly reproduces the #495-class bug (a prompt outside the session causing a mid-action lock); regressing the session bookkeeping would fail these deterministically via the suspend/resign/settle choreography.

Overwhelmingly real-guard: PGPErrorMapper case-by-case mapping, ContactKeyRecord/PGPKeyIdentity computed properties, PGPKeyProfile/OpenPGPCertificationKind historical-raw-value round trips (protects on-disk/Codable compat), SignatureVerification state derivation, ProtectedOrdinarySettingsCoordinator load/lock semantics — all would catch real logic regressions. One test is weaker: it hardcodes a large literal-string table that mirrors the source's static per-family copy verbatim, so it mostly restates the switch cases rather than exercising independently-derived behavior.

Same #495-class composition regression as the mode-switch variant, but through KeyManagementService.modifyExpiry's pre-auth path: asserts the pre-auth runs inside an operation-prompt session, a resign during it is deferred, and the deferred away is processed fail-closed at the prompts' end. Regressing the session begin/end wiring around modifyExpiry would break these.

Exercises the real ModifyExpiryScreenModel: save/remove-expiry invoke the injected action with correct fingerprint/seconds and dismiss+complete exactly once, failures surface a mapped error, and handleDisappear suppresses a late completion/dismiss from an in-flight suspended action. Catches the common SwiftUI async bug class where a screen model fires callbacks after the view has gone away.

Loads the real bundled open-source-notice manifest and license text (not mocks) and checks the app notice/dual-license contents, per-notice license readability, search/sort behavior, and that build-only dependencies (r-efi, wasm-bindgen, windows-sys) are excluded while reachable transitive deps are kept. A broken manifest, a missing bundled license, or a leaked non-Apple-target dependency would fail this — real compliance-asset coverage per docs/RELEASE.md.

Drives the real OperationController with a controllable background runner/gate: verifies cancel keeps busy until the task actually finishes, and that a stale (cancelled/replaced) operation's completion or error never clobbers a newer operation's progress/error state. Regressing the generation-tracking guard would let a cancelled task's late completion overwrite a live one's UI state — exactly what these tests would catch.

Not a test file — a shared composition harness (real AuthenticationPromptCoordinator + AppLockController wired the way AppContainer does) used by the Mode-Switch and Modify-Expiry composition tests. No assertions of its own; nothing to prune, it's load-bearing infrastructure for real-guard tests elsewhere.

Exercises real P-256 ECDH via SecKey and compares the SE-backed derivation against an independently computed shared secret, verifies recipient-binding mismatch throws, verifies cancellation/auth-failure/unknown-error map to the expected ExternalP256KeyAgreementError categories, and rejects a shape-valid off-curve key. A bug in the ECDH wiring, the binding check, or the error-category mapping would fail these against real crypto, not stubs.

Table-drives the real PGPKeyCapabilityResolver security policy: valid vs. invalid configuration/custody pairs, which operations are supported/not-implemented/unavailable per policy (production, test-only signing/key-agreement/generation routes), and metadata-availability-gated export resolutions. This is the authorization matrix gating Secure Enclave private-key operations; a policy regression that silently exposed a not-implemented or blocked operation would be caught here.

Verifies the adapter zeroizes the just-generated/imported secret certificate when a later post-generation/post-import step (parseKeyInfo/detectProfile) throws, and that the zeroizer is never invoked on the success path (would double-zero the returned secret). Directly guards the memory-hygiene invariant (Hard Constraint #5): a regression that dropped the zeroize-on-failure path would leak secret key material in memory and this test would fail.

Round-trips real password-based encryption/decryption (SEIPDv1/v2) including signature preservation and contacts-locked signer resolution, and locates a real ciphertext byte to flip to prove SEIPDv1 tamper -> integrityCheckFailed and SEIPDv2 tamper -> AEAD/integrity hard-fail (never partial plaintext). Guards Hard Constraint #3 (AEAD hard-fail) end to end through the real PgpEngine.

Exercises the real PostGenerationPromptScreenModel: revocation-certificate export prepares the correct payload/filename, surfaces failures, explicitly never marks the key backed-up (a revocation export must not be conflated with a private-key backup), and handleDisappear suppresses a late export payload. Regressing any of these would misrepresent backup state or leak an export after the screen is gone.

Covers both the software-secret-certificate and real Secure-Enclave signer routes (via the real catalog router + shared handle store) for cleartext signing: verifies the correct route is taken without unwrapping when unnecessary, blocked/handle-failure/auth-failure categories propagate without falling back to software signing, and operation authorization is ended exactly once on both success and failure. A regression that fell back to software signing on an SE failure, or leaked an un-ended LAContext authorization, would fail here.

Same routing/auth-lifecycle guarantees as the cleartext signer, applied to third-party user-ID certification: real SE route generates and independently verifies a certification signature via the real catalog router, selector-mismatch is rejected before any handle lookup, and SE handle/auth failures never fall back to software. Also asserts zero unwanted keychain/catalog mutation during a certification. Regressions in any of those invariants would be caught against real crypto verification, not stubs.

Drives real interrupted-rewrap recovery scenarios (commit-required/preparing, with/without pending envelopes, mixed software+device-bound populations) against a real AuthenticationManager and keychain-backed envelopes, asserting the exact recovery outcome per case and that a bundle-less (device-bound) fingerprint blocks target-mode persistence — a documented contract other callers rely on. Also covers post-unlock warning composition/dedup and config resync. A regression in the recovery state machine would leave a device stuck in an inconsistent auth mode; these tests would catch it.

Detached-file-signing analogue of the cleartext-signing tests: real software vs. SE (v4 and v6) routes, real catalog router + shared handle store, progress-cancellation and SE-callback-failure paths never fall back to software, and operation authorization is ended exactly once on success/failure. Verifies signatures independently via the message adapter against real files on disk.

Exercises the real ephemeral-ECDH+HKDF+AES-GCM private-key envelope through MockSecureEnclave (same construction as production): round-trips including a >16KiB payload past a former size limit, rejects tampering of every authenticated field, rejects a re-bound public key (device-binding guard) before any key agreement, rejects the wrong unwrap handle/fingerprint, and rejects malformed/undecodable envelopes. This is the private-key-at-rest wrapping format; any weakening of its AEAD/binding checks would be caught directly.

Covers real SE modify-expiry (v4/v6, extend/remove, re-signing the transport subkey binding past the old expiry, recovery after the key is already expired) plus catalog/keychain-mutation absence, blocking-policy/missing-handle/cancellation/auth-failure categories without software fallback, single custody-authentication-per-call with authorization ended exactly once, and a suspended-mutation race verifying default-key/backup-flag merges land correctly against the reloaded catalog. Broad, behavior-driven coverage of a security-sensitive mutation path.

Real software vs. SE (v4/v6) key-agreement decryption routes, signature folding for signed SE-decrypted messages, real catalog router + shared handle store, role-mismatch/blocked/callback-failure fail without software fallback, recipient mismatch and tampered ciphertext hard-fail (payload authentication, never partial plaintext) for both single and mixed-recipient messages, and repeated decrypts stay consistent. Directly guards Hard Constraint #3 across the private-key decrypt surface.

Exercises the real PrivateKeyOperationRouter end-to-end: software vs. SE routing, blocking policy short-circuits before handle lookup, per-policy signing/key-agreement/generation exposure, public-binding-inspection/fingerprint-mismatch/wrong-role/wrong-binding failure categories, custody-authentication cancellation/failure/lockout mapping without ever threading a context into a load, authorization-ended-exactly-once on success/failure, and (macOS) that the SE authorization window runs inside an operation-prompt session with a deferred-resign decided at the session's end. This is the core security-policy dispatcher for every private-key operation; broad and load-bearing.

Real software/SE (v4/v6) signing routes for password-encrypted messages, decrypted and verified via the real engine; real catalog router + shared handle store path; blocked/missing-handle/cancellation/callback-failure categories without software fallback; wrong-role rejection; and operation authorization ended exactly once on success and on adapter failure.

Real SE (v4/v6) subkey/user-ID revocation-certificate export via the real catalog router, verifying the exported artifact is a well-formed armored PGP signature and that catalog/keychain state is untouched; blocking-policy/selector-mismatch/handle-failure/cancellation categories without software fallback; and custody authentication happening exactly once per export with authorization ended after both success and signing failure across two consecutive exports.

Streaming-file analogue of the message-decryption tests, plus explicit checks that no `.tmp` scratch artifact is left beside the output after success, tamper, mixed-recipient, or repeated decrypts — a concrete guard against leaking partial plaintext to disk on the streaming path, which the message-level tests can't observe.

Real software/SE (v4/v6) signing routes for streaming file encryption verified by independent decryption; unsigned encryption never routes/unwraps a signer; blocked/missing-handle/cancellation/callback-failure without fallback (with output-file-absence checks); real EncryptionService integration proving self-key default resolution and that a mid-flight signing failure leaves no orphaned temporary streaming artifact on disk.

Text-encryption analogue of the streaming/detached tests: real software/SE (v4/v6) signing routes verified by independent decryption, real EncryptionService integration with explicit self-key, blocked/missing-handle/cancellation/callback-failure without software fallback, and authorization ended exactly once on both success and failure.

Table-drives the real ProtectedDataAccessGateClassifier across every bootstrap-outcome/framework-state combination, and exercises the real post-unlock domain-opener coordinator: opens committed domains with a handoff LAContext, refuses to authorize without a context, blocks on a pending mutation without touching the root secret, surfaces mock-keychain interaction-not-allowed as authorizationDenied, and creates+opens the framework sentinel as a genuine second domain. A regression in gate/post-unlock sequencing that skipped a check or read the root secret prematurely would fail these.

Verifies the real AppSessionOrchestrator's handoff-LAContext custody (recorded once, consumed exactly once), content-clear generation bump, discard-on-policy-change, and local-data-reset behavior (with/without preserving lastAuthenticationDate). A regression that let the handoff context be consumed twice, or leaked it past a content-clear, would fail here.

Exercises the real Keychain-backed domain-key store: service-name construction, staged-row-removed/committed-row-written transaction semantics, stale staged-row replacement, missing/corrupt committed row handling. One test (test_legacyFileBackedWrappedDomainMasterKeyIsIgnored) guards that a pre-Keychain file-backed record is never resurrected as valid — a fail-safe against silently treating stray legacy artifacts as real domain keys; it will only become moot once the legacy file-path code itself is deleted (tracked separately), so it is not a prune candidate today.

Real domain-key-manager crypto (deriveWrappingRootKey zeroizes the input, per-domain wrapping keys differ, wrap/unwrap round-trips, malformed-record-length rejection), SensitiveBytes zeroize, and the real ProtectedDataSessionCoordinator's authorize/relock/reauthorize/remove-shared-right lifecycle including relock-participant deduplication/failure-reporting and entering restartRequired on a participant failure. Broad crypto+lifecycle coverage of the session layer.

Exercises real PrivateKeyControlStore bootstrap (requires a handoff context; creates the first domain with standard defaults), a real-Secure-Enclave-gated test proving a second ProtectedData domain requires an authorized wrapping key after a process restart (skips cleanly when SE unavailable), pending-mutation fail-closed, sentinel non-creation from an empty registry, generic recovery-coordinator dispatch/mismatch, and handler-list dispatch by pending domain ID. Directly exercises the production recovery machinery, not a simplified stand-in.

Not a test file — shared typealiases, mocks (RecordingProtectedDataRootSecretStore, MockProtectedDataRelockParticipant, etc.), the ProtectedDataFrameworkTestCase base class, and envelope/registry fixture builders consumed by every other ProtectedData* test file in this directory. No assertions of its own; nothing here should be pruned, it's load-bearing infrastructure.

Exercises the real ProtectedDataRegistryStore state machine: empty/missing-registry bootstrap (including file-artifact and Keychain-artifact detection forcing frameworkRecoveryNeeded), pending-mutation disposition preservation, first-domain shared-right cleanup ordering and every one of its abort conditions (committed membership present, unrelated/concurrent pending mutation, remaining Keychain artifacts), second-domain delete preserving the shared root until the last domain is removed, and abandon/complete-pending-delete transitions including a cleanup-failure path that must leave the pending mutation intact. This is the core crash-consistency contract for ProtectedData; regressions here would cause real data-loss/inconsistency bugs.

Real root-secret envelope round trip via MockProtectedDataDeviceBindingProvider, rejection of tampering on every authenticated field, AAD binding of the ephemeral public key plus rejection of an unsupported AAD version, malformed-contract/unsupported-field rejection, wrong-shared-right-identifier rejection, and an undecodable Keychain payload failing closed through the real KeychainProtectedDataRootSecretStore. Core AEAD/binding guarantees for the device-bound root secret.

Verifies the real storage root lands inside Application Support (not Documents/tmp), that every persisted artifact (registry, bootstrap metadata, staged/promoted domain envelopes) gets NSFileProtectionComplete, and that the production containment contract fails closed before any bootstrap write when the root is outside Application Support, file protection is unsupported, or a root/base-directory symlink escapes Application Support (with a positive case proving a symlink resolving back inside App Support still succeeds). Directly guards the on-disk security contract for ProtectedData.

Real seal/open and encode/decode round trips for the per-domain AEAD envelope, fresh-nonce distinctness, rejection of a wrong domain master key, rejection of tampered ciphertext/tag and of a re-labelled domainID/schemaVersion/generationIdentifier (all AAD-authenticated), and malformed-contract/unsupported-field/undecodable-payload rejection. Core AEAD contract for every protected domain's on-disk payload.

Exercises the real ProtectedSettingsStore against real storage/keychain collaborators: reset requires the wrapping key before deleting when a sentinel domain remains (and refuses to touch the shared root otherwise), fresh install produces the current schema-versioned payload wired through the real ordinary-settings coordinator, ensure-committed is a true no-op on an already-committed domain, corrupt payload / corrupt or missing wrapped domain master key all fail closed into recoveryNeeded, and ordinary-settings mutations persist across an explicit relock+reopen cycle. Broad coverage of the settings domain's crash-consistency and lock/relock behavior.

Exercises real certificate-merge behavior (NoOp vs Updated outcomes, revocation/user-id/subkey absorption, primary-UID selection precedence after merge using real Sequoia certs and checked-in fixture .gpg pairs, fingerprint-mismatch and secret-bearing-input rejection). Assertions check concrete outcome enums, fingerprints, and PgpError variants — a regression in merge logic would break these.

Verifies verify_direct_key_signature returns the correct CertificateSignatureStatus (Valid/Invalid/SignerMissing) and rejects the wrong signature-packet type with CorruptData, using real signatures generated and re-verified through the actual API.

Confirms issuer-guided key selection rejects a signing-only subkey for direct-key signatures, and that certification generation prefers the certification-capable primary key over a certification subkey — genuine selection-algorithm behavior with concrete status/fingerprint assertions.

Round-trips certification kinds across both key profiles, exercises duplicate-occurrence selectors, and asserts specific PgpError variants for public-only signer, selector mismatch/out-of-range, and an unusable (non-certification-capable) signer. All negatives assert a distinct, meaningful error category rather than mere absence.

Covers verification status transitions (SignerMissing/Invalid/Valid), the issuer-subpacket fallback path with a certification subkey signer, and malformed/wrong-type/selector-mismatch inputs, each asserting the specific resulting status or PgpError variant.

Validates the Device-Bound PQ ·High (ML-DSA-87/Ed448 + ML-KEM-1024/X448) certificate shape and algorithm identifiers, and — critically — that a message encrypted by stock Sequoia's native composite encapsulation decrypts through this app's split-custody path (external ML-KEM-1024 decapsulation + vendored combiner); this is the correctness proof for the vendored KEM combiner, not a restated constant.

Covers composite (·768) key-generation shape/policy validity, component inspection, revocation, expiry modification, and certification against real Sequoia certs, plus fail-closed negatives (invalid component key material, provider/public-key mismatch, cancellation) that assert exact PgpError variants and reason substrings.

Exercises the split-custody KEM-combiner against foreign (stock Sequoia) ciphertexts, wildcard-recipient PKESK-skip semantics (two distinct skip reasons), mixed v4/composite recipient format downgrade, wrong-key-share/cancellation fail-closed paths, and full password/file streaming round-trips with signature verification — all genuine crypto-interop coverage.

Directly validates the profile-correct message-format auto-selection rule (a CLAUDE.md hard constraint) for every sender/recipient/encrypt-to-self combination, cross-profile signature verification, match_recipients, and modify_expiry public-key-only rejection. Every assertion checks a concrete format flag, plaintext, fingerprint, or PgpError variant from a real round-trip.

Exercises the detailed multi-signature state model end-to-end (multi-signer, expired signer, repeated signer, known+unknown signer mix, tampered data, decrypt-path multi-sig, cancellation) with per-entry status/state assertions built from real signed/encrypted artifacts.

Shells out to the actual `gpg` binary in an isolated GNUPGHOME to prove Sequoia-generated keys, ciphertext, and signatures interoperate with an independent, real GnuPG implementation (import, decrypt, verify 'Good signature', v6 rejection). Skips gracefully only when gpg is absent; genuinely exercises cross-implementation compatibility otherwise.

Mixed file, three of four tests are prune candidates. test_c3_8_gpg_rejection_output_recorded is a source-audit: it only greps the checked-in fixtures/gpg_v6_import_rejection.txt for string patterns and exit codes and silently `return`s (no failure) if the file is missing — it never calls any pgp_mobile code. test_generate_v6_fixture is a #[ignore]d fixture-generation utility (writes a file to disk) whose only assertion (Advanced profile => key_version 6) is already covered by the real gpg-rejection test in gnupg_binary_tests.rs. test_c3_gpg_rsa_key_fixture_import and test_c3_gpg_revoked_key_fixture are permanently #[ignore]d and reference fixtures (gpg_rsa4096_pubkey.asc/secretkey.asc, gpg_revoked_pubkey.asc) that do not exist anywhere under tests/fixtures/ — dead stubs that could never pass if un-ignored.

The great majority (C3.1-C3.8 fixture-based interop, DEFLATE/ZLIB decompression, bit-tamper error-variant checks, armor round-trip, cross-profile-to-GnuPG format checks) is real-guard: it decrypts/verifies genuine GnuPG-generated fixture artifacts and asserts concrete plaintext, format flags, or PgpError variants. test_c2b_10_compressed_seipd2_verified_by_composition is a documented placeholder (comment says 'KNOWN LIMITATION M6'): it doesn't test a compressed-SEIPDv2 message at all — it just re-runs the separately-covered DEFLATE-decrypt and SEIPDv2-decrypt paths and calls the pairing 'verified by composition', so no assertion ever exercises the combined scenario the test name claims to cover.

Genuine 50MB/100MB encrypt/decrypt round-trip size+content checks for both key profiles; #[ignore]d only for local speed and explicitly run by PR/nightly CI lanes per the file's own header comment — real regression coverage for large-payload streaming, not vacuous.

Validates that the Secure-Enclave-shaped P-256 candidate certificate (both v4 and v6) has the correct structure (no secret material, correct key version/algorithm, distinct signing/agreement keys, policy-valid, discoverable selectors) via the real generation and validation APIs.

Exercises the external-P256-signer certification path for both key versions and all certification kinds, duplicate-occurrence selectors, primary-vs-subkey signer preference, expired-but-not-revoked signer, and a wide set of fail-closed negatives (revoked/unresolved signer, wrong role, malformed/wrong-digest responses, wrong public key, invalid selectors) — each asserting a specific status, hash algorithm, or PgpError variant, with several asserting the runtime signing callback was never invoked.

Round-trips external-signer cleartext signing/verification for v4 and v6, plus cancellation, sanitized-failure-category propagation, revoked-key rejection before the callback fires, malformed/wrong-digest/wrong-key/mismatched-fingerprint rejection, and non-P256/wrong-role input rejection — all real signing and verification paths with concrete state/error assertions.

Same fail-closed/negative matrix as cleartext.rs but for detached-file signing (streaming), including a progress-cancellation path — genuine behavior coverage of the runtime P-256 signer over real temp files.

Covers external-signer expiry modification/removal for v4 and v6 including recovery of an already-expired certificate, SHA-256 binding-hash preservation, software-key hash/backsig preservation across profiles, and a comprehensive fail-closed matrix (revoked cert, cancellation, malformed/wrong-digest/wrong-key responses, mismatched fingerprint, wrong-role signer) — all asserting concrete key-info fields or PgpError variants from real re-signing operations.

Round-trips password-protected encrypt+external-sign for both key versions, both SEIPD formats, and binary/armored variants, decrypting and verifying the signature each time, plus the standard cancellation/sanitized-failure/invalid-response/wrong-key/wrong-role negative matrix.

Exercises external-signer subkey and User ID revocation generation for v4/v6, hash-algorithm selection, duplicate-occurrence selectors, primary-signer/certification-capability gating before the callback, revoked/unresolved-signer and expired-but-live-signer handling, and cancellation/failure-category/invalid-response/wrong-key/selector-mismatch negatives verified by actually inserting the revocation signature and checking RevocationStatus. One test (test_external_signer_runtime_user_id_revocation_accepts_duplicate_occurrence_selector) only asserts the returned bytes are non-empty rather than verifying the revocation applies to the correct occurrence — weaker than its sibling certification-selector test, but not brittle or vacuous enough to warrant deletion.

Covers external-signer streaming file encrypt+sign for v4/v6, mixed-recipient and encrypt-to-self format downgrade to SEIPDv1, and a fail-closed matrix (cancellation, progress-cancellation, sanitized failures, invalid responses, wrong key, mismatched fingerprint, wrong-role input) that also asserts partial output files are removed on failure — a real, security-relevant invariant.

Covers external-signer encrypt+sign for v4/v6 with decrypt+verify, mixed-recipient/encrypt-to-self format downgrade, and the standard cancellation/sanitized-failure/invalid-response/wrong-key/wrong-role negative matrix — real crypto round-trips throughout.

50/100 MB encrypt+decrypt round-trips for Universal and Advanced profiles asserting byte-identical plaintext recovery through the real engine API; genuine regression guard for large-payload streaming correctness, gated #[ignore="slow"] and run explicitly in CI.

Exercises SKESK4/SKESK6 password-message encrypt/decrypt across SEIPDv1/v2, multi-password fallthrough, mixed PKESK+SKESK, tamper/auth-failure detection via targeted packet mutation, and default S2K/cipher assertions — all against real ciphertext produced by the engine and Sequoia's own packet builders.

Verifies v6 Ed25519/X25519 cert shape, profile classification, Argon2id export/import round-trip, and cross-profile mismatch guards (Modern vs Advanced/Universal) — real crypto behavior a regression could silently break.
Verifies ML-DSA-87/ML-KEM-1024 (PQ-High) cert shape, profile classification, Argon2id export requiring every secret packet individually, and PQ vs PQ-High tier mismatch guards in both directions.

Covers RFC 9980 cert shape, algorithm-aware profile classification (including a foreign sq-style cert and the 87-tier boundary), classical-profile fallback stability, Argon2id export coverage per secret packet, and cross-tier mismatch guards.

Drives the real recipient matrix (PQ-only, PQ+v4, PQ+v6, PQ+PQ) through encrypt/decrypt, asserts the AES-256 SEIPD floor by actually decrypting with Sequoia's low-level stream API to observe the negotiated cipher, and exercises message_quantum_safety classification plus cleartext sign/verify.

Every test exercises real code (PgpEngine encrypt/decrypt/sign/verify against a hand-built RFC 9980 cert) so none is meaningless, but the file's own header says 'Spike-only evidence; not part of the product test matrix' (issue #567 Phase 0) and its coverage (cert shape, encrypt/decrypt round-trip, sign/verify, mixed classical+PQ recipients, parse_recipients) is now fully subsumed by the production-path tests in portable_pq_key_lifecycle_tests.rs and portable_pq_message_tests.rs added in later phases. It still runs in the default `cargo test` lane (no #[ignore]) and prints SPIKE-* diagnostics that serve no assertion purpose. This is the kind of superseded exploratory artifact the project's own campaign (#567) flagged for eventual cleanup.

Export/import (incl. Unicode passphrases), wrong-passphrase and wrong-profile rejection, revocation generation/validation, expiry modification (extend/remove/expire) with re-encrypt/decrypt round-trips, and concurrent encrypt from two threads — all against the real v4 Universal-profile path.

Key generation algorithm assertions, sign/verify, encrypt/decrypt (plain, signed, encrypt-to-self), file sizes up to 10 MB, multi-position tamper detection requiring at least one IntegrityCheckFailed hit, detached signatures, armor round-trip, and concurrency — genuine behavioral coverage of the Profile A message path.

Wrong-profile export rejection, revocation validate/cross-key-reject, Argon2id parameter assertions (memory/parallelism/passes match PRD), expiry modify/extend/remove/expire, and match_recipients for v6 — real behavior, not restated constants.

v6 Ed448/X448 generation, sign/verify, encrypt/decrypt with AEAD, multi-position AEAD tamper hard-fail sweep, detached signature, multi-recipient, armor round-trip, and concurrency — real AEAD/OCB behavior under test.

The Argon2id-heavy counterpart to profile_b_key_lifecycle_tests.rs (export/import, wrong passphrase, Unicode passphrase, full round-trip) kept #[ignore="slow"] to keep the default lane fast; genuine coverage, deliberately segregated rather than deleted.
validate_public_certificate accepts real Profile A/B public certs and rejects secret certs with the exact CONTACT_IMPORT_PUBLIC_ONLY_REASON — a genuine security-relevant contact-import guard.

QR/URL round-trip for v4 and v6 keys, malformed-input handling, secret-key rejection at both encode and decode (defense in depth), URL length bounds, and multiple malformed-payload edge cases (empty, extra segments, query params, Unicode, wrong base64 alphabet) — real parsing/validation behavior.

Generates and validates key/subkey/user-ID revocations, rejects public-only and encrypted-secret inputs, exercises selector-based revocation (including duplicate-occurrence and out-of-range/mismatch errors), and asserts the emitted signature packets carry the correct SignatureType — thorough coverage of a security-sensitive code path.

Drives real bidirectional GnuPG interop for the SE-custody v4 family (import, sign-verify, encrypt/decrypt through the production external-signer/key-agreement seams, tamper hard-fail) using a software P-256 stand-in with a `gpg` binary; skips cleanly when gpg is absent unless CYPHERAIR_REQUIRE_GPG=1 mandates it.

Single but substantive smoke test verifying the shared SoftwareP256Material test helper produces a public-only SE-shaped cert whose GnuPG-importable TSK has matching fingerprints and both secret halves — protects a foundational test fixture used by three other real test files.
SEIPDv2/AEAD round-trip, AEAD tamper hard-fail, and signed+encrypted decrypt/verify through the production SE v6 key-agreement seam — real RFC 9580 AEAD correctness evidence, not restated constants.

Error-classification for tampered A/B ciphertext, no-plaintext-leak on wrong key, and legacy SEIPD (tag 9, no MDC) rejection by directly rewriting the packet tag byte — a genuine, currently-active security invariant (never accept unauthenticated legacy encryption), not obsolete migration code.

Armor kind round-trip for all ArmorKind variants, Unknown-kind rejection, Unicode plaintext round-trip for both profiles, and the L2 expiry-timestamp-on-expired-cert fix — each asserts real parser/engine output.

parse_recipients correctness/failure modes, expired/revoked-key encrypt rejection, empty-recipients handling, and the WCR-01 revoked-subkey filtering (reject cert with only a revoked encryption subkey, but use a live sibling subkey and skip the revoked one) with a matching key_info mirror check — substantive security-policy coverage.

Tampered cleartext/detached verification, expired-signer handling across cleartext/detached/decrypt paths, and the WCR-01 signing-side mirror (reject a signing cert whose only signing subkey is revoked, but sign with a live sibling and prove via issuer fingerprint it wasn't the revoked one) for both plain-sign and sign-while-encrypt — real, carefully constructed security regression coverage.

Extensive coverage of discover_certificate_selectors: duplicate User IDs, occurrence-index preservation, primary/revocation state per-occurrence, future-dated/expired bindings and revocations not being 'current', newer-binding-clears-older-revocation ordering, native subkey order, and cross-check that generated selectors actually drive the revocation APIs — genuine time-based edge-case logic that a later refactor could quietly break.

File-based tamper handling for both profiles with no-decrypted-file/no-leftover-.tmp assertions, error reclassification (must not surface as generic FileIoError), progress-reporter cancellation, and cleanup-after-cancel — real streaming-path regression coverage including resource-hygiene assertions.

File encrypt/decrypt round-trip (both profiles), signed variants, detached sign/verify, match_recipients_from_file, and cross-profile SEIPDv1 downgrade — all against real file I/O and the streaming API.

Verifies issuer-guided direct-key-signature verification rejects a signing-only subkey correctly, and that user-ID certification generation prefers the primary key over a certification-capable subkey — real signer-selection logic.

Certification generation/verification preserves kind across all profiles and CertificationKind values, selector duplicate-occurrence handling, and rejection of public-only signer / selector mismatch / out-of-range / unusable-certifier inputs — thorough real coverage.

Signer-missing, invalid-match, issuer-fallback-via-subkey, issuer-guided-rejects-signing-only-subkey, wrong-signature-type, malformed-signature, and selector out-of-range/mismatch cases — all exercise verify_user_id_binding_signature_by_selector against real generated signatures.

Not a test file itself — pure test-fixture helper (generate_foreign_pq) with zero #[test] functions, consumed by portable_pq_key_lifecycle_tests.rs. Forced into this category only because the schema has no 'support module' verdict; it is actively used infrastructure, not a pruning candidate.

Not a test file itself — shared SoftwareP256Material/OracleSigningProvider fixture with zero #[test] functions, consumed by secure_enclave_support_tests.rs, secure_enclave_gnupg_interop_tests.rs, and secure_enclave_v6_aead_evidence_tests.rs. Forced into this category only because the schema has no 'support module' verdict; it is load-bearing shared infrastructure, not a pruning candidate.

Not a test file itself — tamper_near_payload_tail/tamper_at_ratio helpers with zero #[test] functions, consumed by many real tamper-detection tests across the suite. Forced into this category only because the schema has no 'support module' verdict; it is load-bearing shared infrastructure, not a pruning candidate.

Runtime external P-256 signer password-message encrypt/decrypt/verify for v4+v6 across both SEIPD formats and binary/armored, plus cancellation, sanitized failure-category propagation, malformed/wrong-digest/wrong-key rejection, and secret/non-P256/wrong-role input rejection — real seam coverage.

External-signer selective (subkey/user-ID) revocation generation, SHA-256 hash enforcement contrasted with the software SHA-512 default, duplicate-occurrence selector handling, primary-only/certification-capable-primary/secret-input/revoked-or-unresolved 'no callback before validation' guarantees, expired-but-unrevoked-cert allowance, and cancellation/category/malformed-response/wrong-digest/wrong-key rejection — extensive real seam coverage.

Runtime external-signer streaming file encrypt+sign for v4/v6, mixed-recipient/self downgrade to SEIPDv1, cancellation and progress-cancellation with partial-output cleanup, sanitized callback-failure propagation, and malformed/wrong-key/wrong-fingerprint/wrong-role rejection with output-file cleanup assertions — real file-path coverage.

Runtime external-signer sign+encrypt for v4/v6, mixed-recipient/self downgrade, cancellation, sanitized failure propagation, and malformed/wrong-key/wrong-fingerprint/wrong-role rejection — mirrors the file-based test with the in-memory API.

Embedded `mod tests` performs real Ed25519/X25519/Ed448/X448 component round-trips (sign, derive-public, shared-secret agreement) and rejects malformed/all-zero secrets — exercises actual crypto primitives, not restated constants.

Exercises ExternalCompositeDecryptor/ExternalCompositeHighDecryptor: rejects non-composite public keys and mismatched classical components, records skippable vs hard-failure errors correctly for non-composite ciphertext and cancellation, validates the ML-KEM request payload matches the recipient's public key/ciphertext, and asserts malformed/wrong key shares fail closed at AES-key-unwrap — real seam behavior for both the 65/768 and 87/1024 tiers.

ExternalCompositeSigner: self-verifying composite sign, rejection of non-composite keys/mismatched classical component/wrong digest length/malformed or all-zero external signature/corrupted signature via self-verify, and propagation of external failure vs cancellation — genuine crypto-boundary coverage.

The largest and most thorough file in scope: real end-to-end encrypt/decrypt via the external P-256 key-agreement seam for v4 and v6, signature-status preservation, callback cancel/failure/invalid-response mapping to typed categories, hard-abort-before-later-PKESK ordering, hidden-recipient (wildcard) skip-vs-hard-fail semantics, request point-binding assertions, no-fallback-to-secret-cert-decryption, wrong-public-binding and session-key-validation fail-closed checks, payload-authentication hard-fail after session-key acceptance, and streaming file-decrypt variants including repeated-decrypt consistency and no-residual-scratch-file assertions. All assertions test real cryptographic/seam behavior a regression would break.

ExternalP256Signer: real detached-signature verify for v4/v6, no-fallback-to-secret-signing on external failure, key-agreement-role rejection, unsupported-hash/invalid-shape rejection, and wrong-digest/wrong-public-key rejection — genuine boundary coverage.

ML-KEM-768/ML-DSA-65 public-key validators: accepts canonical coefficients at boundary values (0, 1, Q-1), rejects non-canonical (Q, 4095) and malformed shapes — real input-validation logic protecting composite key generation.

generate_secure_enclave_public_certificate for v4/v6: full cert-shape/feature/validation/selector/revocation/inspection assertions, rejection of invalid/duplicate public keys, non-SE-cert and wrong-role/missing-material rejection for the inspection helper, non-distinct role-point rejection, expired-binding recoverability, and external-signer failure/cancellation/category/wrong-digest/wrong-key fail-closed behavior — real, security-relevant generator coverage.

Thin embedded smoke test (`test_engine_smoke`) that constructs PgpEngine and asserts decode_qr_url rejects invalid input — minimal but exercises real behavior, not a restated constant; low value but not vacuous or brittle.

SignatureCollector summary-folding logic: VerifyLike freezes on first Valid/hard-failure, DecryptLike follows later results until Valid, repeated signers are not collapsed, and no-signatures defaults to NotSigned — real state-machine behavior a later refactor of the fold logic could quietly break (ties to the memory-noted signature state-model follow-up debt).

sites.precondition: privateKeyControlStore.recoveryJournal().rewrapTargetMode != nil (an auth-mode-switch journal entry survived from a previous run), sub-branched on rewrapPhase == .preparing vs .commitRequired
sites.readsState: PrivateKeyControlRecoveryJournal.rewrapTargetMode / .rewrapPhase, persisted inside the AEAD-sealed private-key-control protected-data domain payload (PrivateKeyControlStore.Payload.recoveryJournal)
sites.producer: PrivateKeyControlStore.beginRewrap(targetMode:) sets .preparing; markRewrapCommitRequired() sets .commitRequired. Both are called synchronously, in that order, from PrivateKeyRewrapWorkflow.run(), which is invoked only from AuthenticationManager.performSwitchMode (Sources/Security/AuthenticationManager.swift:814) -- i.e. every time the user toggles Standard/High Security mode in Settings (SettingsScreenModel -> AuthenticationManager.switchMode).
sites.evidence: AppContainer.recoverPrivateKeyControlJournalsAfterPostUnlock (Sources/App/AppContainer.swift:1494-1519) calls authManager.checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedRewrap(fingerprints:) unconditionally on every post-unlock load, i.e. every app launch/unlock. PrivateKeyRewrapWorkflow Phase A/B (Sources/Security/PrivateKeyRewrapWorkflow.swift) writes exactly the pending/permanent Keychain bundle states and journal transitions that KeyMigrationCoordinator.recoveryAction reads back; this is an in-app two-phase-commit for a user action, not a version migration, and it produces state on every fresh install the first time the user switches auth mode. Confirmed live, not tied to any prior-shipped-version artifact (app is unpublished).

sites.precondition: privateKeyControlStore.recoveryJournal().modifyExpiry != nil (an interrupted modify-expiry operation's journal entry survived from a previous run)
sites.readsState: PrivateKeyControlRecoveryJournal.modifyExpiry (ModifyExpiryRecoveryEntry.fingerprint), persisted in the same protected-data payload as the rewrap journal
sites.producer: KeyMutationService.performModifySoftwareExpiry (Sources/Services/KeyManagement/KeyMutationService.swift:279-284) calls privateKeyControlStore.beginModifyExpiry(fingerprint:) after staging the pending SE-wrapped bundle and before deleting/promoting it. Runs whenever a user modifies a software-custody key's expiry via Key Detail > Modify Expiry.
sites.evidence: AppContainer.recoverPrivateKeyControlJournalsAfterPostUnlock calls keyManagement.checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedModifyExpiry() unconditionally on every post-unlock load, alongside the rewrap recovery call. Test coverage exists for both reachable outcomes (Tests/ServiceTests/KeyManagementServiceExpiryRecoveryTests.swift: test_modifyExpiryCrashRecovery_retryableFailure_keepsFlags, test_modifyExpiryCrashRecovery_unrecoverable_clearsFlags), confirming the mechanism is exercised and current-code-produced.

sites.symbol: KeyMigrationCoordinator.recoveryAction(for:) — the KeyBundleState.partial arms: (.complete, .partial) at line 104, (.partial, .complete) at line 108, and 3 of the 4 alternatives in the unrecoverable case at lines 110-112 ((.partial,.missing), (.partial,.partial), (.missing,.partial))
sites.precondition: bundleStore.bundleState(fingerprint:namespace:) returns .partial for the permanent and/or pending namespace
sites.producer: KeyBundleStore.bundleState() itself: it maps a single Keychain existence check (keychain.exists) directly to .complete or .missing and never returns .partial. The file's own doc comment (lines 17-22) states: 'the bundle is a single self-contained PrivateKeyEnvelope row, so bundleState only ever returns .missing or .complete. .partial is retained so the interrupted-rewrap recovery coordinators stay exhaustive and fail closed if a future storage shape can ever observe a partial row.'
sites.evidence: Grep across Sources/Tests shows KeyBundleState.partial is never constructed/returned anywhere except as an enum case and as switch-arm patterns in KeyMigrationCoordinator and PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator. Tests/ServiceTests/KeyMigrationCoordinatorTests.swift:25-28 explicitly documents this: 'The single-row envelope makes intra-bundle .partial states impossible ... the coordinator keeps its .partial arms as fail-closed dead-defense.' This is exactly the 'speculative forward-compat version scaffolding' the task calls out — the unpublished app's only storage shape (single atomic Keychain row) can never produce .partial, so these arms, and the .partial case itself, are dead weight kept only for a hypothetical future storage format that doesn't exist.

sites.symbol: PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator.recoverCommitRequiredRewrapMigration(for:) — the KeyBundleState.partial arms: (.partial, .complete) at line 87, the whole (.complete, .partial) arm at line 101 (always returns .retryableFailure, unreachable), and 3 of 4 alternatives in the unrecoverable case at lines 103-105
sites.precondition: bundleStore.bundleState(fingerprint:namespace:) returns .partial for the permanent and/or pending namespace, evaluated only when journal.rewrapPhase == .commitRequired
sites.readsState: KeyBundleState, produced by KeyBundleStore.bundleState(fingerprint:namespace:) (same producer as the sibling finding above)
sites.producer: KeyBundleStore.bundleState() — same as above; cannot return .partial for this single-row envelope storage model.
sites.evidence: Tests/ServiceTests/PrivateKeyControlRecoveryTests.swift:373-378 records that the maintainer already removed the one test that exercised this file's (.complete, .partial) commit-required arm, with the comment: 'the single-row private-key envelope makes a partially-written pending bundle structurally impossible ... so the (.complete, .partial) commit-required arm it exercised is now unreachable.' The dead arms were deliberately left in the switch statement after the test was deleted, per the same speculative-forward-compat pattern as the sibling KeyMigrationCoordinator finding.

sites.symbol: KeyMutationService.checkAndRecoverFromInterruptedModifyExpiry() — the `guard let fingerprint = entry.fingerprint, !fingerprint.isEmpty else { ...; return .unrecoverable }` branch at lines 544-548
sites.precondition: entry.fingerprint == nil, or entry.fingerprint == "" (an empty string), where entry = privateKeyControlStore.recoveryJournal().modifyExpiry
sites.producer: The only two producers are PrivateKeyControlStore.beginModifyExpiry(fingerprint:) and InMemoryPrivateKeyControlStore.beginModifyExpiry(fingerprint:) (both take a non-optional String parameter), and both are only ever called from KeyMutationService.performModifySoftwareExpiry with `fingerprint = route.identity.fingerprint`, a real non-empty hex fingerprint from an existing key identity. No code path ever constructs ModifyExpiryRecoveryEntry with a nil or empty fingerprint.
sites.evidence: The field is typed String? purely so Codable's synthesized decodeIfPresent tolerates a hypothetically-missing key, not because any writer ever sets it to nil; grep across Sources/Tests shows every construction site passes a real fingerprint. No test in Tests/ServiceTests/KeyManagementServiceExpiryRecoveryTests.swift (or elsewhere) exercises the nil/empty branch — both existing crash-recovery tests (test_modifyExpiryCrashRecovery_retryableFailure_keepsFlags, test_modifyExpiryCrashRecovery_unrecoverable_clearsFlags) use a real fingerprint and instead exercise the bundle-state-derived unrecoverable path, not this guard.

sites.precondition: A file exists at <domainDir>/wrapped-dmk.plist or wrapped-dmk.staged.plist for the Contacts domain
sites.producer: None. sites.precondition: For the "contacts" ProtectedData domain, the bootstrap.plist sidecar's `expectedCurrentGenerationIdentifier` field is non-nil (i.e. holds a generation-slot identifier string).
sites.producer: The only code that ever wrote a non-nil value here was the pre-SQLCipher Contacts implementation, which (like the other 4 domain stores today) used a current/previous/pending generation-slot envelope rotation and recorded the authoritative slot's generation id in bootstrap metadata. That code path was deleted wholesale in commit f55c556 ("feat: cut over contacts storage to sqlcipher"), the same commit that introduced this guard and switched ContactsDomainStore.saveBootstrapMetadata() (lines 274-284) to always write `expectedCurrentGenerationIdentifier: nil`. No line of code in the current tree can produce a non-nil value for this domain.
sites.evidence: git show f55c556 diff adds both the nil-only write and the `guard ... == nil else { throw "...legacy generation authority." }` read in the same commit, replacing the old generation-identifier-bearing write path it deletes. CypherAir X has never shipped externally, so no real device could carry the old pre-cutover shape forward into a fresh install — only an internal/dev build running the deleted code could have produced it, and that code no longer exists in the repository. This mirrors an already-completed sibling cleanup for Contacts' payload schema (commit 28fd675, "refactor: remove Contacts snapshot v1→v2 migration") and for KeyMetadata's payload schema (commit 2fbc4d2, "retire Phase 3 key-metadata legacy surface") — this bootstrap-metadata-level check is the one layer those two sweeps didn't reach.

sites.symbol: ContactsDomainStore.validateBootstrapMetadata() — `coarseRecoveryReason == nil` guard (lines 302-306); field declared in ProtectedDomainBootstrapStore.swift:6
sites.precondition: The bootstrap.plist `coarseRecoveryReason: String?` field (shared by all 5 ProtectedData domain stores) is non-nil for the "contacts" domain.
sites.producer: None. Every write site in the codebase — KeyMetadataDomainStore, ProtectedSettingsStore, PrivateKeyControlStore, ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore, and ContactsDomainStore — passes the literal nil for this field, and has done so since the field's introduction in the very first ProtectedData commit (302ed3c, "feat: add app data phase 1 foundation"). `git log --all -S"coarseRecoveryReason" -p` across the whole history shows only ever `coarseRecoveryReason: nil` at every call site; no commit has ever constructed a non-nil value.
sites.evidence: Full-history grep confirms zero non-nil writes ever; grep for "recovery reason" across Tests/ and docs/ returns zero hits — the field, and this specific guard, have no test coverage at all. It reads as a speculative "record a coarse machine-readable recovery reason for future diagnostics" field declared at framework inception and never wired up on the write side by any subsequent feature (private-key rewrap recovery, SE custody recovery, etc. all use other mechanisms — ProtectedDataCommittedDomainState.recoveryNeeded, KeyMigrationCoordinator — instead).

sites.precondition: Bootstrap.plist's `wrappedDomainMasterKeyRecordVersion` (for domain "contacts") differs from the live `WrappedDomainMasterKeyRecord.currentFormatVersion` constant (currently 2).
sites.producer: current-code — every one of the 5 domain stores (including ContactsDomainStore itself) always writes `WrappedDomainMasterKeyRecord.currentFormatVersion` (the live symbol, not a frozen literal), so no code path today can produce a mismatched value. The one real historical bump of this constant (1→2, commit 9df380a, "give the wrapped domain master key record modern envelope discipline") shipped with the explicit note "Pre-release: no migration; existing dev/test installs may be reset" — the project's own policy for this field is reset-on-mismatch, never migrate.
sites.evidence: This check is redundant with WrappedDomainMasterKeyRecordCodec.decode()'s own formatVersion==currentFormatVersion validateContract(), which already runs moments later in the same openAuthoritativeDatabase() call against the authoritative Keychain-stored record — this bootstrap-metadata mirror field can only ever lag or match that authoritative check, never override it. It is also the only one of the 5 stores that reads this field back at all (the other 4 write it via bootstrapStore.saveMetadata but never call bootstrapStore.loadMetadata). No test exercises a genuine mismatch for this field (only ProtectedDataStorageRootTests.swift:96-113 writes the struct with a literal 1 in an unrelated file-protection test on the "settings" domain, which never reaches this guard). Leaning vestigial given the project's confirmed no-migration precedent and full redundancy with the codec-level check, but left as unknown since it does add a second, independent check against a non-AEAD-bound sidecar plist.

sites.symbol: ProtectedSettingsStore.writePayloadGeneration() bootstrapStore.saveMetadata(...) call (lines 499-507); same shape in PrivateKeyControlStore.swift (~508-516) and ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore.swift (230-238)
sites.precondition: n/a — not a guard branch; this is a write with no reader. Question: is ProtectedDomainBootstrapMetadata (schemaVersion / expectedCurrentGenerationIdentifier / coarseRecoveryReason / wrappedDomainMasterKeyRecordVersion) ever read back for the "protected-settings", "private-key-control", or "protected-framework-sentinel" domains?
sites.producer: current-code — each store calls bootstrapStore.saveMetadata(...) on every successful write (every settings change, every rewrap, every sentinel bootstrap).
sites.evidence: Repo-wide grep shows bootstrapStore.loadMetadata( is called from exactly two places in all of Sources/: KeyMetadataDomainStore (for its own domain, to find the single authoritative generation) and ContactsDomainStore (for the 3 guards above). ProtectedSettingsStore, PrivateKeyControlStore, and ProtectedDataFrameworkSentinelStore each only call saveMetadata/removeMetadata and never loadMetadata anywhere in the file; their own generation-authority selection instead scans all 3 envelope slots (current/previous/pending) and takes the max readable generationIdentifier, never consulting the bootstrap sidecar's expectedCurrentGenerationIdentifier. bootstrapMetadataURL(for:) is likewise referenced only inside ProtectedDomainBootstrapStore itself — no other code even checks the file's existence. So for 3 of 5 domains the entire per-domain bootstrap.plist sidecar (file-protected, written and removed on every mutation) is produced but never consumed by any code path. Included because it directly concerns the same schemaVersion/wrappedDomainMasterKeyRecordVersion fields the audit targets, at the granularity of "the whole record" rather than a specific field guard.

sites.precondition: A decoded CAPKEV1 envelope's magic/formatVersion/algorithmID/aadVersion do not exactly equal the single current constant values (formatVersion has been 1 since introduction; never bumped).
sites.producer: current-code only — PrivateKeyEnvelopeCodec.seal() is the sole producer and always writes the current constants.
sites.evidence: Classified live, not vestigial, despite having only ever had one value in this app's history: this struct's fields are folded into the HKDF sharedInfo / AES-GCM AAD as fixed constants (not the on-disk field values), so validateContract() is the only structural check that a tampered or corrupted on-disk formatVersion/magic/algorithmID field is caught before any cryptographic operation proceeds. This is the documented, deliberate "envelope discipline" pattern applied uniformly across every SECURITY-CRITICAL envelope in Sources/Security/ProtectedData/ and is exactly the kind of check the AEAD-hard-fail hard constraint requires; it is tamper/corruption defense-in-depth that runs on every load, not migration scaffolding. Included to show the pattern was evaluated and deliberately excluded from the vestigial findings above.

sites.precondition: ProtectedDataRegistry.plist's formatVersion field differs from currentFormatVersion (1, never bumped).
sites.producer: current-code only — ProtectedDataRegistry.emptySteadyState() is the sole producer and always writes currentFormatVersion.
sites.evidence: Unlike the AEAD-sealed envelopes, ProtectedDataRegistry.plist is written via a plain PropertyListEncoder with no AES-GCM sealing (ProtectedDataRegistryStore calls storageRoot.writeProtectedData directly on the encoded plist, no seal/AAD step) — file-protection class is its only other integrity mechanism. This formatVersion guard is therefore the sole structural defense against a corrupted or malformed registry file before the security-critical recovery-disposition state machine runs on it. Included for contrast with the vestigial findings above — not obsolete, it gates on genuine runtime data-integrity risk for a non-AEAD sidecar file.

sites.precondition: Compares KeyBundleState of the .permanent vs .pending Keychain-envelope rows for a fingerprint during auth-mode rewrap / modify-expiry recovery.
sites.producer: Current code: PrivateKeyRewrapWorkflow / KeyMutationService write the pending row mid-rewrap and can be interrupted (app killed) between writing pending and promoting/deleting it, which is exactly what this coordinator recovers from on next launch.
sites.evidence: Included for contrast with the ContactsDomainStore findings above: this looks superficially like legacy/migration code (types are literally named KeyMigrationCoordinator/KeyMigrationRecoveryOutcome) but it gates on a runtime crash-interruption window that can happen on a completely fresh install, not on any prior shipped version's on-disk state, so it is live and should not be removed. Aside, not itself a separate finding: the .partial KeyBundleState branches inside recoveryAction's switch are unreachable dead code since KeyBundleStore.bundleState only ever returns .missing/.complete by its own doc comment — but per task scope that is ordinary dead-code, not a reachable-but-obsolete legacy branch.

sites.evidence: Despite the case name .migrationOrRecoveryRequired, this gates on an in-app crash/interruption window during Device-Bound Post-Quantum key generation, not on any prior app version's data. Included only for completeness/contrast.

staleFacts: Section 3 'Family Test Matrix' table (lines 210-224) only has three columns — Profile A, Profile B, Post-Quantum. CLAUDE.md and docs/PRD.md Section 3 now document nine key families (Portable Legacy, Portable Modern, Portable Modern · High, Portable Post-Quantum, Portable Post-Quantum · High, Device-Bound Legacy, Device-Bound Modern, Device-Bound Post-Quantum, Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High), completed via the #591 campaign (PR #603, merged 2026-07-06 per repo memory) — one day after this doc's 'Last reviewed: 2026-07-05' stamp. The matrix has no column for the baseline 'Portable Modern' family (Ed25519+X25519 v6, distinct from Profile B/Ed448+X448) and does not split 'Post-Quantum' into base (ML-DSA-65/ML-KEM-768) vs · High (ML-DSA-87/ML-KEM-1024) tiers, even though both now have dedicated Rust test suites (portable_pq_high_key_lifecycle_tests.rs, composite_custody_high_tests.rs).

Section 5 (line 239) reads 'Interop applies to Profile A (software v4) and the Device-Bound Compatible (v4) custody family' — 'Device-Bound Compatible' is the pre-rename technical/internal name (still used in Swift string keys like keyFamily.deviceBoundCompatible.name); the current product-facing name per CLAUDE.md/docs/PRD.md is 'Device-Bound Legacy'. This is part of the already-tracked-but-deferred repo-wide 'Profile A/B naming retirement' cleanup (maintainer decided 2026-07-06 it is a separate future PR, not urgent), so it is a known, currently-accepted staleness rather than a new regression, but it is still a stale/renamed-type fact in this doc's prose.

Section 4.10 Self-Test: "One-tap diagnostic: key generation, encrypt/decrypt, sign/verify, tamper, and key export/import round-trip run for each software profile (Profile A, Profile B, Post-Quantum), plus one profile-agnostic QR round-trip." This undercounts what the shipped self-test actually exercises. `Sources/Services/SelfTestService.swift` runs `for profile in PGPKeyProfile.allCases`, and `Sources/Models/PGPKeyProfile.swift` now declares 5 cases (`universal`=Legacy, `modern`=baseline Modern, `advanced`=Profile B/Modern·High, `postQuantum`, `postQuantumHigh`). The PRD text lists only 3 of the 5 software profiles the self-test covers, omitting baseline 'Portable Modern' and 'Portable Post-Quantum · High' -- a leftover from before the nine-families expansion (issue #591) added the two missing profiles.

Top-of-file positioning line: "CypherAir X is the enhanced edition of CypherAir -- it adds the three Device-Bound (Secure Enclave) families and Portable Post-Quantum on top of CypherAir's two portable families." This describes a two-product split (a base 'CypherAir' plus a paid 'CypherAir X' add-on) that no longer matches the repo: there is exactly one Xcode app target (`PRODUCT_BUNDLE_IDENTIFIER = com.chentianren.cypherair`), one display name fallback `"CypherAir X"` (`Sources/App/Common/AppProductIdentity.swift`), and `docs/PRD.md` §3 now describes a single unified key-generation surface offering all nine families in the one app -- no base-tier/enhanced-tier product boundary exists in code or in the current PRD.

App Review notes: "What is new relative to CypherAir: - Four additional key families on top of CypherAir's two, for six total." -- stale count. `docs/PRD.md` §3 and the shipped `PGPKeyFamily`/`PGPKeyProfile` types define nine key families, not six; this doc's family list (lines 42-45, and the zh-Hans mirror at lines 117-120) never mentions 'Portable Modern · High', 'Portable Post-Quantum · High', or 'Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High' at all.

Description bullets: "• Device-Bound Compatible -- P-256 keys held in the Secure Enclave" and "Beyond the Portable Compatible and Portable Modern key families of CypherAir..." -- renamed-type reference. Current code presents these families to the user as 'Portable Legacy' / 'Device-Bound Legacy' (`Sources/App/Common/PGPKeyFamily+Presentation.swift`, e.g. `defaultValue: "Device-Bound Legacy"`), matching `docs/PRD.md`'s 'Portable Legacy'/'Device-Bound Legacy' vocabulary; the internal localization keys still say `deviceBoundCompatible` but the user-facing name has moved on from 'Compatible'.

Screenshot checklist: "iPhone 6.9\" set -- Home, Encrypt, key generation (six families), About." -- stale count; the shipped key-generation list has nine families, not six, so a screenshot depicting the full list would no longer match this caption.

"What's New -- 1.5.0 template" (en-US and zh-Hans) only lists the original post-quantum + Secure Enclave custody launch feature set and the welcome-tour/settings redesign; the project is now at MARKETING_VERSION 1.5.2 (CURRENT_PROJECT_VERSION 15200) after the nine-families expansion (issue #591, PRs #601-#603), and this template makes no mention of the three added '· High' families.
staleRequirements: Description bullet "GnuPG-compatible: exchange messages with any standards-compliant PGP software" is stated unqualified under "EVERYTHING YOU NEED FOR OPENPGP", but per `docs/PRD.md` §3.2 seven of the current nine families (all RFC 9580/9980 v6 configurations) are explicitly 'Not compatible with GnuPG' -- only the two Legacy (v4) families interoperate with GnuPG, so the blanket claim overstates current behavior.

Row 'Secure Enclave composite (post-quantum) private-key blob rows': "Keychain `kSecClassGenericPassword` rows, service `com.cypherair.v1.secure-enclave-composite.<role>` + account `<random-handle-set-id>`; two rows per Device-Bound Post-Quantum identity holding the CryptoKit Secure Enclave ML-DSA-65 / ML-KEM-768 `dataRepresentation` blob... user-reachable on Secure Enclave hardware via Device-Bound Post-Quantum generation." This describes only the base tier and is now incomplete: `Sources/Security/SecureEnclaveCompositeHandle.swift` defines a `tier.serviceNamespaceSuffix` appended to the service string specifically 'so the two tiers never collide' for the ML-DSA-87/ML-KEM-1024 'Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High' tier added by the nine-families expansion -- a second Keychain service namespace this row never mentions, and the row's 'Current status' text doesn't list Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High as user-reachable even though it is.

Row 'Composite classical-component envelope row': "...one `CAPKEV1` envelope per Device-Bound Post-Quantum key sealing the concatenated 32-byte Ed25519 + 32-byte X25519 classical component secrets under a fixed-access (`privateKeyUsage`+`biometryAny`) Secure Enclave wrapping key." Incomplete: `pgp-mobile/src/composite_classical.rs` documents a second, structurally different classical-component tier for '· High' -- a 57-byte Ed448 secret + a 56-byte X448 secret (`X448_SECRET_LENGTH: usize = 56`), per `Sources/Security/SecureEnclaveCompositeClassicalComponentStore.swift`'s own comment ('a 32-byte Ed25519 + 32-byte X25519 pair for `.postQuantum`, or a 57-byte Ed448 + 56-byte X448 pair for [the High tier]') -- that this inventory row's byte-length/algorithm description never accounts for.

bloat: Tech Stack > 'Key families' bullet (line 12) inlines the full nine-family enumeration with per-family algorithm/format detail. docs/WORKFLOW.md §4 classifies CLAUDE.md as an 'entry' doc that should orient and point to canon rather than restate it — docs/PRD.md §3 (and docs/TDD.md §1) already carry this exact list as the canonical home. This is the same duplication pattern that let AGENTS.md and README.md's copies of the family list drift stale (still six families / old 'Compatible' naming) after the nine-family migration (issue #591) shipped — a second inline copy in CLAUDE.md is one more place that can silently fall out of sync the next time a family is added or renamed.
