touchpoints.why: Encryptor::for_recipients auto-selects SEIPDv1 vs SEIPDv2 by recipient key version (documented as audit finding M1); comment states packet-level tests in pgp-mobile/tests/cross_profile_tests.rs are the only verification of this invariant.

setup_signer/setup_external_*_signer map Sequoia builder errors and external-signer errors into PgpError; four near-duplicate encrypt_with_external_*_signer functions must stay in sync on error-mapping behavior.

decrypt_with_helper zeroizes partial plaintext on any read_to_end error before propagating; classify_decrypt_error is a hybrid structured-downcast + string-matching classifier explicitly documented as needed because the crypto-openssl backend surfaces AEAD tag failures as opaque openssl::error::ErrorStack, not openpgp::Error::ManipulatedMessage.

DecryptHelper::check() always returns Ok(()) even for bad signatures (graded-result design, deliberately different from verify.rs's hard-fail); is_expired_error() is shared logic used to distinguish expired-signer from tampered.

message_quantum_safety()/parse_recipients()/match_recipients() all walk PKESK packets and stop at the first SEIP container, classifying by PKESK public-key algorithm; a parsing bug here misreports which format/algorithm a message actually used.
plaintext.zeroize() is called explicitly on the AEAD/MDC failure path in decrypt_with_helper, the literal enforcement point of the 'no partial plaintext' hard constraint for in-memory decryption.

Central invariant file: writes to a randomly-suffixed .tmp file and only fs::rename()s to the final path after full decrypt+auth success; every error path (AEAD, MDC, cancel, I/O) calls secure_delete_file first. This is the file-based analog of the AEAD hard-fail rule.

zeroing_copy() deliberately avoids std::io::copy/BufReader/BufWriter because their internal buffers are not zeroized; uses Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> for every intermediate buffer instead — foundational to the project's memory-zeroize invariant for file I/O.
openpgp::crypto::random() generates an 8-byte hex suffix for the temp output path (documented as fix for predictable-temp-path finding M3); a weak/predictable RNG here reintroduces that finding.

StreamingProgressReporter is a #[uniffi::export(with_foreign)] Swift-implemented callback; cancellation must surface as the non-retryable StreamingCancelled marker error rather than io::ErrorKind::Interrupted, which buffered-reader would silently retry.

verify_detached_file_detailed mirrors verify.rs's parse-time hard-fail vs per-signature grading split, but over a streamed reader with its own error classification (classify_detached_verify_reader_error) that must not let a cancellation or I/O failure collapse into a 'Bad' signature grade.

Explicit top-of-file comment disclaiming any zeroize call because Sequoia's KeyPair type is trusted to manage secret lifecycle internally — a trust assumption on an upstream Drop impl rather than a locally-enforced guarantee.

select_external_signing_key() pins the external (SE-backed) signer to a caller-supplied fingerprint and filters .revoked(false) — the gate that decides which Device-Bound key the external signer is allowed to represent.

extract_signing_keypair/select_external_signing_key feed both in-memory and streaming/cert_signature signing paths; map_signing_error funnels errors through the shared map_external_signing_error dispatcher.

verify_cleartext_detailed hard-fails (returns Invalid/Expired with empty signatures) on parser setup failure, contrasting with decrypt.rs's always-succeed grading — this asymmetry is the crux of the dual-model design and must be preserved deliberately, not accidentally unified.
PasswordMessageFormat::Seipdv1/Seipdv2 is caller-selected (not auto-detected like encrypt.rs); collect_message_context validates SEIP::V2 symmetric/AEAD algorithm support before proceeding.

decrypt() tries every SKESK candidate and threads AeadAuthenticationFailed/IntegrityCheckFailed/UnsupportedAlgorithm through as deferred hard errors distinct from a benign 'wrong password' (PasswordRejected) — getting this classification wrong could mask a tampered message as a bad password.

derive_candidate()/SKESK::decrypt(password) is the password-message KDF path (Argon2id for v6 SKESK, Iterated+Salted for v4); validate_skesk gates unsupported algorithms before key derivation is attempted.

PasswordDecryptResult.plaintext and the derived SessionKey cross/exist at the FFI boundary; doc comment states the Swift caller must zeroize the returned plaintext — an unenforced cross-boundary contract.

verify_direct_key_signature/verify_user_id_binding_signature_by_selector implement issuer-guided-then-fallback verification with three outcomes (Valid/Invalid/SignerMissing) that are crypto-only (do not factor in signer revocation/expiry) — a distinct verification model from verify.rs/decrypt.rs worth checking for consistency.

generate_user_id_certification_by_selector_with_external_*_signer routes certification signing through the same external-signer custody boundary as message signing, with its own revocation/primary-key checks (ensure_external_certification_certificate_not_revoked, select_external_certification_primary_signing_key).

This is the file implementing the memory-noted 'dual status/verificationState' debt: DetailedSignatureStatus vs SignatureVerificationState are two parallel enums, and SummaryFoldMode::VerifyLike (freeze on first conclusive) vs DecryptLike (follow until valid wins) encodes the whole security-messaging asymmetry between verify and decrypt flows.
decode_armor uses armor::ReaderMode::Tolerant(None), auto-detecting binary vs ASCII-armored input; a detection bug could misinterpret binary ciphertext bytes as armor or vice versa.
Generates/derives the classical (non-Secure-Enclave) halves — Ed25519/X25519/Ed448/X448 — of every split-custody composite key family; this is the software side of the split-custody trust boundary.

generate_ed25519_component/generate_x25519_component/generate_ed448_component/generate_x448_component call OpenSSL's PKey::generate_* (OpenSSL RNG); all-zero-secret/all-zero-shared-secret checks are a sanity backstop, not an RNG-quality guarantee.
Every generated or derived secret (component secrets, X25519/X448 shared secrets) is wrapped in Zeroizing<Vec<u8>>/[u8;N]-then-Zeroizing at the return boundary.

multi_key_combine is the RFC 9980 §4.2.1 KEM key-combiner (SHA3-256 over mlkem||ecdh shares + ciphertext + pubkey + algid + domain-separation string) producing the AES-256 KEK — a hand-ported construction that must byte-match Sequoia's private implementation exactly, including the 'OpenPGPCompositeKDFv1\x15' domain string.

unwrap_session_key performs RFC 3394 AES key unwrap; a wrap/unwrap failure must propagate as a hard error rather than silently yielding a wrong session key that later produces a misleading AEAD failure downstream.

File header explicitly documents this as a vendored copy of a pub(crate)-only upstream Sequoia function pending export via gitlab.com/sequoia-pgp/sequoia issue #1249 — a duplication-drift risk to re-diff on every Sequoia bump, not a literal FFI boundary but an external-API-shape dependency.

Defines the entire custody type/trait surface: GeneratedKey and SecureEnclaveCompositeGeneratedCertificate doc comments spell out the SE-wrap-then-zeroize contract, and all six #[uniffi::export(with_foreign)] provider traits (ExternalP256Signing/KeyAgreement, ExternalMlDsa65/87Signing, ExternalMlKem768/1024Decapsulation) are the custody boundary itself.

Two `.expect("active binding signatures/revocation signatures always have a creation time")` calls in current_user_id_occurrence_state (via make_user_id_candidate's max_by_key) are panics reachable from discover_certificate_selectors on FFI-supplied certificate bytes if the preceding filter's invariant is ever loosened.

Doc comments on GeneratedKey/SecureEnclaveCompositeGeneratedCertificate specify a caller-side (Swift) zeroize contract for cert_data/revocation_cert/classical_eddsa_secret/classical_ecdh_secret that Rust cannot enforce past the FFI boundary.

KeyProfile::Universal explicitly strips SEIPDv2 from the Features subpacket (forcing SEIPDv1-only advertisement for GnuPG compatibility) while Advanced/Modern/PostQuantum*/RFC9580 profiles leave SEIPDv2 default-advertised — this is the generation-time half of the format-selection contract that encrypt.rs's auto-selection depends on.
cert_data is built as Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> and moved out via std::mem::take to avoid a non-zeroized copy escaping into the returned GeneratedKey.
CertBuilder::generate() is Sequoia's RNG-backed key generation for every software (non-SE) key family; this is the point where weak system randomness would surface.

parse_s2k_params extracts Argon2id t/p/m and computes memory_kib = 2^m — the pre-import memory-budget gate that prevents iOS Jetsam kills; also selects the highest-memory S2K across primary+all subkeys, which must stay correct as new key families are added.

encrypt_key_argon2id hardcodes the authoritative Argon2id export parameters (t=3, p=4, m=19 → 512 MiB) for every v6 profile; this is the single source of truth that must match docs/SECURITY.md's stated parameters.
Generates a 16-byte salt via openpgp::crypto::random() for each Argon2id export — salt reuse or weak randomness here would undermine the whole passphrase-protection scheme.

Both export and import comments explicitly trust Sequoia's SecretKeyMaterial Drop impl to zeroize on error paths, and wrap the import output in Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> — worth re-verifying this trust holds with the crypto-openssl backend specifically.

export_secret_key/import_secret_key is the passphrase-based (non-SE) software-custody path; profile-vs-key-shape mismatch is explicitly checked against classify_profile() to prevent exporting under the wrong S2K/profile assumption.

classify_profile() determines a certificate's family purely from primary-key algorithm (not just version), since v6 spans Modern/Advanced/PostQuantum/PostQuantumHigh — every downstream format/feature decision (export S2K choice, UI display) depends on this classification being exhaustive and correct as new algorithms are added.

has_encryption_subkey explicitly mirrors encrypt.rs's .revoked(false) recipient filter (WCR-01 consistency); a UI/business-logic surface (is_revoked/is_expired/has_encryption_subkey) that must stay in lockstep with the actual crypto-path selection logic or users get a misleading trust signal.

validate_public_certificate/merge_public_certificate_update both reject cert.is_tsk() input — the guard preventing a secret key from being accidentally imported or merged as a public-only 'contact' certificate.

modify_expiry_with_external_*_signer operates on Device-Bound public-only certs; ensure_external_expiry_signer_is_primary and ensure_external_expiry_subkeys_do_not_require_subkey_signers gate what an external-signer caller can re-date without SE-held subkey material.
modify_expiry (secret-cert path) wraps the re-serialized full certificate in Zeroizing<Vec<u8>>; the primary KeyPair extracted via into_keypair() relies on Sequoia's Protected<> zeroize-on-drop.

Four modify_expiry_with_external_*_signer variants (P-256, composite, composite-high) share the generic modify_expiry_with_signer core but each has its own hash-algorithm-forcing strategy and error mapping — a place where the four variants could silently drift.

generate_*_revocation_with_external_*_signer functions are the only way to revoke a Device-Bound key's subkey/User ID; select_external_revocation_primary_signing_key's fingerprint-pinning-to-primary check is the integrity control preventing a non-primary/wrong key from producing a trusted revocation.

Builds Device-Bound Legacy/Modern (P-256) public certificates entirely through the external signer callback (no secret material ever touches Rust); validate_public_key_point performs actual on-curve validation of SE-supplied points, guarding against malformed/invalid-curve injection at generation time.
user_id_builder sets Features::seipdv1() for V4 vs Features::seipdv2() for V6 Secure Enclave custody certs — must stay aligned with encrypt.rs's recipient-driven format auto-selection.

Builds Device-Bound Post-Quantum(·High) split-custody certificates; classical component secrets are generated in Rust (not the SE) and returned to the caller for enveloping — this file is where the split-custody trust boundary is drawn. validate_mldsa65/87_public_key and validate_mlkem768/1024_public_key (including the FIPS 203 canonical-coefficient modulus check) gate malformed SE-supplied public keys.

assemble_composite_public_certificate always sets Features::seipdv2() (v6-only, no v4 fallback) for every composite tier — worth confirming this is the intended permanent policy as new tiers are added.

ExternalP256Signer is the Rust-side bridge for Device-Bound Legacy/Modern signing; acceptable_hashes() pins to SHA-256 only, preventing a hash-algorithm downgrade/substitution in the signing pipeline.

validate_response() self-verifies the externally-produced ECDSA signature against the digest and public key before ever returning it to the caller — the only check that a malicious or buggy external (SE) provider cannot forge a signature that doesn't actually verify.

map_external_signing_error is the single funnel that downcasts both ExternalP256SignerError and ExternalCompositeSignerError out of a shared generic anyhow error chain — used by every signing pipeline (message, streaming, cleartext, certification, revocation, expiry); a missed downcast here degrades a typed cancellation/failure category into an opaque string everywhere.
signer_for_provider bridges the Arc<dyn ExternalP256SigningProvider> UniFFI callback into the internal ExternalP256Signer, hardcoding SHA-256-only acceptance at the seam.

ExternalDecryptHelper bridges Device-Bound Legacy/Modern decryption to the SE via the ExternalP256KeyAgreementProvider callback; select_external_p256_key_agreement_key enforces exactly one matching, validated ECDH subkey.

is_external_operation_failure() distinguishes a pre-callback 'wrong recipient, skip and try next PKESK' rejection from a genuine external-operation failure that must hard-abort — an inverted condition here would either wrongly abort a decryptable multi-recipient message or silently swallow a real SE failure.

ExternalP256SharedSecret wraps the raw SE-returned shared secret in Zeroizing<Vec<u8>>, then converts to Protected for ecdh::decrypt_unwrap; validate_shared_secret rejects wrong-length or all-zero responses before use.

CompositeSigner::new() (via core.rs) enforces that the caller-supplied classical secret's derived public key matches the EdDSA half actually bound in the certificate — the check that prevents an envelope mix-up (wrong classical secret paired with the wrong SE key) for split-custody signing.

validate_response() self-verifies the assembled RFC 9980 composite signature (AND over EdDSA+ML-DSA halves) against the public key before release — this is the only verification that the ML-DSA half returned by the external (SE) provider is genuine.
classical_eddsa_secret is stored as Protected for the signer's lifetime rather than a plain Vec<u8>.

ExternalCompositeSignerError variants (including ClassicalComponentFailure, distinct from ExternalFailure) must be exhaustively handled by external_signer/error.rs's shared map_external_signing_error dispatcher.

ExternalCompositeDecryptHelper/ExternalCompositeHighDecryptHelper bridge Device-Bound Post-Quantum(·High) decryption to the SE via ML-KEM decapsulation callbacks; select_external_composite_key_agreement_key enforces exactly one matching, validated composite subkey.

Same explicit-vs-wildcard-recipient fail-closed logic as external_decryptor.rs (is_external_operation_failure), duplicated across two tiers (65/768 and 87/1024) — a place the two copies could drift out of sync.

CompositeDecryptor::new() enforces classical-secret-to-certificate ECDH-half binding match before any SE decapsulation is attempted — the decrypt-side analog of the signer's envelope-mixup protection.

`T::cert_ecdh_public_key(&public_key).expect("validated above")` in CompositeDecryptor::new relies on T::validate_public_key having already succeeded earlier in the same function; if that ordering is ever changed this becomes a live panic reachable from Swift-supplied certificate/PKESK bytes.

decrypt() deliberately runs the classical (X25519/X448) shared-secret derivation before the external ML-KEM decapsulation call ('cheap, deterministic, fail first'), then feeds both into the KEM combiner/AES unwrap; combiner/unwrap errors are returned raw to match the native Sequoia fail-closed 'no key to decrypt' path — any reordering or swallowed error here risks accepting a partially-validated session key.

Explicit design note (audit finding H1): there is intentionally NO blanket From<anyhow::Error> for PgpError impl, specifically to prevent the ? operator from silently misclassifying AEAD/MDC/wrong-key errors as generic InternalError — every Sequoia error must be routed through an explicit classify function.

PgpError is the 1:1 enum mapped to Swift's generated CypherAirError; every new variant added here must be matched on the Swift adapter side (per CLAUDE.md's Services/FFI/ boundary normalization rule).

The entire #[uniffi::export] PgpEngine surface and uniffi::setup_scaffolding!() call — every Arc<dyn External*Provider> callback and every raw byte buffer from Swift enters the crate through this single file.

This is the first point where secret byte buffers arriving from Swift (signing_key, secret_keys, classical_eddsa_secret, classical_ecdh_secret, cert_data, password) get wrapped in Zeroizing<>/Password::from(); a new FFI method that forgets this wrap is a real, easily-missed gap since nothing downstream re-checks it.

Defines the paired entry points for every custody family (software key-material vs public-cert-plus-external-signer variants for P-256, ML-DSA-65/composite, and ML-DSA-87/composite-High) — the natural place to check that no custody family is missing an operation (encrypt/decrypt/sign/verify/revoke/expiry) that its siblings have.

Orchestrates text/file encryption and encrypt-to-self; doc comment states message format (SEIPDv1 vs SEIPDv2) is auto-selected by recipient key version in the Rust engine. Confirm the mixed-recipient fallback rule (all-v4→SEIPDv1, all-v6→SEIPDv2, mixed→SEIPDv1) is actually enforced downstream and never overridden by caller input.

Two-phase decrypt is explicitly marked SECURITY-CRITICAL: Phase 1 (parseRecipients) must never access the private key, Phase 2 must only run after phase 1 identified the key, and the unwrapped private key must exist in memory only for the decrypt call. Verify no code path can skip Phase 1 or leak the phase1 ciphertext/key across calls, and that AEAD failures from the engine propagate as hard aborts (no partial plaintext returned).

Owns cleartext/detached signature creation and verification, funneling into DetailedSignatureVerification; verify the known dual status/verificationState debt (per memory: SignatureVerification has both status and verificationState, and empty signatures ≠ notSigned) doesn't cause a forged/absent signature to read as verified in this call path.

Password-based encrypt/decrypt is explicitly documented as bypassing SE unwrapping and PKESK matching; format is caller-selected (PasswordMessageEnvelopeFormat seipdv1/v2) rather than auto-selected by recipient key version — confirm this divergence from the recipient-key auto-selection rule is intentional and doesn't let a caller pick SEIPDv2 output for a context expecting v4 compatibility. Decrypt result has explicit noSkesk/passwordRejected/decrypted states worth checking for hard-fail semantics on auth failure.

Verifies direct-key and User-ID-binding certificate signatures and builds certification artifacts gated on `verification.status == .valid`; confirm canonicalSignatureData (dearmor-or-passthrough) can't be abused to smuggle a different signature than what was verified into the persisted artifact.

The single largest FFI seam: every encrypt/decrypt/sign/verify/password path (including P-256 and ML-DSA/ML-KEM external-provider variants) crosses into Rust here via `@concurrent` static funcs and PgpEngine calls, with PGPErrorMapper normalizing thrown PgpError. Check every call site maps AEAD/bad-signature/corrupt-data errors to hard failures (no plaintext returned on catch), and that Data/callback ownership across the UniFFI boundary (progress bridges, external signer/decapsulation providers) can't panic or leak partial state.

Maps raw FFI SignatureVerificationState/DetailedSignatureStatus into the app's SignatureVerification.VerificationState, including the contactsContextUnavailable special-casing of signerCertificateUnavailable. This is exactly the surface where the known dual status/verificationState debt and 'empty signatures ≠ notSigned' issue live (per project memory) — verify every FFI state has a corresponding, non-ambiguous app state.

Central PgpError→CypherAirError normalization boundary for the whole app; `.AeadAuthenticationFailed` must always become `.aeadAuthenticationFailed` and never get swallowed by a generic fallback in any of the six mapping variants (mapRecipientMatching, mapExternalP256Signing/KeyAgreement, mapExternalCompositeSigning/KeyAgreement).

UniFFI callback invoked by Rust to perform SE-resident P-256 signing; verify SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleError→ExternalP256SigningFailureCategory mapping can't collapse a role-mismatch or binding-mismatch into a generic 'externalOperationFailed' that hides a real custody bug.

Bridges the SE P-256 ECDH shared-secret across the UniFFI boundary; the code explicitly zeroizes the SecureEnclaveP256RawSharedSecret and the raw copy via `defer`, with a comment noting Rust immediately re-wraps the received Vec in Zeroizing. Confirm no earlier copy (e.g. inside SecKeyCopyKeyExchangeResult's CFData bridging) survives past the defer.

Device-Bound Post-Quantum split-custody signing callback: only the pure ML-DSA-65 signature is produced in the enclave, with Ed25519 half and composite assembly in Rust. Verify failure-category mapping doesn't misclassify a classicalComponentFailed as externalOperationFailed in a way that hides which half of the composite signature failed.
Same as the ML-DSA-65 bridge but for the · High tier (ML-DSA-87 + Ed448); same category-mapping concern applies.

Decapsulates ML-KEM-768 in the enclave and zeroizes the resulting 32-byte key share via `defer` before copying into the FFI-owned Data; confirm the copy-then-zero ordering can't leave an un-zeroized duplicate if `Data(buffer)` triggers copy-on-write before the defer fires.
Same pattern as the ML-KEM-768 bridge but for the · High tier (ML-KEM-1024).

Generate/import/modifyExpiry all use a `shouldZeroizeSecret` flag pattern with `defer` to zeroize secret cert data on every early-return/throw path but skip zeroizing on the success path (ownership transfers to caller). Verify every one of the four call sites (generate, import, modifyExpiry, and the P256/composite modifyExpiry variants) correctly flips the flag before returning, since a missed flip either double-zeroizes valid returned key material or leaks it on an unanticipated throw.

Central certificate signature verification (direct-key + User-ID binding) and certification/revocation generation across P-256, ML-DSA composite, and composite-High signer paths; confirm verifyDirectKeySignature/verifyUserIdBindingSignature can't be satisfied by a candidate signer key that isn't actually a trusted contact/own key (candidateSigners is caller-supplied).

Owns selector discovery/validation (subkey, User ID) used to gate certificate operations before signing/verification; `validatedCatalog` enforces the stored metadata fingerprint matches the discovered certificate fingerprint — a bypass here would let a stale/substituted certificate pass selector validation.

Enforces 'contact import requires public certificate only' via a magic reason-token string match (`publicOnlyReasonToken`) rather than a typed error case; if the Rust-side reason string ever changes, secret key material could silently import as a contact instead of being rejected.

One-tap self-test generates and exports real key material (`PGPSelfTestGeneratedKey.zeroizeSensitiveMaterial()`); confirm every self-test call site actually invokes zeroize before the struct goes out of scope, since this is diagnostic code that's easy to skip review on.

Builds the Device-Bound (Legacy/Modern) self-certificate from SE-resident P-256 handles via an external signer callback; the exhaustive switch over `configuration.identity` throws for any non-P256 configuration — verify this stays exhaustive as new key families are added (per the profile-AB retirement / nine-families work already in flight).

Parses the public certificate to recover the SE signing/key-agreement subkey fingerprints and X9.63 public keys used to locate the matching Keychain handle; a parsing bug here would misroute private operations to the wrong SE handle.

Returns freshly generated classical (Ed25519/X25519 or Ed448/X448) component secrets in `PGPSecureEnclaveCompositeGeneratedMaterial` with an explicit comment that the caller must seal and zeroize them; verify every caller (SecureEnclaveCustodyGenerationService) does so on all paths including rollback.

Same binding-inspection role as the P-256 inspector but for Device-Bound Post-Quantum (both tiers); a tier-mismatch here (e.g. treating a ·High key's bindings as the base tier) would misroute to the wrong enclave handle store.

The single chokepoint that routes every private-key operation (sign/decrypt/keyAgreement) to software-secret, SE P-256, or SE composite handling, including the one-fresh-biometric-per-operation pattern (`systemBiometricCustodyOperationAuthenticator`) and locate-before-authenticate ordering. This is exactly the kind of central routing logic flagged as the prior WCR-01 signing-side chokepoint fix — verify the P-256 and composite branches stay symmetric as new custody kinds are added.

Defines `endAuthorizedOperation()`, which must zeroize the classical component secret and end the LAContext authorization for composite routes on every exit; a missing call site would leave a raw Ed25519/X25519 (or Ed448/X448) secret un-zeroized after the operation window.

Owns SE reconstruction + software secret-certificate unwrap; explicitly documents that the returned Data must be zeroized by the caller and runs the biometric off the main actor to avoid the auth-lifecycle scene-transition race described in the code comment — worth checking every call site actually zeroizes, and that the off-main hop can't be raced by app backgrounding.

Routes recipient-message decryption through custody: software path unwraps+zeroizes a secret certificate then calls the standard decrypt; SE/composite paths hand only a raw shared secret/key share to Rust. Comment states 'there is no software fallback for a Secure Enclave route' — confirm the .secureEnclaveSigner/.secureEnclaveCompositeSigner branches truly can't be reached for a decrypt operation (role mismatch).

Signing-key dispatch for text encryption across software/SE/composite custody; secretKey is zeroized via `defer` in the software branch only — confirm the SE/composite branches truly never hold decrypted secret bytes in this layer.

Same signing-key routing as the text service but for streaming file encryption; verify the streaming path's progress/cancellation handling can't leave a partially-written output file with signing key material still resident.

Streaming file decrypt via custody routing; the two-phase (parse-then-decrypt) discipline from DecryptionService must hold here too, plus success-only file protection application after decrypt (see DecryptionService's `applyAndVerifyCompleteProtection`) — verify a mid-stream AEAD failure can't leave partial plaintext at the output path.
Signing-key routing for cleartext signatures; same secretKey zeroize-on-defer pattern as the other Private*SigningService files.
Signing-key routing for detached file signatures; same pattern as PrivateKeyCleartextSigningService.

Signing-key routing for password-message encryption (optional signing); confirm the format-selection divergence noted in PasswordMessageService.swift (caller picks SEIPDv1/v2 explicitly) is intentional here too.
Signing-key routing for User-ID certification generation across all custody kinds; a bug here would let a certification be signed with the wrong key or custody kind.

Guards secret-key export to `.softwareSecretCertificate` custody only (SE custody keys can never be exported), unwraps and zeroizes the secret cert, then re-exports with passphrase-based S2K via the FFI adapter — this is the kdf/Argon2id-adjacent export path worth checking against the Argon2idMemoryGuard import-side guard for symmetry.

Owns modify-expiry (unwrap→mutate→rewrap with a pending/permanent two-phase commit) and key deletion including SE handle teardown; the software modifyExpiry path threads a pre-authenticated LAContext through unwrap+rewrap windows and zeroizes secretKey/result.certData via defer — the pending-bundle crash-recovery sequencing here is exactly the kind of multi-step commit protocol worth re-verifying for atomicity.

In-memory PGPKeyIdentity cache backed by KeyMetadataPersistence; `updateExpiry` validates the modified certificate's fingerprint still matches the stored identity before accepting the update — a missing check here would let modifyExpiry silently swap in a different key's metadata.

Generate/import key provisioning; import path invokes `Argon2idMemoryGuard` before touching secret key bytes, and both generate/import zeroize secret cert data via defer. The two-phase commit (`commitIdentity` with bundle-then-identity ordering and rollback) is a crash-consistency-critical sequence.

Tracks active-commit count so relock can wait for in-flight key provisioning to finish before locking the key-metadata domain; a race here could relock mid-commit and corrupt the catalog/bundle pairing.

Generation-counter cancellation gate checked at every step of key generation/import; if `invalidate()` isn't called on every relock/reset path, a stale provisioning operation could commit a key after the domain was supposed to be locked.

Pure policy resolver deciding which (configuration, custody-kind) pairs are valid and which operations each custody kind supports; the exhaustive `isValidConfigurationCustodyPair` switch is the single source of truth preventing e.g. a software-custody key from being routed through SE-only operations — must stay exhaustive as new families are added.

Normalizes SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleError/CypherAirError into the sanitized PGPKeyOperationFailureCategory vocabulary shown to callers; `publicCertificateAssociationCategory` folds several distinct CypherAirError cases into one category — verify this doesn't hide a real fingerprint/binding mismatch behind a generic message.

SE/composite modify-expiry routing that mutates only the public certificate via the external signer (never touches secret key material) — confirm the P-256 vs composite (both tiers) branches route to the correct signer/certificate builder.
SE/composite subkey and User-ID revocation-signature generation; a bug here produces a revocation that either doesn't validate or revokes the wrong subkey/User-ID.

Facade over selective revocation across all custody kinds; explicitly documents selector re-validation against the stored public certificate *before* any SE unwrap/biometric prompt — verify a malformed/stale selector truly fails closed before triggering authentication.

Maps LAError codes to CypherAirError; verify the mapping can't accidentally turn a genuine authentication failure into a benign-looking `.operationCancelled`, which several call sites treat as a silent no-op.

Data model for the SE custody generation health report (handle-only orphans, partial sets, etc.); shape drives what the UI can detect as unrecoverable, so a missing case here is a silent-data-loss risk.

Classifies every SE-custody identity's public-material/revocation/handle availability, deliberately running the handle inventory scan unconditionally (before filtering to SE identities) specifically to catch handle-only orphans left by interrupted generation — verify this ordering is preserved on any future refactor.

Full SE/composite key generation with rollback-on-any-failure (deletes handles, discards committed identity, rolls back the classical-component keychain receipt); the composite path's `defer` zeroizes the raw classical secrets on every exit, and the commit closure deliberately only reads public metadata so secrets stay valid until it returns — this ordering is load-bearing and worth re-verifying on any refactor.

Top-level facade wiring all provisioning/mutation/export/revocation services together and exposing `unwrapPrivateKey` and the private-key operation router factory directly to callers — a natural place to check that every public method that touches secret material documents (and callers honor) the zeroize-after-use contract.

Converts a 32-byte raw domain master key into a SQLCipher hex key-spec string and back; `zeroize` uses `@_optimize(none)` specifically to defeat dead-store elimination of the scrub — worth confirming this technique is actually effective against the current compiler/optimization level and that every caller (ContactsSQLCipherDatabase) zeroizes both the raw key copy and the generated key-spec bytes.

Diagnostic probe that creates/opens a real encrypted SQLCipher database with a throwaway key to assert `SQLITE_HAS_CODEC`/`SQLITE_TEMP_STORE=2` and that a wrong key is rejected; confirm `assertWrongKeyFails`'s catch-and-rethrow logic can't accidentally swallow a genuine 'wrong key accepted' finding.

The explicit Argon2id memory-guard: enforces the RFC 9580 max encoded_m (2^31 KiB) bound with overflow-checked multiplication before the 75%-of-available-memory check, guarding against Jetsam termination during passphrase-protected key import. This is the canonical kdf-parameter touchpoint — verify the 75% threshold and overflow handling against any future S2K parameter changes.

`SensitiveData` auto-zeroizing wrapper (zeroize on deinit and explicit call); the doc comment explicitly warns that `.data` returns an unmanaged copy-on-write copy NOT covered by zeroize — any call site that stores `.data` into a local `Data` var without its own zeroize would leak plaintext, so this pattern's actual usages across the codebase are worth auditing.

Supplies `availableMemoryBytes()` to the Argon2id memory guard; on iOS this wraps the undocumented `os_proc_available_memory()` via `@_silgen_name` — worth confirming this stays behaviorally stable across iOS versions since the guard's correctness depends on it.

Production Keychain implementation (SystemKeychain) using kSecClassGenericPassword + kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly / SecAccessControl; marked SECURITY-CRITICAL. Confirm `save`/`update`/`delete`/`listItems` all consistently set `kSecUseDataProtectionKeychain` and that no code path allows iCloud sync (`kSecAttrSynchronizable`) for private-key items.

Protocol + `KeychainConstants` defining every Keychain service-name namespace (private-key envelope, pending envelope, ProtectedData device-binding key, domain-key records); a naming collision or missing namespace separation here would cross-contaminate unrelated secrets.

Classifies Keychain errors into itemNotFound/duplicateItem/userCancelled/authenticationFailed/interactionNotAllowed/unhandled, feeding decisions like 'treat itemNotFound as benign, everything else as a real failure' throughout custody code — a wrong classification could mask a real Keychain integrity problem as a benign no-op.

HardwareSecureEnclave: production SE wrap/unwrap via ephemeral-static ECDH → HKDF-SHA256 → AES-GCM seal, marked SECURITY-CRITICAL. `unwrap` explicitly fails closed before ECDH if the envelope's bound SE public key doesn't match the handle (`deviceBindingMismatch`), in addition to AES-GCM's own auth-tag check — verify both checks are truly independent defenses and neither can be bypassed alone.

Protocol contract for SE wrap/unwrap/reconstruct, marked SECURITY-CRITICAL; documents that `unwrap`'s returned Data 'MUST be zeroized after use' — worth cross-checking every implementation call site against this contract.

The self-contained per-key SE-wrap envelope (magic CAPKEV1): ephemeral-static ECDH + HKDF + AES-GCM with public-parameter AAD, marked SECURITY-CRITICAL. `validateContract` enforces exact field shapes and a strict allowed-keys set on decode (rejecting extra/missing fields) before any secret material is derived — verify this strict decode is exercised on every decode path, not just the happy path, and that `randomData` failure (SecRandomCopyBytes) truly aborts sealing rather than falling back to a weaker source.

Keychain-backed storage for the pending/permanent SE-wrapped bundle rows, including promote/replace/rollback logic used by the modify-expiry and mode-switch crash-recovery state machines; an ordering bug here (e.g. promoting before validating) could commit a corrupt bundle as permanent.

Protocol + in-memory implementation for key-identity metadata persistence; the production implementation lives in ProtectedData/KeyMetadataDomainStore.swift — confirm the in-memory fallback (used by tutorial/UI-test graphs) can never be wired into a production composition root by mistake.

Shared permanent/pending bundle-state recovery-action state machine used by both auth-mode rewrap and modify-expiry; the exhaustive switch over (permanentState, pendingState) pairs is the single source of truth for what's safely recoverable vs. unrecoverable after a crash — any missing case is a silent data-loss risk.

Two-phase (create-pending / delete-old-and-promote) auth-mode rewrap of every SE-wrapped key bundle; Phase A failure must leave old items intact, Phase B failure must preserve pending items for recovery. `rawKeyBytes` is zeroized via defer during the per-key rewrap — verify the phase boundary (`markRewrapCommitRequired`) is durably persisted before Phase B ever deletes a permanent item.

Startup crash-recovery for interrupted rewrap, phase-aware (preparing vs commitRequired); an empty fingerprint list with a stale recovery flag is explicitly treated as unrecoverable rather than silently completing — confirm this fail-closed choice is preserved.
Requires fresh authentication under the CURRENT mode before any mode-switch rewrap begins; verify this authentication can't be satisfied by a stale/reused LAContext from an unrelated prior operation.

Marked SECURITY-CRITICAL: owns Face ID/Touch ID evaluation for both per-key (`evaluate(mode:)`) and app-session policies, plus the atomic mode-switch orchestration (requires a backed-up key before enabling High Security, authenticates under the old mode first). Confirm the `isUITestAuthenticationBypassEnabled` escape hatch can never be true in a production build/signing configuration.

Shared 'exactly these keys' strict-decode primitive used by every self-describing envelope (PrivateKeyEnvelope, ProtectedData root-secret/domain-master-key/domain-payload); marked SECURITY-CRITICAL as it's what rejects an envelope with extra or missing fields before any payload is trusted — a single shared bug here affects every envelope format at once.

SystemSecureEnclaveCompositeOperations performs the actual ML-DSA-65/87 sign and ML-KEM-768/1024 decapsulate primitives, with explicit signature/ciphertext/key-share length checks; a length check removed here would let a malformed enclave result reach the OpenPGP layer unchecked.

Marked SECURITY-CRITICAL: seals/unseals the classical (Ed25519+X25519 or Ed448+X448) component secrets under a FIXED biometric access control (deliberately independent of Standard/High-Security mode) using the shared SE-wrap envelope; `load` copies each half into freshly allocated storage specifically so the `defer` zeroizes the real concatenated plaintext buffer rather than a copy-on-write duplicate — worth re-verifying this copy pattern still holds under future Swift/CoW changes.

P-256 custody handle-pair lifecycle (create/load/locate/delete) including the partial-handle-pair repair logic (`inspectHandlePair`, `locateHandlePair` grouping by handleSetIdentifier); the ambiguous-handle and partial-set fail-closed paths are exactly the kind of edge case worth re-verifying after any keychain-query change.

Mirrors SecureEnclaveCustodyHandleStore for the post-quantum composite family (both tiers); `locateHandlePair`'s (true,false)/(false,true) cases deliberately throw a binding-mismatch rather than silently matching a partial set — confirm this stays symmetric with the P-256 store as the two evolve independently.

Production kSecClassKey-backed store for P-256 SE custody handles; `validatePrivateKeyAttributes` re-checks keyType/keySize/tokenID after `SecKeyCreateRandomKey` and deletes the just-created key on any post-creation validation failure — verify this cleanup can't race with a concurrent generation of the same handle-set.

Production kSecClassGenericPassword-backed store for ML-DSA/ML-KEM composite handles (CryptoKit dataRepresentation blobs); `loadKey` treats the reconstructed key's public key as authoritative over the stored `kSecAttrGeneric` binding and throws on divergence — confirms a corrupted/substituted row fails closed rather than trusting stale metadata.

Signs a SHA-256 digest with the SE P-256 handle, immediately self-verifying the signature with `SecKeyVerifySignature` before returning it, and has a manual DER-to-raw-(r,s) parser as a fallback path when RFC4754 raw signing isn't supported — the manual ASN.1 parser (`rawSignatureFromDER`) is hand-rolled and worth fuzzing/re-checking for malformed-length edge cases.

Derives the SE P-256 ECDH shared secret from an untrusted peer (PKESK) ephemeral public key; explicitly comments a KNOWN LIMITATION that `SecKeyCopyKeyExchangeResult`'s CFData-bridged Data may leave a lingering immutable backing buffer even after `resetBytes` triggers copy-on-write — this is an accepted residual exposure worth re-confirming is still bounded as described (short lifetime, ASLR, MIE).

Defines the single `privateKeyUsageBiometryAny` access-control policy used for every custody handle; `makeSecAccessControl` re-validates its own static properties against the one production preset before calling `SecAccessControlCreateWithFlags` — an oddly defensive pattern worth understanding (guards against a future added policy variant silently getting different flags than expected).

Generates the handle-set identifier via `SecRandomCopyBytes` and defines the Keychain applicationTag encoding/parsing (round-trip via `init(applicationTagString:)`); a parsing bug here (e.g. an identifier containing a literal '.') could mis-split role from handleSetIdentifier.

Validates the uncompressed P-256 X9.63 public-key shape (0x04 prefix, 65 bytes, non-all-zero) used everywhere handles are located/verified by public key; a shape-check gap here would let a malformed or all-zero public key bind to a handle.

Defines the composite tier (postQuantum/postQuantumHigh) parameter lengths, the `deviceBoundCompositeTier` exhaustive family-to-tier dispatch, and the public-binding/handle-pair value types; the exhaustive switch is the single dispatch point that must be updated whenever a new device-bound family is added.

Central error type + failureCategory mapping + shared OSStatus/CFError classification (`SecureEnclaveCustodyOSStatusMapper`) used by every custody handle store/signer/key-agreement implementation; a wrong OSStatus→category mapping here (e.g. treating errSecInteractionNotAllowed as authenticationFailed) would surface a misleading error category app-wide.

Marked SECURITY-CRITICAL: analogous per-app-data-root envelope (magic CAPDSEV3) to PrivateKeyEnvelope, domain-separated by distinct magic/HKDF/AAD prefixes so it can't be misread as a private-key envelope; same strict-decode and SecRandomCopyBytes-or-throw discipline applies.

Persists/loads the sealed ProtectedData root secret; `reprotectRootSecret` re-derives and byte-compares the secret before and after an access-policy update to verify the reprotect didn't corrupt the underlying value, throwing `internalFailure` on mismatch — confirm this verification read can't itself trigger a second biometric prompt in a confusing way for the user.

Orchestrates root-secret persist/load/reprotect with tracing; `loadRootSecretForAuthorization`'s `usesHandoffContext` branch skips the normal operation-prompt window when a pre-authenticated context is handed off — worth confirming this handoff path can't be reached with a stale/expired LAContext.

HardwareProtectedDataDeviceBindingProvider seals/opens the root secret under a fresh, non-persisted SE P-256 key folded entirely into the envelope; `openRootSecret` fails closed on both a `deviceBindingKeyIdentifier` mismatch and a reconstructed-public-key mismatch before attempting ECDH — confirm both checks are truly required (not just defense-in-depth for one already-redundant with AES-GCM auth).

Owns the wrapping-root→per-domain HKDF key derivation and AES-256-GCM wrap/unwrap of each domain's master key (WrappedDomainMasterKeyRecord, magic CADMKV2), plus a stage/validate/promote transaction (`writeWrappedDomainMasterKeyRecordTransaction`) that re-decrypts the staged record before committing it as authoritative. This file is the fan-out point for every ProtectedData domain's key material — verify the HKDF salt/info strings stay stable (a change would make all existing wrapped domain keys unwrappable) and that `cacheUnlockedDomainMasterKey`/`clearUnlockedDomainMasterKeys` truly zeroize on relock.

Applies the SQLCipher raw key (`keyDatabase`), validates SQLITE_HAS_CODEC/SQLITE_TEMP_STORE=2/cipher_integrity_check on every open, and zeroizes the domain-master-key copy and key-spec bytes via defer; confirm `createFresh`/`openExisting`'s error paths always call `close()` so a failed key application can't leave an unkeyed (or wrong-keyed) SQLite handle usable elsewhere.

Persists the app's authentication mode plus the rewrap/modify-expiry recovery journal as an encrypted ProtectedData domain payload; `requireUnlockedAuthMode` additionally checks the recovery journal's `rewrapPhase == .commitRequired` with a mismatched target mode and fails closed rather than returning a stale mode — this is the authoritative source powering PrivateKeyOperationRouter and every custody operation, so a bug here has app-wide blast radius.

Persists all PGPKeyIdentity metadata as an encrypted ProtectedData domain; `Payload.validateContract`'s per-identity custody/configuration cross-check (e.g. rejecting a P-256 configuration paired with software custody) is a second, independent enforcement of the same invariant PGPKeyCapabilityResolver enforces at runtime — worth confirming the two never drift apart as new families are added.

Debug-only trace sink that `print()`s every auth/lifecycle/operation event (DEBUG builds only, gated by `isEnabled`); this is exactly the surface the hard constraint 'never print key material/passphrase/decrypted content' applies to. The store itself only accepts `[String: String]` metadata dictionaries, so the real risk is at each of the ~50+ call sites across custody code — worth spot-checking that none of them ever pass a fingerprint-adjacent raw byte string, passphrase, or plaintext fragment into `metadata:`.

deadcode.removalNote: Never constructed by any Rust path, but it is an FFI-exported uniffi::Error variant mirrored 1:1 into generated bindings (pgp_mobile.swift case 6) and consumed by PGPErrorMapper.swift:91 exhaustive switch. UniFFI decodes error variants by ordinal index, so removing a middle variant shifts subsequent ordinals; deleting the variant, regenerating bindings, dropping the mapper arm, and rebuilding is what settles clean removability. (Swift's own CypherAirError.badSignature is thrown independently from SelfTestService.swift:243,265 and VerifyScreenModel.swift:374 — not derived from this variant.)

vestigial_verdicts.symbol: KeyMigrationCoordinator.recoveryAction(for:) — the KeyBundleState.partial arms ((.complete,.partial) L104, (.partial,.complete) L108, and the (.partial,.missing)/(.partial,.partial)/(.missing,.partial) alternatives L110-112)
vestigial_verdicts.behavioralProof: The only constructor of KeyBundleState anywhere is KeyBundleStore.bundleState() (KeyBundleStore.swift:149-159), which returns `exists ? .complete : .missing` from a single keychain.exists boolean. .partial has no producer. In a fresh CypherAir X install the per-key envelope is a single self-contained PrivateKeyEnvelope Keychain row that is atomically present or absent, so bundleState() can only ever hand recoveryAction() .complete or .missing. The (permanent,pending) tuple can therefore never contain .partial, making all five .partial-bearing arms unreachable regardless of install history. The reachable 4-arm space ((.complete,.missing)→none, (.complete,.complete)→deletePending, (.missing,.complete)→promotePending, (.missing,.missing)→unrecoverable) is exactly what the tests exercise; the (.missing,.missing) alternative stays live.
vestigial_verdicts.producerFound: none in current code — .partial is never constructed; KeyBundleStore.bundleState() only returns .complete/.missing
vestigial_verdicts.cascade: Removing the trunk KeyBundleState.partial case (required to keep the switch exhaustive; the arms cannot be deleted individually while .partial exists) orphans KeyMigrationRecoveryAction.replacePermanentWithPending — it is produced ONLY by the (.partial,.complete) arm here — and its now-unreachable handler arm in recoverInterruptedMigration (L139-145). Note KeyBundleStore.replacePermanentWithPending (the store method) stays LIVE: it is still called by PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator's reachable (.complete,.complete) arm.
vestigial_verdicts.removalRisk: Fail-closed dead-defense; unreachable so behavior is unchanged. The arms can only be removed by deleting the KeyBundleState.partial enum case, which also touches PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator (candidate 2). Security-adjacent recovery code — human review required per docs/SECURITY.md.

vestigial_verdicts.symbol: PrivateKeyRewrapRecoveryCoordinator.recoverCommitRequiredRewrapMigration(for:) — the KeyBundleState.partial arms ((.partial,.complete) L87, the whole (.complete,.partial)→.retryableFailure arm L101, and the (.partial,.missing)/(.partial,.partial)/(.missing,.partial) alternatives L103-105)
vestigial_verdicts.behavioralProof: Same sole producer as candidate 1: KeyBundleStore.bundleState() (KeyBundleStore.swift:149-159) never returns .partial. This switch is evaluated only when journal.rewrapPhase == .commitRequired, but the (permanent,pending) tuple it inspects still comes entirely from bundleState(), so no .partial value can appear on a fresh install (or any install — the single-row storage shape guarantees it). The (.complete,.partial)→.retryableFailure arm at L101 is thus dead, and .retryableFailure remains produced by the reachable catch blocks (L91-93, L98-99). The reachable arms ((.complete,.missing)→noActionSafe, (.complete,.complete)→replacePermanentWithPending, (.missing,.complete)→promotePendingToPermanent, (.missing,.missing)→unrecoverable) fully cover behavior.
vestigial_verdicts.producerFound: none in current code — .partial is never constructed by KeyBundleStore.bundleState()
vestigial_verdicts.cascade: No additional orphans beyond the shared KeyBundleState.partial trunk removal. After deleting the .partial arms, the (.complete,.complete) arm still calls bundleStore.replacePermanentWithPending, so that store method stays live. The KeyBundleState.partial case removal is shared with candidate 1.

docs_verdicts.claim: Positioning line (lines 8-11): 'CypherAir X is the enhanced edition of CypherAir — it adds the three Device-Bound (Secure Enclave) families and Portable Post-Quantum on top of CypherAir's two portable families.'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: There is one unified app offering all nine key families; no base-tier/enhanced-tier product boundary exists. The 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 family framing also undercounts the shipped nine families.
docs_verdicts.evidence: One Xcode app target: PRODUCT_BUNDLE_IDENTIFIER = com.chentianren.cypherair (project.pbxproj:972,1201,1350). Single display-name fallback 'CypherAir X' (Sources/App/Common/AppProductIdentity.swift:7). docs/PRD.md §3 describes a single flat 'Key Type' list offering all nine families (Portable/Device-Bound Legacy, Modern, Modern · High, Post-Quantum, Post-Quantum · High), no base/enhanced split.

docs_verdicts.claim: App Review notes (line 167): 'Four additional key families on top of CypherAir's two, for six total.'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: Nine key families, not six. The doc's en list (lines 42-45) and zh-Hans mirror (lines 117-120) never mention 'Portable Modern · High', 'Portable Post-Quantum · High', or 'Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High'.
docs_verdicts.evidence: docs/PRD.md §3 defines nine families; Sources/App/Common/PGPKeyFamily+Presentation.swift lists nine defaultValue names (Portable Legacy, Portable Modern, Portable Modern · High, Portable Post-Quantum, Portable Post-Quantum · High, Device-Bound Legacy, Device-Bound Modern, Device-Bound Post-Quantum, Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High).

docs_verdicts.claim: Description bullets: 'Device-Bound Compatible — P-256 keys held in the Secure Enclave' (line 42) and 'Beyond the Portable Compatible and Portable Modern key families...' (line 41).
docs_verdicts.correctValue: User-facing names are 'Portable Legacy' / 'Device-Bound Legacy', not 'Portable Compatible' / 'Device-Bound Compatible'. (Internal localization keys still read deviceBoundCompatible/portableCompatible, but the presented name changed.)
docs_verdicts.evidence: Sources/App/Common/PGPKeyFamily+Presentation.swift:33 defaultValue 'Portable Legacy' (key keyFamily.portableCompatible.name) and :43 defaultValue 'Device-Bound Legacy' (key keyFamily.deviceBoundCompatible.name). docs/PRD.md §3 uses 'Portable Legacy' / 'Device-Bound Legacy' vocabulary.

docs_verdicts.claim: Screenshot checklist (line 193): 'iPhone 6.9" set — Home, Encrypt, key generation (six families), About.'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: The key-generation list has nine families, not six.
docs_verdicts.evidence: Sources/App/Common/PGPKeyFamily+Presentation.swift enumerates nine family names/subtitles; docs/PRD.md §3.3 lists nine 'Key Type' rows. A screenshot of the full list no longer matches the 'six families' caption.

docs_verdicts.claim: 'What's New — 1.5.0 template' (en-US lines 80-86, zh-Hans lines 150-156) lists only the original post-quantum + SE-custody launch set and the tour/settings redesign.
docs_verdicts.correctValue: The project is at MARKETING_VERSION 1.5.2 / CURRENT_PROJECT_VERSION 15200 after the nine-families expansion; the template header says 1.5.0 and never mentions the three added '· High' families (Portable Modern · High, Portable Post-Quantum · High, Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High).
docs_verdicts.evidence: CypherAir.xcodeproj/project.pbxproj: MARKETING_VERSION = 1.5.2 (lines 964,1193,1342) and CURRENT_PROJECT_VERSION = 15200 (lines 933,1162,1311). docs/APP_STORE_LISTING.md:80 header '1.5.0 template'; bullets at :83-85 / :153-155 mention only Post-Quantum + Device-Bound Compatible/Modern custody + welcome tour/settings.

docs_verdicts.claim: §1 (line 14): 'The product presents it as three of the six key families...' listing Device-Bound Compatible, Device-Bound Modern, Device-Bound Post-Quantum.
docs_verdicts.correctValue: Four of the nine families. The list is missing 'Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High' (RFC 9980 ML-DSA-87/ML-KEM-1024 split custody). The 'Device-Bound Compatible' label should be 'Device-Bound Legacy' to match code/PRD vocabulary.
docs_verdicts.evidence: docs/PRD.md §3 (the cited taxonomy owner) defines nine families with four Device-Bound. Sources/Security/SecureEnclaveCompositeHandle.swift:12-16 declares SecureEnclaveCompositeTier .postQuantum and .postQuantumHigh; :70-81 maps .deviceBoundPostQuantumHighV6 → .postQuantumHigh. User-facing 'Device-Bound Legacy' at PGPKeyFamily+Presentation.swift:43.

docs_verdicts.claim: §5 'Compatibility language' (lines 83-85) lists three bullets — Device-Bound Compatible (v4), Device-Bound Modern (v6), Device-Bound Post-Quantum — omitting a fourth for 'Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High'.
docs_verdicts.correctValue: A fourth bullet for 'Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High' (ML-DSA-87+Ed448 / ML-KEM-1024+X448) is needed; the family is implemented and tested. The v4 bullet's label should also read 'Device-Bound Legacy'.
docs_verdicts.evidence: pgp-mobile/src/keys.rs:24-25 documents the Post-Quantum · High composite (ML-DSA-87+Ed448 signing, ML-KEM-1024+X448 encryption); :605-645 defines the High-tier signer/decapsulation seams. Tests/DeviceSecurityTests/DeviceSecureEnclaveCompositeCustodyTests.swift:140 test_compositeHighSplitCustodyLifecycle_generateDecryptSign_onDevice exercises it end-to-end.

docs_verdicts.claim: §8.1 evidence row (line 117): single 'split-custody composite' row citing DeviceSecureEnclaveCompositeCustodyTests, without acknowledging the High-tier method covering a fourth family.
docs_verdicts.correctValue: The same test file covers two tiers; the evidence row/scenario text reflects only the base Device-Bound Post-Quantum tier and does not surface the Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High coverage — mirroring the §1/§5 taxonomy gap.
docs_verdicts.evidence: Tests/DeviceSecurityTests/DeviceSecureEnclaveCompositeCustodyTests.swift contains BOTH test_compositeSplitCustodyLifecycle_generateDecryptSign_onDevice (line 21) and test_compositeHighSplitCustodyLifecycle_generateDecryptSign_onDevice (line 140); docs §8.1:117 parenthetical lists only the base-tier scenarios and the doc's own §1/§5 taxonomy omits the High family.

docs_verdicts.claim: Row 'Secure Enclave composite (post-quantum) private-key blob rows' (line 56): service 'com.cypherair.v1.secure-enclave-composite.<role>', ML-DSA-65/ML-KEM-768, reachable via Device-Bound Post-Quantum generation only.
docs_verdicts.correctValue: There is a second Keychain service namespace for the High tier: the '.high' suffix produces 'com.cypherair.v1.secure-enclave-composite.high.<role>' holding ML-DSA-87/ML-KEM-1024 blobs, reachable via Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High generation. The row must list both the .high namespace and High-tier reachability.
docs_verdicts.evidence: Sources/Security/SecureEnclaveCompositeHandle.swift:54-59 tier.serviceNamespaceSuffix = '' for .postQuantum, '.high' for .postQuantumHigh; :114-116 serviceString interpolates that suffix ('so the two tiers never collide', :7). PrivateKey enum :198-203 includes mldsa87Signing / mlkem1024KeyAgreement.

docs_verdicts.claim: Row 'Composite classical-component envelope row' (line 57): '...concatenated 32-byte Ed25519 + 32-byte X25519 classical component secrets...'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: The row must also account for the High tier: a 57-byte Ed448 secret + a 56-byte X448 secret for Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High (in addition to the base 32-byte Ed25519 + 32-byte X25519 pair).
docs_verdicts.evidence: pgp-mobile/src/composite_classical.rs:27 ED448_SECRET_LENGTH = 57, :30 X448_SECRET_LENGTH = 56. Sources/Security/SecureEnclaveCompositeClassicalComponentStore.swift:5-11,17-20 comment: 'a 32-byte Ed25519 + 32-byte X25519 pair for .postQuantum, or a 57-byte Ed448 + 56-byte X448 pair for .postQuantumHigh'. Tier lengths in SecureEnclaveCompositeHandle.swift:36-50.

docs_verdicts.claim: §1 line 11: 'Three software profiles with different algorithm suites; the composite Post-Quantum suite also backs the Device-Bound Post-Quantum split-custody family.'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: Five software profiles. Sources/Models/PGPKeyProfile.swift enumerates universal, modern, advanced, postQuantum, postQuantumHigh. There are TWO composite Post-Quantum suites: the ML-DSA-65/ML-KEM-768 suite (mldsa65Ed25519Mlkem768X25519) backs Portable Post-Quantum AND Device-Bound Post-Quantum, and the ML-DSA-87/ML-KEM-1024 suite (mldsa87Ed448Mlkem1024X448) backs Portable Post-Quantum · High AND Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High. §1 also lacks 'Modern' and 'Post-Quantum · High' subsections (only Profile A, Profile B, Post-Quantum exist).
docs_verdicts.evidence: Sources/Models/PGPKeyProfile.swift lines 8-13 define 5 cases. Sources/Models/PGPKeyConfiguration.swift lines 113-132 (postQuantumSoftwareV6 uses .mldsa65..., postQuantumHighSoftwareV6 uses .mldsa87...) and lines 156-177 (deviceBoundPostQuantumV6 uses mldsa65, deviceBoundPostQuantumHighV6 uses mldsa87) show each composite suite backs two families. docs/SECURITY.md §1 has subsections only for Profile A, Profile B, Post-Quantum.

docs_verdicts.claim: §7 line 203: 'Used for private-key export and for importing passphrase-protected key files with Argon2id S2K (Profile B and Portable Post-Quantum).'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: Four portable profiles use Argon2id S2K, not two: Portable Modern (modernSoftwareV6), Portable Modern · High / Profile B (modernHighSoftwareV6), Portable Post-Quantum (postQuantumSoftwareV6), and Portable Post-Quantum · High (postQuantumHighSoftwareV6). The line names only 2 of the 4.
docs_verdicts.evidence: Sources/Models/PGPKeyConfiguration.swift: modernSoftwareV6 (line 100), modernHighSoftwareV6 (line 110), postQuantumSoftwareV6 (line 119), postQuantumHighSoftwareV6 (line 131) all set softwareExportProtection: .argon2idS2K. compatibleSoftwareV4 uses .iteratedSaltedS2K (line 88).

docs_verdicts.claim: §2 Services Layer table, KeyManagementService row: 'Key generation across all six families — Portable Compatible (Cv25519/RFC 4880), Portable Modern (Cv448/RFC 9580), Portable Post-Quantum (RFC 9980 composite), Device-Bound Compatible/Modern (Secure Enclave custody P-256 v4/v6), Device-Bound Post-Quantum (split custody...)'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: Nine families, not six. And the algorithm attribution is wrong: Portable Modern uses CipherSuite::Cv25519 (Ed25519+X25519 v6), while Cv448 is the Advanced tier (Portable Modern · High). The row omits three shipped families: Portable Modern · High, Portable Post-Quantum · High, and Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High.
docs_verdicts.evidence: pgp-mobile/src/keys/generation.rs: KeyProfile::Modern sets CipherSuite::Cv25519 (lines 45-51), KeyProfile::Advanced sets CipherSuite::Cv448 (lines 37-39). CLAUDE.md 'Key families' bullet and Sources/Models/PGPKeyProfile.swift + PGPKeyConfiguration.swift enumerate nine families total.

docs_verdicts.claim: §2 FFI Adapters, External-provider bridges bullet: 'Swift implementations of the Rust private-operation seams: P-256 signing and key agreement, ML-DSA-65 signing, ML-KEM-768 decapsulation.'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: Also add ML-DSA-87 signing and ML-KEM-1024 decapsulation bridges (the Post-Quantum · High tier).
docs_verdicts.evidence: Sources/Services/FFI/ contains PGPExternalMlDsa87SigningProviderBridge.swift and PGPExternalMlKem1024DecapsulationProviderBridge.swift alongside the ML-DSA-65 / ML-KEM-768 / P-256 bridges (confirmed via directory listing).

docs_verdicts.claim: §2 Security Layer table: SecureEnclaveCompositeHandleStore 'enclave-resident ML-DSA-65/ML-KEM-768 component blobs of Device-Bound Post-Quantum identities'; SecureEnclaveCompositeOperations 'In-enclave ML-DSA signing and ML-KEM decapsulation primitives'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: These components also serve Device-Bound Post-Quantum · High (ML-DSA-87 4627-byte signature / ML-KEM-1024 1568-byte ciphertext); the table names only the base-tier sizes.
docs_verdicts.evidence: Sources/Security/SecureEnclaveCompositeOperations.swift documents the 4627-byte pure ML-DSA-87 signature (line 17) and the 1568-byte ML-KEM-1024 ciphertext (line 34), with constants mldsa87SignatureLength = 4627 (line 48) and mlkem1024CiphertextLength = 1568 (line 49), alongside the 3309-byte ML-DSA-65 / 1088-byte ML-KEM-768 base tier.

docs_verdicts.claim: §1.3 table header: 'Setting | Profile A (Universal) | Profile B (Advanced) | Post-Quantum' — only three profile columns, no Modern column.
docs_verdicts.correctValue: Missing a 'Modern' column for the shipped Portable/Device-Bound Modern configuration (Ed25519+X25519 v6, RFC 9580, SEIPDv2, Argon2id) — modernSoftwareV6 is a distinct configuration, not a variant of Profile A or B.
docs_verdicts.evidence: docs/TDD.md §1.3 line 23 shows only three columns. Sources/Models/PGPKeyConfiguration.swift modernSoftwareV6 (lines 94-101): keyVersion 6, algorithmSuite .ed25519X25519, rfc9580Oriented, seipdV2Aead, argon2idS2K — a fully distinct profile. Self-acknowledged as unresolved in commit 52796e3's message.

docs_verdicts.claim: §1.3 classification-rule prose (line 37): 'an RFC 9980 composite primary ... classifies as Post-Quantum; any other v6 primary is Profile B; v4 is Profile A.'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: An Ed25519 v6 primary classifies as Modern, not Profile B. classify_profile() has a dedicated Ed25519 => KeyProfile::Modern arm distinct from Ed448 => KeyProfile::Advanced. 'Any other v6 primary is Profile B' describes only the defensive catch-all (_ if version >= 6 => Advanced); the prose never mentions Modern.
docs_verdicts.evidence: pgp-mobile/src/keys/profile.rs classify_profile(): line 24 PublicKeyAlgorithm::Ed25519 => KeyProfile::Modern; line 25 Ed448 => KeyProfile::Advanced; line 28 catch-all _ if version >= 6 => KeyProfile::Advanced; line 29 _ => KeyProfile::Universal.

docs_verdicts.claim: §4 line 100: 'Applies to the Argon2id S2K families (Profile B and Portable Post-Quantum); Profile A uses Iterated+Salted (mode 3).'
docs_verdicts.correctValue: Omits Portable Modern (modernSoftwareV6) and Portable Post-Quantum · High (postQuantumHighSoftwareV6), which also use .argon2idS2K. Four portable families use Argon2id, not two.
docs_verdicts.evidence: Sources/Models/PGPKeyConfiguration.swift: modernSoftwareV6 (line 100), modernHighSoftwareV6/Profile B (line 110), postQuantumSoftwareV6 (line 119), postQuantumHighSoftwareV6 (line 131) all set softwareExportProtection: .argon2idS2K. Same omission as SECURITY.md §7.
